options: Photo 51 provided measurements and structural evidence that Watson and Crick used to build their model without Franklin's knowledge.
Watson and Crick took Photo 51 themselves and then asked Franklin to verify their findings.
Photo 51 proved that Watson and Crick's earlier theories about DNA structure were incorrect.
Franklin shared Photo 51 with Watson and Crick so they could work together on the discovery.
questions.explanation: The passage states Wilkins showed the photo without Franklin's permission, and Watson and Crick used its information to build their model. Option B reverses who took the photo, C is not mentioned, and D contradicts the passage's account of events.

articles.topic_title: M.C. Escher's impossible drawings
articles.title: The Impossible Worlds of M.C. Escher
articles.passage_html: Picture a staircase where monks climb endlessly upward, yet always return to where they started. Their robes brush stone steps that twist in an eternal loop, neither ascending nor descending, defying every rule you learned about gravity and space. This brain-bending image sprang from the mind of Maurits Cornelis Escher, a Dutch artist born on June 17, 1898, in Leeuwarden, Netherlands. Escher carved and printed more than 400 lithographs and woodcuts during his lifetime, each one challenging viewers to question what their eyes reported. In his 1960 lithograph "Ascending and Descending," that impossible staircase materializes on paper—a rectangular path that climbs forever while simultaneously descending. Your gaze travels around the structure, searching for the trick, but the illusion remains perfect from every angle. The artist discovered his mathematical muse in an unlikely place. During visits to the Alhambra palace in Granada, Spain, in 1922 and 1936, Escher traced the intricate Islamic tile patterns covering the ancient walls. These repeating geometric designs, called tessellations, sparked his fascination with how shapes could fit together like puzzle pieces that never end. Remarkably, Escher never studied mathematics formally, yet his work dazzled scientists at the 1954 International Congress of Mathematicians in Amsterdam. Escher's genius flourished when architects Roger Penrose and his father created the impossible triangle—a three-sided figure that cannot exist in our three-dimensional world. This inspired his 1961 lithograph "Waterfall," where water cascades downward through a channel, then impossibly flows upward again to its starting point in a perpetual cycle. The paradox mesmerizes: the water obeys gravity while simultaneously ignoring it. Perhaps his most disorienting creation, "Relativity" from 1953, portrays sixteen human figures inhabiting a single building where three different directions serve as "down." People walk on ceilings that are floors, climb staircases that are simultaneously right-side-up and upside-down. In "Drawing Hands" (1948), measuring 28.2 by 33.2 centimeters, two hands emerge from flat paper, each sketching the other into three-dimensional life—creator and creation intertwined. Escher's impossible worlds remind us that imagination can construct what reality cannot. His drawings whisper a tantalizing question: if our eyes can be fooled so completely, what other impossibilities might we learn to see?
articles.passage_raw:
Picture a staircase where monks climb endlessly upward, yet always return to where they started. Their robes brush stone steps that twist in an eternal loop, neither ascending nor descending, defying every rule you learned about gravity and space. This brain-bending image sprang from the mind of Maurits Cornelis Escher, a Dutch artist born on June 17, 1898, in Leeuwarden, Netherlands.

Escher carved and printed more than 400 lithographs and woodcuts during his lifetime, each one challenging viewers to question what their eyes reported. In his 1960 lithograph "Ascending and Descending," that impossible staircase materializes on paper—a rectangular path that climbs forever while simultaneously descending. Your gaze travels around the structure, searching for the trick, but the <vocab>illusion|something that deceives the eye</vocab> remains perfect from every angle.

The artist discovered his mathematical muse in an unlikely place. During visits to the Alhambra palace in Granada, Spain, in 1922 and 1936, Escher traced the <vocab>intricate|containing many small, complex parts</vocab> Islamic tile patterns covering the ancient walls. These repeating geometric designs, called tessellations, sparked his <vocab>fascination|intense interest</vocab> with how shapes could fit together like puzzle pieces that never end. Remarkably, Escher never studied mathematics formally, yet his work dazzled scientists at the 1954 International Congress of Mathematicians in Amsterdam.

Escher's genius flourished when architects Roger Penrose and his father created the impossible triangle—a three-sided figure that cannot exist in our three-dimensional world. This inspired his 1961 lithograph "Waterfall," where water cascades downward through a channel, then impossibly flows upward again to its starting point in a perpetual cycle. The <vocab>paradox|a statement or situation that contradicts itself</vocab> mesmerizes: the water obeys gravity while simultaneously ignoring it.

Perhaps his most disorienting creation, "Relativity" from 1953, portrays sixteen human figures inhabiting a single building where three different directions serve as "down." People walk on ceilings that are floors, climb staircases that are simultaneously right-side-up and upside-down. In "Drawing Hands" (1948), measuring 28.2 by 33.2 centimeters, two hands emerge from flat paper, each sketching the other into three-dimensional life—creator and creation <vocab>intertwined|twisted together</vocab>.

Escher's impossible worlds remind us that imagination can construct what reality cannot. His drawings whisper a <vocab>tantalizing|tempting and appealing</vocab> question: if our eyes can be fooled so completely, what other impossibilities might we learn to see?

vocab.definition: a statement or situation that contradicts itself

Option B is incorrect because the passage states Escher never received formal mathematical training. Options C and D represent narrow topics mentioned in the passage but not its main focus.

articles.topic_title: Penicillin: the accidental discovery that saved millions
articles.title: How a Messy Lab Changed Medical History
articles.passage_html: September 1928. Scottish scientist Alexander Fleming stepped into his laboratory at St. Mary's Hospital in London after a long vacation. Bacterial culture plates covered his workbench, but something looked wrong. A fuzzy blue-green mold had invaded one dish, and around this uninvited guest, the Staphylococcus bacteria lay dead. Most scientists would have tossed the contaminated plate into the trash. Fleming studied it instead. This accident deserves recognition as one of history's most important medical breakthroughs because it led directly to the development of penicillin, a drug that has saved millions of lives. The discovery transformed how doctors treat bacterial infections and proved that sometimes the greatest advances come from unexpected moments. First, penicillin revolutionized medicine by providing the first effective weapon against deadly bacterial infections. Before penicillin became widely available in the 1940s, infections like pneumonia, scarlet fever, and infected wounds killed hundreds of thousands of people each year in the United States alone. Fleming identified the mold as belonging to the genus Penicillium and published his findings in the British Journal of Experimental Pathology in 1929. Though the medical community initially showed little interest, Fleming's observation planted seeds for a medical revolution. Second, the story demonstrates how scientific collaboration turns discoveries into practical solutions. Fleming's observation remained mostly unused for a decade until 1939, when a team of scientists at Oxford University led by Howard Florey and Ernst Boris Chain began working to purify penicillin and transform it into a medicine doctors could actually use. Their perseverance paid off. By 1942, penicillin production had increased enough to treat approximately 100 patients. By June 1944, Allied forces had stockpiled 2.3 million doses to treat wounded soldiers during the D-Day invasion of Normandy. In 1945, Fleming, Florey, and Chain shared the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine. The trajectory from a contaminated dish to a world-changing medicine reminds us that progress requires both chance observations and determined effort. Fleming could have simply cleaned his messy lab, but his curiosity unlocked a discovery that continues saving lives today. How many other breakthroughs are waiting in unexpected places?

articles.topic_title: Adolphe Sax: how the saxophone was invented (1846)
articles.title: The Brass Voice That Changed Music
articles.passage_html: In a cluttered Paris workshop in the early 1840s, metal shavings glinted on workbenches as Adolphe Sax bent over his latest creation. Born in Dinant, Belgium, on November 6, 1814, Sax had grown up watching his father Charles-Joseph craft instruments. Now, he was forging something entirely new—a hybrid that would blend the warm, reedy breath of a clarinet with the bold, gleaming body of a brass ophicleide. The saxophone's curved brass body caught lamplight as Sax tested its voice. This ingenious instrument bridged two musical families, woodwind and brass, in ways no one had attempted before. On March 20, 1846, Sax secured his patent in Paris for two groups of saxophones: seven designed for orchestras and eight for military bands. Composer Hector Berlioz praised the new sound in the Paris Journal des Débats in 1842, describing its rich, versatile tone. Yet success brought bitter challenges. Competing instrument makers filed over thirty lawsuits, trying to invalidate Sax's patents and destroy his business. Despite winning a competition in 1857 that made saxophones official instruments of French military bands, Sax's finances remained precarious. He declared bankruptcy three times and died in poverty on February 7, 1894. The legacy Sax left behind, however, proved far richer than gold—a voice that would eventually define jazz, blues, and countless musical genres worldwide.

Now, he was forging something entirely new—a hybrid that would blend the warm, reedy breath of a clarinet with the bold, gleaming body of a brass ophicleide.

The saxophone's curved brass body caught lamplight as Sax tested its voice. This <vocab>ingenious|cleverly inventive</vocab> instrument bridged two musical families, woodwind and brass, in ways no one had attempted before. On March 20, 1846, Sax secured his patent in Paris for two groups of saxophones: seven designed for orchestras and eight for military bands. Composer Hector Berlioz praised the new sound in the Paris Journal des Débats in 1842, describing its rich, <vocab>versatile|adaptable to many uses</vocab> tone.

Yet success brought bitter challenges. Competing instrument makers filed over thirty lawsuits, trying to <vocab>invalidate|make legally void</vocab> Sax's patents and destroy his business. Despite winning a competition in 1857 that made saxophones official instruments of French military bands, Sax's finances remained <vocab>precarious|dangerously unstable</vocab>. He declared bankruptcy three times and died in poverty on February 7, 1894.

The <vocab>legacy|something handed down from the past</vocab> Sax left behind, however, proved far richer than gold—a voice that would eventually define jazz, blues, and countless musical <vocab>genres|categories of artistic work</vocab> worldwide.

articles.topic_title: Ada Lovelace: the first computer programmer (1843)
articles.title: The Countess Who Imagined the Future
articles.passage_html: Ink scratched across parchment as Ada Lovelace bent over her desk in 1843, scribbling mathematical symbols that danced between French phrases. Before her lay an article about Charles Babbage's Analytical Engine—a magnificent machine of brass gears and metal rods that existed only in diagrams. Most people saw it as a mere calculating device. Ada glimpsed something far grander. Born Augusta Ada Byron in London on December 10, 1815, Ada was the daughter of the famous poet Lord Byron. Yet her mother, Lady Byron, deliberately steered her toward mathematics and science, hoping to suppress any dangerous poetic tendencies. Numbers became Ada's poetry instead. When she married William King in 1835, becoming the Countess of Lovelace, she never abandoned her intellectual pursuits. Her translation of Babbage's article stretched far beyond the original text. Ada's notes—three times longer than the article itself—contained something revolutionary. In Note G, she composed a detailed algorithm for calculating Bernoulli numbers. This sequence of instructions represented the world's first computer program, written for a machine that wouldn't be built for over a century. Most remarkably, Ada predicted that such engines could compose music and create graphics, not merely crunch numbers. She envisioned machines that could manipulate symbols to produce art—a prediction that seemed utterly preposterous in her era. When Ada died in 1852 at age thirty-six, few recognized how her visionary ideas would reshape human civilization.

Ada glimpsed something far grander.

Born Augusta Ada Byron in London on December 10, 1815, Ada was the daughter of the famous poet Lord Byron. Yet her mother, Lady Byron, deliberately steered her toward mathematics and science, hoping to <vocab>suppress|hold back or prevent</vocab> any dangerous poetic tendencies. Numbers became Ada's poetry instead. When she married William King in 1835, becoming the Countess of Lovelace, she never abandoned her intellectual pursuits.

Her translation of Babbage's article stretched far beyond the original text. Ada's notes—three times longer than the article itself—contained something revolutionary. In Note G, she composed a detailed <vocab>algorithm|step-by-step procedure for solving a problem</vocab> for calculating Bernoulli numbers. This sequence of instructions represented the world's first computer program, written for a machine that wouldn't be built for over a century.

Most remarkably, Ada predicted that such engines could compose music and create graphics, not merely crunch numbers. She envisioned machines that could <vocab>manipulate|skillfully handle or control</vocab> symbols to produce art—a prediction that seemed utterly <vocab>preposterous|absurd or ridiculous</vocab> in her era. When Ada died in 1852 at age thirty-six, few recognized how her <vocab>visionary|showing foresight about the future</vocab> ideas would reshape human civilization.

vocab.definition: step-by-step procedure for solving a problem

articles.topic_title: The Iditarod: 1,000 miles of dogsledding
articles.title: Racing Through Alaska's Frozen Wilderness
articles.passage_html: Dick Wilmarth crossed the finish line in Nome after more than twenty days of battling subzero winds and blinding snow. The year was 1973, and he had just won the first-ever Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race, covering approximately 1,000 miles of Alaska's most formidable terrain. His feet ached, his sled dogs panted with exhaustion, but they had made history together. Today, the Iditarod honors the heroic 1925 serum run to Nome, when twenty mushers and roughly 150 sled dogs relayed diphtheria medicine 674 miles in just 5.5 days to stop a deadly epidemic. Modern racers follow routes between 975 and 998 miles, alternating paths in even and odd years. Champions like Susan Butcher, who won four times between 1986 and 1990, and Rick Swenson, who claimed five victories, have demonstrated extraordinary skill navigating frozen rivers and mountain passes. Mushers must carry mandatory gear weighing at least fifteen pounds, including snowshoes, an ax, and protective booties for each dog's paws. The grueling journey demands both human and canine endurance. In 2017, Mitch Seavey shattered records at age fifty-seven, finishing in just eight days, three hours, and forty minutes. While the winner receives $50,000 and a truck, the last-place finisher earns the Red Lantern Award—proof that completing this extraordinary race is itself a remarkable achievement.

articles.topic_title: Bletchley Park's code breakers: puzzles that won WWII
articles.title: The Secret Puzzles That Changed the War
articles.passage_html: Inside a Victorian mansion in Buckinghamshire, England, machines clicked and hummed day and night. At Bletchley Park during World War II, thousands of workers raced against time to solve the most dangerous puzzle in history: cracking Germany's secret military codes. The fate of nations rested on their ability to decipher messages that seemed impossible to read. The German military used a machine called Enigma to scramble their messages into nonsense. With 159 quintillion possible settings—that's 159 followed by eighteen zeros—the Enigma appeared unbreakable. Each day, German operators changed the settings, creating fresh codes that protected battle plans, submarine locations, and troop movements. Even if someone stole an Enigma machine, finding the correct daily settings would take longer than a human lifetime. British mathematician Alan Turing refused to accept defeat. In 1939, he designed the Bombe, an electromechanical device that could test thousands of Enigma settings per hour. The machine's rotating drums whirred through possibilities, searching for patterns in the encrypted messages. A major breakthrough arrived on May 9, 1941, when the Royal Navy captured German submarine U-110 along with its Enigma machine and codebooks. This vital information gave Bletchley Park's codebreakers the clues they needed to unlock German secrets faster. By 1945, approximately 9,000 people worked at Bletchley Park, and seventy-five percent were women. They operated machines, analyzed language patterns, and solved complex mathematical problems. Engineer Tommy Flowers pushed technology even further. In 1943, he built Colossus, the world's first programmable electronic digital computer, to break the even more difficult Lorenz cipher. These inventions transformed what seemed insurmountable into achievable goals. Historians estimate that Bletchley Park's intelligence shortened the war in Europe by two to four years, saving countless lives. Yet the British government kept this achievement secret until 1974, nearly thirty years after the war ended. The codebreakers who changed history remained silent about their contributions, proving that sometimes the most powerful victories are the ones nobody sees.

questions.explanation: The passage states that U-110's capture provided 'vital information' that gave codebreakers 'the clues they needed to unlock German secrets faster,' showing it was mentioned to illustrate how this breakthrough helped their work.

questions.explanation: The passage mentions the government 'kept this achievement secret until 1974' and required declassification, suggesting they protected the information for security reasons rather than the workers' personal choices or technological obsolescence.

articles.topic_title: The 1925 serum run to Nome: sled dogs that saved a town
articles.title: Racing Against Death in the Frozen North
articles.passage_html: The thermometer plunged to 85 degrees below zero as Leonhard Seppala squinted through the frozen darkness. Ice crystals obscured the trail ahead, but his lead dog Togo never hesitated. The twelve-year-old Siberian Husky pulled the sled forward, his paws crunching rhythmically across the frozen Norton Sound. Behind them, wrapped in fur and canvas, sat a twenty-pound package that could save thousands of lives. In January 1925, Nome, Alaska faced a catastrophic threat. Dr. Curtis Welch, the town's only doctor, discovered diphtheria spreading among the population of 10,000 people in Nome and the surrounding areas. The town's antitoxin serum had expired—useless against the deadly disease. On January 22, Dr. Welch sent an urgent telegram warning that an epidemic could kill the entire Native population within weeks. The nearest fresh supply waited in Anchorage, 1,000 miles away. Winter had locked the region in its icy grip, making airplane flight impossible and ship transport through frozen waters unfeasible. Alaska's mushers devised a daring plan. Twenty drivers and approximately 150 sled dogs would relay the serum across 674 miles of frozen terrain from Nenana to Nome. Teams would hand off the precious cargo like runners in a race, each musher braving hostile conditions to reach the next station. Seppala covered the longest and most dangerous portion—91 miles through a blizzard that would have stopped most men. Wind shrieked across the ice, threatening to hurl his team into open water. Yet Togo, though small for a sled dog, possessed an unwavering spirit. The veteran lead dog navigated treacherous ice formations and treacherous shortcuts across Norton Sound that shaved precious hours from the journey. Gunnar Kaasen and his lead dog Balto completed the final 53-mile stretch. At 5:30 AM on February 2, 1925, they stumbled into Nome through whiteout conditions, the serum still frozen but intact. The relay had taken 127.5 hours—normally a 25-day journey by conventional transportation. The Great Race of Mercy demonstrated what collaboration and courage could achieve. While Balto received immediate fame, mushers later recognized that Togo had conquered the most perilous sections. Both dogs proved that true heroism often runs on four legs through impossible cold.

articles.topic_title: Origami: the math hidden in paper folding
articles.title: The Hidden Mathematics of Paper Folding
articles.passage_html: A single pull extends a massive solar panel into space, unfurling like a metal flower blooming in the darkness. This seemingly simple motion relies on the Miura fold, a folding pattern invented by astrophysicist Koryo Miura in 1970 that transforms flat sheets into compact, deployable structures. The secret behind this spacecraft technology? The same mathematical principles that guide the ancient art of origami. Origami is far more than a charming craft—it is a rigorous mathematical discipline that deserves recognition alongside geometry and algebra in our understanding of spatial reasoning. While many people view paper folding as merely decorative, the mathematical foundations embedded within each crease reveal sophisticated relationships between angles, ratios, and dimensions that can solve real-world engineering challenges. First, origami demonstrates precise mathematical relationships that can be proven and replicated. Japanese mathematician Kazuo Haga discovered in the 1970s that folding a square paper so one corner touches the opposite edge creates a crease with an exact 1:2:√5 ratio relationship. This wasn't a lucky accident—it's a mathematical certainty that occurs every single time. Similarly, the traditional origami crane requires exactly 17 precise folds, each one creating angular measurements and proportions that never vary. In 2002, researchers at MIT proved mathematically that any shape with straight edges and flat surfaces can theoretically be folded from a single sheet of paper using computational algorithms. These aren't artistic guidelines; they're mathematical theorems as reliable as the Pythagorean formula. Second, origami mathematics solves practical problems that traditional engineering cannot address as efficiently. Robert Lang, an American physicist and origami artist, used mathematical principles to design an origami insect with 6 legs, 2 antennae, and 4 wings from a single uncut square of paper in the 1990s. His work required computational planning using box-pleating techniques, where the paper is divided into grids like 32x32 squares. This mathematical approach to folding now helps engineers design heart stents that collapse for insertion into arteries, airbags that fold compactly in steering wheels, and yes, solar panels that journey into space. The ancient rule requiring folders to use 'one cut, one sheet' wasn't simply tradition—it was a mathematical constraint that forced creative problem-solving within defined parameters. When Akisato Rito published the first known written origami instructions in 1797 in 'Sembazuru Orikata,' showing how to fold connected paper cranes, he was documenting mathematical sequences disguised as art. Perhaps the next time you see a paper crane, you'll recognize it for what it truly is: a theorem you can hold in your hand.

articles.topic_title: The Sherpas of Mount Everest
articles.title: Sherpas Deserve Recognition as True Everest Experts
articles.passage_html: On May 29, 1953, Tenzing Norgay planted his foot on the highest point on Earth alongside Edmund Hillary. While history books often celebrate Hillary as the conqueror of Everest, Norgay and his fellow Sherpas remain the true masters of the mountain. The Sherpa people, who migrated from Tibet to Nepal's high-altitude Solu-Khumbu region 500 years ago, deserve full recognition as the world's greatest mountaineering experts. First, Sherpas possess biological advantages that make them uniquely suited to extreme heights. Scientists discovered that Sherpas carry the EPAS1 gene, which allows their bodies to process oxygen with remarkable efficiency where oxygen levels drop 40% below normal. This genetic adaptation developed over generations of living in villages perched between 12,000 and 14,000 feet, where thin air would leave most people gasping. No artificial equipment can match this natural ability. Second, their achievements speak louder than any argument. Kami Rita Sherpa reached Everest's summit 28 times by May 2023—a record no foreign climber approaches. Lhakpa Sherpa, who now lives in America, conquered the peak 10 times, more than any other woman. These aren't just numbers; they represent years of skill, knowledge, and courage on ice slopes that have claimed countless lives, including 13 Sherpas killed in a single 2014 avalanche. Yet despite their unmatched expertise, Sherpas earn only $3,000 to $6,000 per climbing season while guiding clients who pay over $50,000. Their contribution to mountaineering extends beyond carrying equipment through perilous conditions. They protect climbers, predict weather patterns, and navigate routes through deadly ice falls near Namche Bazaar and beyond. The world celebrates mountaineers who stand on Everest's summit, but rarely asks: Who led them there?

articles.topic_title: The Lantern Festival: lights of Chinese New Year
articles.title: Why the Lantern Festival Deserves Global Recognition
articles.passage_html: Thousands of glowing red spheres float skyward as a dragon stretches seventy meters through crowded streets, animated by fifty performers moving in perfect rhythm. This spectacle unfolds each year during the Lantern Festival, which marks the final day of Chinese New Year celebrations on the fifteenth day of the first lunar month. Though this festival has captivated millions across China for over 2,000 years, it deserves far greater recognition worldwide as one of humanity's most remarkable cultural celebrations. The Lantern Festival merits international acclaim because of its profound historical roots that connect ancient traditions to modern practice. The festival originated during the Han Dynasty (206 BCE – 220 CE), when Emperor Ming of Han promoted Buddhism and commanded that lanterns illuminate the imperial palace and temples to honor Buddha. This initial religious observance evolved over centuries, with each dynasty adding layers of meaning. During the Song Dynasty (960-1279 CE), scholars introduced caidengmi—the tradition of solving riddles written on paper and attached to lanterns. These riddles challenged participants' wit and knowledge, transforming the festival into an intellectual celebration as well as a visual one. Today, cities like Zigong in Sichuan Province create elaborate lantern installations exceeding 100 meters in length, powered by thousands of LED lights that blend ancient artistry with contemporary technology. Beyond its rich history, the festival deserves global attention because it strengthens family bonds through shared rituals and symbols. Families gather to eat yuanxiao, round glutinous rice balls filled with sweet paste, whose circular shape represents family reunion and togetherness. The predominant red color of lanterns symbolizes good fortune and joy in Chinese culture, creating a unified visual message that transcends language barriers. These traditions possess universal appeal—every culture values family unity and the hope for prosperity. The festival demonstrates how simple acts, like sharing special food or lighting lanterns together, can reinforce connections between generations. Recognition came late in official terms; China only established the Lantern Festival as a public holiday in 2008, despite its two-millennium existence. This recent designation highlights how even ancient traditions require contemporary validation. When people worldwide understand the festival's historical depth, intellectual tradition, and celebration of human connection, they recognize a cultural treasure that illuminates universal values through distinctly Chinese expression.

articles.topic_title: The history of the high-five (invented in 1977)
articles.title: How a Spontaneous Gesture Became a Celebration
articles.passage_html: Dusty Baker's bat cracked against the ball, sending it soaring over the outfield fence. The crowd at Dodger Stadium roared as Baker rounded the bases on October 2, 1977, celebrating his 30th home run of the season. Waiting at home plate, his 23-year-old teammate Glenn Burke felt the energy of the moment. Without thinking, Burke thrust his hand high above his head. Baker didn't hesitate. He slapped Burke's raised palm, and the high-five was born. Neither player could have predicted how this spontaneous gesture would spread. At first, the move seemed like just another way teammates celebrated on the field. But something about the high-five felt different—it was quick, energetic, and infectious. The University of Louisville basketball team noticed. During the 1978-1979 season, they adopted the celebration, propelling it beyond baseball stadiums into gymnasiums nationwide. By the early 1980s, the high-five had transcended professional sports entirely. Kids on playgrounds, friends at school, even strangers used it to mark moments of success or connection. Glenn Burke played only 225 Major League games before his career ended in 1979, but his legacy outlasted any statistic. Today, millions of people worldwide raise their hands in celebration, often without knowing they're commemorating that October afternoon when two Dodgers created a gesture that would unite people across generations.

He slapped Burke's raised palm, and the high-five was born.

At first, the move seemed like just another way teammates celebrated on the field. But something about the high-five felt different—it was quick, energetic, and <vocab>infectious|spreading easily to others</vocab>. The University of Louisville basketball team noticed. During the 1978-1979 season, they adopted the celebration, <vocab>propelling|pushing forward with force</vocab> it beyond baseball stadiums into gymnasiums nationwide.

By the early 1980s, the high-five had <vocab>transcended|gone beyond limits</vocab> professional sports entirely. Kids on playgrounds, friends at school, even strangers used it to mark moments of success or connection. Glenn Burke played only 225 Major League games before his career ended in 1979, but his <vocab>legacy|something handed down from the past</vocab> outlasted any statistic. Today, millions of people worldwide raise their hands in celebration, often without knowing they're <vocab>commemorating|honoring the memory of</vocab> that October afternoon when two Dodgers created a gesture that would unite people across generations.

articles.topic_title: Pizza: from poor people's food to global icon
articles.title: Pizza's Journey from Streets to Stardom
articles.passage_html: In 1889, chef Raffaele Esposito nervously arranged tomatoes, mozzarella, and basil atop a flatbread crust. Queen Margherita of Italy would soon taste his creation. This moment transformed pizza from humble street food into something celebrated worldwide. Before that royal meal, pizza belonged to Naples' poorest workers. Street vendors sold the inexpensive flatbreads to people who owned no plates or forks. Customers folded the oily slices and ate while standing. Nobody imagined this simple food would become a global phenomenon. Italian immigrants carried pizza recipes across the Atlantic Ocean in the late 1800s. Gennaro Lombardi opened America's first pizzeria in New York City in 1905, introducing the dish to curious customers. Still, pizza remained relatively unknown outside immigrant neighborhoods. World War II sparked pizza's dramatic rise. American soldiers stationed in Italy between 1941 and 1945 developed cravings for the savory pies. Returning home, they searched for pizza in their own cities. By 1958, brothers Dan and Frank Carney founded Pizza Hut in Wichita, Kansas, launching the chain restaurant era. Today's numbers tell pizza's remarkable story. Americans devour approximately 3 billion pizzas yearly—46 slices per person. The global pizza market exceeds 145 billion dollars. In 2017, UNESCO recognized Neapolitan pizza-making as significant cultural heritage. What once fed Naples' workers now feeds the world.

Gennaro Lombardi opened America's first pizzeria in New York City in 1905, introducing the dish to curious customers. Still, pizza remained <vocab>relatively|compared to something else</vocab> unknown outside immigrant neighborhoods.

World War II sparked pizza's dramatic rise. American soldiers <vocab>stationed|assigned to a place</vocab> in Italy between 1941 and 1945 developed cravings for the savory pies. Returning home, they searched for pizza in their own cities. By 1958, brothers Dan and Frank Carney founded Pizza Hut in Wichita, Kansas, launching the chain restaurant era.

Today's numbers tell pizza's remarkable story. Americans devour approximately 3 billion pizzas yearly—46 slices per person. The global pizza market <vocab>exceeds|goes beyond</vocab> 145 billion dollars. In 2017, UNESCO recognized Neapolitan pizza-making as <vocab>significant|important and meaningful</vocab> cultural heritage. What once fed Naples' workers now feeds the world.

options: A food once sold to workers without plates now generates over 145 billion dollars globally and receives cultural recognition from UNESCO.

questions.explanation: This answer synthesizes information from the beginning (poor workers, no plates) and end (145 billion dollar market, UNESCO recognition) to show pizza's complete transformation.

articles.topic_title: Captain Cook: mapping the Pacific Ocean
articles.title: Charting the Unknown: Cook's Pacific Adventures
articles.passage_html: The HMS Endeavour creaked and groaned as waves slammed against its wooden hull, spray misting the deck where Captain James Cook bent over his charts. The 106-foot vessel, packed with 94 crew members, plunged through swells that stretched endlessly in every direction. Cook's ink-stained fingers traced coastlines that no European map had ever shown, transforming blank spaces into detailed shores. In August 1768, the Endeavour departed Plymouth, England, beginning the first of Cook's three monumental voyages across the Pacific Ocean. Between 1768 and 1779, Cook would revolutionize European understanding of the world's largest ocean, filling charts with islands and continents that sailors had only imagined. His first journey alone proved that New Zealand consisted of two separate islands rather than forming part of a mysterious southern continent that many believed existed. Cook's mapping achievements surpassed anything explorers had accomplished before. In 1770, he charted over 5,000 miles of Australia's eastern coastline, claiming the territory for Britain and naming it New South Wales. The captain's pen scratched across parchment as he recorded every bay, headland, and river mouth with meticulous attention to detail. During his second voyage from 1772 to 1775, Cook pushed his ship into freezing Antarctic waters, becoming the first explorer to cross the Antarctic Circle. On January 17, 1773, he reached 71 degrees south latitude, where colossal icebergs towered above his masts like frozen mountains. Cook's expeditions also brought Hawaii, Tahiti, Tonga, and Easter Island onto European maps with remarkable accuracy. His charts proved so reliable that British naval ships continued sailing by them into the 1900s—more than a century after his death on February 14, 1779, in Kealakekua Bay, Hawaii, during a violent confrontation with islanders. The blank spaces on eighteenth-century maps represented more than missing geography; they symbolized an entire ocean concealed from European knowledge. Cook's voyages transformed the Pacific from a realm of speculation into a mapped reality, connecting distant shores through the steady work of observation, measurement, and an explorer's determination to see what lay beyond the horizon.

articles.topic_title: China's Great Green Wall: holding back the desert
articles.title: China's Great Green Wall Fights the Desert
articles.passage_html: Golden sand swirls through the air, coating windows and streets in a choking dust storm. This scene once plagued Beijing 56 days each year during the 1950s, as winds carried the Gobi Desert's grit hundreds of miles eastward. Today, these storms strike the capital only about 10 days annually—a dramatic transformation brought about by one of Earth's most ambitious environmental projects. China's Great Green Wall, officially called the Three-North Shelter Forest Program, began in 1978 with an extraordinary goal: plant a 4,500-kilometer barrier of trees across northern China by 2050. The Gobi Desert had been spreading relentlessly, swallowing roughly 3,600 square kilometers of farmland each year—an area larger than Rhode Island. Communities watched helplessly as their fertile fields turned to barren wasteland. The response was unprecedented. By 2018, workers had planted more than 66 billion trees, creating forests covering an area larger than Germany. This green barrier spans 13 provinces, including Inner Mongolia, Shaanxi, and Gansu. In the Kubuqi Desert, the results are tangible: approximately 6,000 square kilometers have shrunk back since the 1980s. Forest coverage in the Three-North region has surged from 5.05% in 1977 to 13.57% by 2017. Scientists project it will reach 15% by 2050. Each seedling represents a small testament to human determination—proof that deserts need not always advance, and that patience can turn sand into soil.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?

questions.explanation: The passage focuses on how China's tree-planting program has reduced desert expansion and dust storms, making option 0 correct. Option 1 is a supporting detail, not the main idea. Option 2 is true but too general and not stated in the passage. Option 3 makes a comparison not supported by the passage.

questions.stem: The author mentions the decrease in Beijing's dust storm days in order to...

questions.stem: The passage suggests that the Great Green Wall project...
options: was completed ahead of schedule due to advanced technology
has already achieved its final goal of 15% forest coverage

Option 1 is false—13.57% has been reached, not 15%. Option 3 overstates the goal; the passage shows slowing, not complete stopping, of expansion.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word 'relentlessly' most nearly means...
options: surprisingly
occasionally

continuously
questions.explanation: The context describes the desert 'swallowing' land each year without stopping, making 'continuously' the best match. The spreading was ongoing and persistent, not surprising, occasional, or necessarily violent.

options: admiring
skeptical
neutral
cautionary
questions.explanation: Words like 'dramatic transformation,' 'extraordinary goal,' and 'testament to human determination' reveal the author's positive, admiring tone. The passage emphasizes successes without expressing doubt (skeptical), remaining completely objective (neutral), or warning of dangers (cautionary).

articles.topic_title: The Great Pacific Garbage Patch: one man's cleanup mission
articles.title: Cleaning the Ocean, One Invention at a Time
articles.passage_html: When Boyan Slat was sixteen, he went scuba diving in Greece and saw more plastic bags than fish. That single dive changed everything. Three years later, in 2013, the Dutch inventor founded The Ocean Cleanup at age eighteen. His mission? To rid the world's oceans of floating plastic pollution. Slat's target was formidable: the Great Pacific Garbage Patch, a swirling mass of trash floating between Hawaii and California. Scientists estimate this patch contains approximately 1.8 trillion pieces of plastic weighing about 80,000 metric tons. The debris field covers 1.6 million square kilometers—roughly twice the size of Texas. Microplastics, pieces smaller than five millimeters, make up 94 percent of those trillion floating fragments. Slat designed enormous floating barriers to intercept the drifting plastic. In September 2018, The Ocean Cleanup launched System 001, a 600-meter-long barrier that used ocean currents to gather debris. But System 001 broke apart in rough seas. Critics questioned whether such an ambitious plan could succeed. October 2019 marked the turning point. System 002 successfully extracted plastic from the patch, proving the technology worked. Then in July 2021, the organization deployed an improved version nicknamed "Jenny." During one extraction, Jenny hauled in 63,182 pounds of trash—fishing nets, buoys, crates, and baskets tangled together. The cleanup continues today. Slat's team aims to remove 90 percent of floating ocean plastic by 2040 using a fleet of these systems. Each retrieval brings hope that human innovation can remedy human mistakes. After all, if an eighteen-year-old's idea can pull thousands of pounds of trash from the middle of the Pacific, perhaps the ocean's future isn't as polluted as its present.

articles.topic_title: The Library of Alexandria: what we lost when it burned
articles.title: The Greatest Library the World Ever Lost
articles.passage_html: A sailor watched nervously as Alexandria's harbor guards confiscated his ship's books in 48 BCE. This was routine—the library demanded every scroll for copying. But as Julius Caesar's warships clashed nearby, flames leaped from burning vessels toward the city's warehouses. What happened next would erase knowledge that took centuries to gather. Built around 283 BCE under Ptolemy II Philadelphus, the Library of Alexandria represented an audacious goal: collect all the world's knowledge in one place. At its peak, between 400,000 and 700,000 scrolls lined its shelves. Greek philosophy sat beside Egyptian medicine and Persian astronomy. Indian mathematics mixed with works from civilizations across the known world. Ships arriving at Alexandria's harbor faced an unusual law—surrender all books for copying. The library kept the originals and returned copies to the owners, a practice that steadily enriched its collection. The Museion, which housed the library, employed approximately 100 scholars at a time. These researchers received free room, board, and salaries to study whatever intrigued them. Around 245 BCE, head librarian Eratosthenes calculated Earth's circumference and came within 2-4% of the modern measurement of 40,075 kilometers. Centuries later, Hero of Alexandria invented the aeolipile, an early steam engine, around 50 CE—technology Europe wouldn't rediscover until the 1600s. The library didn't vanish in one catastrophic moment. Instead, it suffered multiple disasters. In 48 BCE, during Caesar's civil war, fires from the harbor spread through parts of the city, destroying irreplaceable sections of the collection. Other incidents followed over the centuries. Consider the playwright Sophocles, who wrote approximately 123 plays. Only seven survived to modern times. The rest—116 works—disappeared, likely consumed when ancient libraries burned. Multiply that loss across thousands of authors, scientists, and philosophers. We'll never know what revelations those scrolls contained, what medical cures or mathematical proofs turned to ash. The Library of Alexandria didn't just store books. It safeguarded human curiosity itself—and when it burned, we lost answers to questions we didn't even know to ask.

questions.explanation: The passage's central focus is on what was lost when the library burned, using specific examples like Sophocles' plays and Hero's inventions to illustrate the irreplaceable knowledge that disappeared.

questions.stem: The author mentions the library's policy of confiscating books from ships in order to
options: explain why sailors feared arriving at Alexandria's harbor during wartime
illustrate one method the library used to steadily expand its vast collection
show that ancient libraries competed with each other to own the most scrolls
demonstrate that Ptolemy II Philadelphus wanted to control all knowledge
questions.explanation: The passage explicitly states this practice 'steadily enriched its collection,' showing how the library grew to contain 400,000-700,000 scrolls from diverse civilizations.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that Hero of Alexandria's steam engine invention was particularly remarkable because
options: it proved that ancient scholars understood physics better than modern scientists
it was powered entirely by water instead of coal or other fuel sources
similar technology would not appear again in Europe for over 1,500 years
he created it without receiving any salary or financial support from the Museion
questions.explanation: The passage states Hero invented the aeolipile around 50 CE and that Europe wouldn't rediscover this technology until the 1600s, suggesting a gap of over 1,500 years.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "audacious" most nearly means
options: impossible to achieve
expensive and wasteful
requiring great wealth
daringly ambitious
questions.explanation: The context describes the library's goal to collect 'all the world's knowledge,' which was bold and daring, making 'daringly ambitious' the best match for audacious.

questions.stem: Which best describes how the passage is organized?
options: introducing a dramatic moment, then explaining the library's history and significance before returning to its destruction
comparing the Library of Alexandria to modern libraries to show how knowledge storage has changed
describing events in strict chronological order from the library's founding to its final burning
presenting different theories about what caused the library's destruction, then evaluating each theory
questions.explanation: The passage opens with the 48 BCE fire scene, then moves backward to explain the library's founding and achievements, before returning to discuss its destruction and losses.

articles.topic_title: Vertical farms: growing lettuce in skyscrapers
articles.title: Growing Greens in the Sky
articles.passage_html: Inside a massive warehouse in Newark, New Jersey, rows of lettuce sprout from white towers that stretch toward the ceiling. No soil clings to their roots. No sunlight streams through windows. Instead, thousands of purple-pink LED lights bathe the leafy greens in an eerie glow. This is AeroFarms, a 69,000-square-foot vertical farm that opened in 2016 and can produce up to 2 million pounds of lettuce and herbs each year. Vertical farms grow crops indoors, stacking plants in layers like books on shelves. These farms utilize LED lights engineered to provide the exact light spectrum plants need for photosynthesis. Compared to traditional high-pressure sodium lamps, these LEDs consume 40% less energy. The lights hum softly as they work, creating an artificial sunrise that never sets. Water efficiency makes vertical farming particularly innovative. Traditional outdoor farms lose water to evaporation and runoff, but vertical farms recycle 95% of their water through closed-loop systems. Plants absorb what they need, then sensors collect the excess moisture and pump it back to the roots. In Copenhagen, Denmark, the world's largest vertical farm spans 75,000 square feet and produces approximately 1,000 tons of lettuce and herbs annually using this water-saving technology. Space savings prove equally impressive. A single acre of vertical farming can yield the same amount of lettuce as 10 to 20 acres of traditional field farming. At Plenty Unlimited's facility in Compton, California, strawberries climb towers reaching 30 feet tall inside climate-controlled warehouses. Singapore's Sky Greens farm uses rotating aluminum towers powered by hydraulic systems that gently turn vegetables like Chinese cabbage through the light, ensuring every leaf receives equal exposure. Speed completes the vertical farming advantage. From seed to harvest, lettuce matures in just 30 to 35 days—approximately half the time required for soil-based farming. As cities expand and farmland shrinks, these towering indoor gardens might transform how we feed growing populations.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: Vertical farms use innovative technology to grow crops indoors more efficiently than traditional farming methods.
AeroFarms in Newark, New Jersey is the most important vertical farm in the United States.
Vertical farms will completely replace traditional outdoor farming within the next decade.
LED lights are more energy-efficient than high-pressure sodium lamps for growing plants.
questions.explanation: The passage describes multiple aspects of vertical farming efficiency (water use, space, speed, energy) rather than focusing on one farm or making predictions about replacing traditional farming.

questions.stem: The author mentions closed-loop water systems in order to
options: explain how vertical farms monitor plant growth using sensors
demonstrate how vertical farms achieve their water efficiency
compare water usage between different vertical farms around the world
describe the most expensive technology used in vertical farming

questions.stem: The passage suggests that vertical farms would be especially useful in
options: rural areas with abundant farmland and water supplies
regions where traditional farming produces the highest crop yields
densely populated areas where land for traditional farming is limited
countries that prefer to import most of their fresh produce
questions.explanation: The passage emphasizes space efficiency and ends by noting these farms might help 'as cities expand and farmland shrinks,' suggesting they address land scarcity in urban areas.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word 'spectrum' most nearly means
options: brightness level
electrical current
temperature range
range of colors
questions.explanation: The passage uses 'spectrum' to describe the exact light wavelengths (colors) that LEDs provide for photosynthesis, not brightness, electricity, or temperature.

questions.stem: Which statement best summarizes the relationship between vertical farming technology and crop production time?
options: Controlled indoor conditions enable lettuce to mature in half the time required by traditional farming.
LED lights allow vertical farms to grow crops year-round regardless of season.
Vertical farms can produce more crops because they stack plants in multiple layers.
Water recycling systems help plants grow faster than soil-based methods.
questions.explanation: The passage directly states lettuce matures in 30-35 days, 'approximately half the time required for soil-based farming,' connecting the controlled technology to faster growth. Other options mention real advantages but not the speed relationship.

articles.topic_title: The Thai Cave Rescue: 13 boys, 18 days, and a global team (2018)
articles.title: Why the Thai Cave Rescue Proved Cooperation Saves Lives
articles.passage_html: When British divers John Volanthen and Rick Stanton plunged through murky floodwater on July 2, 2018, they had no guarantee they would find anyone alive. Nine days earlier, twelve soccer players and their coach had vanished into Tham Luang cave in Thailand's Chiang Rai province. Then, four kilometers from the entrance and 1,000 meters underground, flashlight beams illuminated thirteen faces. The boys were alive. The Thai cave rescue demonstrates that international cooperation achieves what individual nations cannot. First, the complex rescue required specialized skills from multiple countries. More than 10,000 volunteers participated, including approximately 100 divers from Thailand, the United States, Australia, China, and the United Kingdom. Dr. Richard Harris, an Australian anesthesiologist and cave diver, determined which boys were healthy enough to be rescued first—a crucial decision no single expert could make alone. Second, the mission demanded extraordinary resources and sacrifice. Saman Kunan, a 38-year-old former Thai Navy SEAL, died on July 6 while delivering oxygen tanks through the flooded passages. His death reminded rescuers that success required not just equipment but shared commitment. The intricate three-day extraction from July 8-10 involved sedating the boys and navigating them through narrow, submerged tunnels—a technique developed through international collaboration. When the final four boys and their coach emerged on July 10, ending the 18-day ordeal, the world witnessed what happens when nations place human life above borders. Some challenges are simply too immense for any country to face alone.

The <vocab>intricate|having many small parts or details</vocab> three-day extraction from July 8-10 involved sedating the boys and <vocab>navigating|finding a way through</vocab> them through narrow, submerged tunnels—a technique developed through international collaboration.

vocab.definition: having many small parts or details

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: The Thai cave rescue succeeded because countries worked together and pooled their specialized skills and resources.
British divers discovered thirteen people trapped deep inside a flooded cave in Thailand after nine days of searching.
Dr. Richard Harris made important medical decisions that saved the lives of the trapped soccer team.
The rescue operation was dangerous because divers had to navigate through narrow, flooded passages.
questions.explanation: The passage argues that international cooperation was essential to the rescue's success, supporting this claim with examples of specialized skills and shared resources from multiple countries.

questions.stem: The author mentions Dr. Richard Harris in order to
options: explain how Australian medical experts train for cave rescue operations
illustrate that the rescue required specialized expertise from multiple countries
show that doctors are better equipped for cave rescues than professional divers
demonstrate that medical decisions were more important than diving skills
questions.explanation: Dr. Harris is presented as an example supporting the claim that international cooperation brought specialized skills—his role as an Australian expert illustrates the passage's argument about multinational collaboration.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that without international cooperation, the rescue would have
options: taken place much earlier because fewer people would have caused less confusion
been impossible since the boys were trapped too deep underground for any single team
succeeded anyway because the Thai Navy SEALs had all the necessary diving equipment
been less likely to succeed because no single country possessed all the required expertise and resources
questions.explanation: The passage argues that the rescue's complexity and demands exceeded what individual nations could provide, implying that success depended on combining international skills and resources.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "intricate" most nearly means
options: dangerous
expensive
successful
detailed
questions.explanation: The passage uses 'intricate' to describe the three-day extraction process involving sedation and navigation through narrow tunnels, emphasizing its many complex steps and details.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the international rescue effort can best be described as
options: admiring
skeptical
neutral
cautionary
questions.explanation: The author presents the international cooperation positively, highlighting achievements and sacrifices while arguing that such collaboration 'achieves what individual nations cannot,' revealing an admiring tone.

articles.topic_title: The Globe Theatre: Shakespeare's wooden O
articles.title: The Wooden O That Burned and Rose Again
articles.passage_html: Sparks flew from the cannon. The theatrical gun fired during the scene in Henry VIII, just as the script demanded. No one worried—until flames began licking the thatched roof above the stage. On June 29, 1613, spectators at London's Globe Theatre scrambled from their seats as fire consumed the wooden structure. Within hours, Shakespeare's famous playhouse had vanished. The Globe had stood for only fourteen years, yet it had become the heart of English theater. Built in 1599 by Shakespeare's company, the Lord Chamberlain's Men, the theater rose from timber salvaged from their previous playhouse simply called The Theatre. The builders constructed a polygon with twenty or twenty-four sides, measuring roughly 100 feet across. This design created what Shakespeare himself called a "wooden O" in the prologue to Henry V—a circular wooden building with an empty center where actors performed beneath open sky. Nearly 3,000 people could pack inside. Commoners called groundlings paid one penny to stand in the yard, exposed to weather and pigeons. Wealthier patrons paid more for seats in the covered galleries that ringed the space. All eyes focused on the same stage, where Shakespeare's words brought kings, lovers, and ghosts to life. After the 1613 fire, the company refused to abandon their venture. They rebuilt the Globe on the same site within a year, making one crucial change: they replaced the flammable thatch with fireproof tiles. This second Globe thrived until 1642, when austere Puritan leaders closed all London theaters, viewing them as immoral. Centuries passed. The Globe's location faded from memory. Then American actor Sam Wanamaker visited London in 1970 and discovered nothing marked where Shakespeare's theater once stood. He founded the Shakespeare Globe Trust and devoted twenty-three years to recreating the lost playhouse. Wanamaker died before construction finished, but in 1997, a faithful replica opened approximately 750 feet from the original site. Today's Globe proves that some stories refuse to end. Like the phoenix of ancient legend, the wooden O has returned—rebuilt, remembered, and ready for new audiences to gather beneath its open sky.

No one worried—until flames began licking the thatched roof above the stage. On June 29, 1613, spectators at London's Globe Theatre scrambled from their seats as fire consumed the wooden structure. Within hours, Shakespeare's famous playhouse had vanished.

The Globe had stood for only fourteen years, yet it had become the heart of English theater. Built in 1599 by Shakespeare's company, the Lord Chamberlain's Men, the theater rose from timber salvaged from their previous playhouse simply called The Theatre. The builders constructed a polygon with twenty or twenty-four sides, measuring roughly 100 feet across. This design created what Shakespeare himself called a "wooden O" in the prologue to Henry V—a circular wooden building with an empty center where actors performed beneath open sky.

Nearly 3,000 people could pack inside. Commoners called groundlings paid one penny to stand in the yard, exposed to weather and pigeons. Wealthier patrons paid more for seats in the covered galleries that ringed the space. All eyes focused on the same stage, where Shakespeare's words brought kings, lovers, and ghosts to life.

After the 1613 fire, the company refused to abandon their <vocab>venture|risky business project</vocab>. They rebuilt the Globe on the same site within a year, making one crucial change: they replaced the flammable thatch with fireproof tiles. This second Globe thrived until 1642, when <vocab>austere|strictly disciplined and severe</vocab> Puritan leaders closed all London theaters, viewing them as <vocab>immoral|wrong or wicked in behavior</vocab>.

Centuries passed. The Globe's location faded from memory. Then American actor Sam Wanamaker visited London in 1970 and discovered nothing marked where Shakespeare's theater once stood. He founded the Shakespeare Globe Trust and devoted twenty-three years to recreating the lost playhouse. Wanamaker died before construction finished, but in 1997, a faithful <vocab>replica|exact copy</vocab> opened approximately 750 feet from the original site.

vocab.definition: relating to the theater or performances
mythical bird that rises from its own ashes

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: The Globe Theatre has been destroyed and rebuilt multiple times, demonstrating the lasting power of Shakespeare's theatrical legacy.
The Globe Theatre was an important building in London that burned down in 1613 during a performance.
Sam Wanamaker spent his entire life working to recreate historical buildings in England.
Shakespeare wrote many famous plays that were performed at various theaters throughout London.
questions.explanation: The passage traces the Globe's destruction, rebuilding in 1614, loss, and modern recreation, emphasizing its enduring significance. Option B covers only part of the story, option C misstates Wanamaker's focus (23 years, not entire life), and option D shifts focus from the Globe to Shakespeare's general work.

questions.stem: The author mentions the difference between groundlings and wealthier patrons in order to
options: explain why the Globe Theatre eventually closed in 1642
illustrate the social divisions reflected in the theater's seating arrangements
prove that Shakespeare's plays appealed only to educated audiences
describe the various jobs available at theaters in Shakespeare's time
questions.explanation: The passage contrasts one-penny groundlings standing in the yard with wealthier patrons in covered galleries to show how social class determined theater experience. Option A connects unrelated facts, option C contradicts the passage (3,000 people of different classes attended), and option D invents content not discussed.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that the 1614 Globe was built with tiles instead of thatch because
options: tiles were cheaper and easier to find than thatched roofing materials
Puritan authorities required all new buildings to use fireproof materials
the builders wanted to prevent another fire like the one that destroyed the first Globe
Sam Wanamaker recommended tiles when he consulted on the reconstruction project
questions.explanation: The passage states the cannon misfired and 'ignited the thatched roof,' then notes the rebuild made 'one crucial change' to 'fireproof tiles,' implying fire prevention motivated the switch. Option A might be true generally but isn't supported, option B is false (Puritans closed theaters later), and option D confuses the 1614 and 1997 reconstructions.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "austere" most nearly means
options: wealthy and powerful
foreign and unfamiliar
harsh and strict
ancient and outdated
questions.explanation: The passage describes Puritan leaders as 'austere' in the context of closing theaters they viewed as immoral, indicating strictness and severity. Options A, B, and D don't capture the disciplined, severe quality that characterizes Puritan attitudes toward entertainment.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the rebuilding of the Globe Theatre can best be described as
options: admiring
skeptical
neutral
cautionary
questions.explanation: The author uses positive language like 'refused to abandon,' describes Wanamaker's 'devoted' efforts, and ends with the phoenix metaphor and phrase 'ready for new audiences,' showing appreciation and respect. The passage doesn't question the project (skeptical), remain detached (neutral), or warn against it (cautionary).

articles.topic_title: Frida Kahlo: how a bus accident made her a painter
articles.title: From Medical Student to Masterpiece Maker
articles.passage_html: On September 17, 1925, the wooden bus carrying eighteen-year-old Frida Kahlo through Mexico City collided with an electric streetcar. The impact shattered the bus into jagged pieces. A steel handrail tore through Frida's body, piercing her left hip and exiting through her pelvis. Her spinal column broke in three places. Her right leg snapped in eleven separate spots. Before the accident, Frida had been studying to become a doctor at the National Preparatory School, one of only 35 girls among 2,000 students. Those dreams vanished as she lay immobile in a full body cast for three months. Her mother installed a special easel so Frida could paint while lying flat. A mirror hung above the bed, reflecting Frida's own face back at her. In 1926, during her slow recovery, Frida painted her first self-portrait as a gift for her boyfriend Alejandro, who had survived the crash beside her. The chronic pain from her injuries never disappeared. Throughout her lifetime, she endured at least 30 surgeries. Yet this suffering became her art. Of her 200 total paintings, 143 were inspired by the accident's pain. At least 55 were self-portraits, her face transformed from medical student to one of history's most distinctive artists. The bus crash that destroyed her body created her vision.
articles.passage_raw:
On September 17, 1925, the wooden bus carrying eighteen-year-old Frida Kahlo through Mexico City <vocab>collided|crashed violently</vocab> with an electric streetcar. The impact shattered the bus into jagged pieces. A steel handrail tore through Frida's body, piercing her left hip and exiting through her pelvis. Her spinal column broke in three places. Her right leg snapped in eleven separate spots.

Before the accident, Frida had been studying to become a doctor at the National Preparatory School, one of only 35 girls among 2,000 students. Those dreams vanished as she lay <vocab>immobile|unable to move</vocab> in a full body cast for three months. Her mother installed a special easel so Frida could paint while lying flat. A mirror hung above the bed, reflecting Frida's own face back at her.

In 1926, during her slow recovery, Frida painted her first self-portrait as a gift for her boyfriend Alejandro, who had survived the crash beside her. The <vocab>chronic|lasting a long time</vocab> pain from her injuries never disappeared. Throughout her lifetime, she <vocab>endured|suffered through</vocab> at least 30 surgeries. Yet this suffering became her art. Of her 200 total paintings, 143 were inspired by the accident's pain. At least 55 were self-portraits, her face <vocab>transformed|changed completely</vocab> from medical student to one of history's most <vocab>distinctive|unique and recognizable</vocab> artists. The bus crash that destroyed her body created her vision.

questions.stem: Which would be the BEST title for this passage?
options: How a Terrible Accident Changed Frida Kahlo's Career Path
The Most Famous Self-Portrait Painter in Mexican History
Why Bus Safety Improved in Mexico City After 1925
Frida Kahlo: A Pioneer for Women in Medical School
questions.explanation: The passage focuses on how the 1925 bus accident ended Frida's medical studies and led her to become a painter, making this the central idea.

questions.stem: The author mentions Frida's mother installing a special easel in order to
options: explain how Frida learned to paint from professional artists
show how Frida's family helped her paint despite being bedridden
describe the type of equipment all painters used in 1926
prove that Frida had always wanted to become an artist
questions.explanation: The passage states the mother installed an easel that allowed Frida to paint while lying down, demonstrating family support during her immobile recovery period.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that Frida's self-portraits were
options: painted only for her boyfriend Alejandro throughout her career
created because she had no money to hire other models
influenced by the physical and emotional pain she experienced
considered less important than her other 145 paintings
questions.explanation: The passage states 143 paintings were inspired by accident-related pain and 55 self-portraits 'often depicted her physical suffering,' suggesting pain influenced this work.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "endured" most nearly means
options: avoided completely
scheduled carefully
requested repeatedly
survived painfully
questions.explanation: In context, 'endured at least 30 surgeries' means she survived or suffered through them, as the passage emphasizes her chronic pain and suffering.

questions.stem: Which best describes how the passage is organized?
options: describing a traumatic event, then explaining its long-term consequences
comparing Frida's art before and after she became famous
presenting arguments for why accidents can inspire creativity
listing all of Frida Kahlo's paintings in chronological order
questions.explanation: The passage begins with the 1925 bus accident details, then describes how this event led to her painting career and influenced her artwork.

articles.topic_title: Empress Dowager Cixi: China's most powerful woman
articles.title: The Concubine Who Ruled an Empire
articles.passage_html: The sixteen-year-old girl trembled as palace guards ushered her through the crimson gates of Beijing's Forbidden City in 1851. Cixi had arrived as a low-ranking concubine to Emperor Xianfeng, one of hundreds of women competing for imperial attention. Few could have imagined she would command China's fate for nearly half a century. Cixi's ascent began in 1856 when she gave birth to the emperor's only surviving son, Zaichun. This single event transformed her status overnight. When Emperor Xianfeng died in 1861, Cixi refused to fade into the background. She orchestrated a palace coup and seized control as co-regent for her five-year-old son, the new Tongzhi Emperor. For 47 years, until her death in 1908, Cixi made decisions for three different emperors. Her rule revealed stark contradictions. She diverted 30 million taels of silver meant for China's navy to rebuild the Summer Palace instead—a decision that left the nation vulnerable to foreign attack. In 1900, she supported the Boxer Rebellion against foreign influence, which backfired disastrously when eight nations invaded and forced her to flee Beijing. Yet late in life, Cixi reversed course. In 1905, she abolished the civil service examination system that had existed for 1,300 years, signaling China's reluctant march toward modernization. She died on November 15, 1908—suspiciously, just one day after the emperor she had confined under house arrest for a decade.

Few could have imagined she would command China's fate for nearly half a century.

Cixi's <vocab>ascent|rise to power</vocab> began in 1856 when she gave birth to the emperor's only surviving son, Zaichun. This single event transformed her status overnight. When Emperor Xianfeng died in 1861, Cixi refused to fade into the background. She <vocab>orchestrated|carefully planned and executed</vocab> a palace coup and seized control as co-regent for her five-year-old son, the new Tongzhi Emperor.

For 47 years, until her death in 1908, Cixi made decisions for three different emperors. Her rule revealed stark contradictions. She <vocab>diverted|redirected from the original purpose</vocab> 30 million taels of silver meant for China's navy to rebuild the Summer Palace instead—a decision that left the nation <vocab>vulnerable|exposed to danger</vocab> to foreign attack. In 1900, she supported the Boxer Rebellion against foreign influence, which backfired disastrously when eight nations invaded and forced her to flee Beijing.

Yet late in life, Cixi reversed course. In 1905, she abolished the civil service examination system that had existed for 1,300 years, signaling China's <vocab>reluctant|unwilling or hesitant</vocab> march toward modernization. She died on November 15, 1908—suspiciously, just one day after the emperor she had <vocab>confined|imprisoned or restricted</vocab> under house arrest for a decade.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: Cixi rose from humble origins to rule China for nearly fifty years, making both destructive and progressive decisions.
China's civil service examination system was abolished after more than a thousand years of tradition.
The Forbidden City in Beijing served as the home of Chinese emperors and their families.
Women in ancient China faced significant obstacles when trying to gain political influence.
questions.explanation: The passage traces Cixi's rise from concubine to ruler and discusses both her poor decisions (diverting navy funds, supporting the Boxer Rebellion) and progressive ones (modernization reforms), making option A correct. Option B is a detail, not the main idea. Option C is barely mentioned. Option D is true in general but not the passage's focus.

questions.stem: The author mentions the Summer Palace in order to
options: illustrate the architectural achievements of the Qing Dynasty
demonstrate how Cixi's personal priorities sometimes harmed China's security
explain why China's navy became the strongest in Asia during her reign
describe the location where Cixi hid during the Boxer Rebellion
questions.explanation: The passage states Cixi diverted 30 million taels meant for the navy to rebuild the Summer Palace, leaving China vulnerable—showing poor prioritization. Option A is plausible but not stated. Option C contradicts the passage. Option D uses passage words (Summer Palace, Boxer Rebellion) but misconnects them.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that Cixi's decision to support the Boxer Rebellion
options: strengthened China's relationship with European nations
demonstrated her consistent opposition to all forms of foreign contact
revealed her willingness to take risks that sometimes resulted in serious consequences
was universally supported by Chinese citizens throughout the empire
questions.explanation: The passage describes how supporting the Boxer Rebellion 'backfired disastrously' when eight nations invaded, showing Cixi took a risk with severe consequences. Option A contradicts the invasion. Option B is partially true but ignores her later modernization efforts. Option D is not mentioned in the passage.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "orchestrated" most nearly means
options: conducted a musical performance
accidentally caused to happen
observed from a distance
skillfully arranged and directed
questions.explanation: The passage describes Cixi orchestrating a palace coup to seize power, meaning she carefully planned and executed it. Option D captures this meaning. Option A is the literal musical meaning. Options B and C contradict the intentional nature of a coup.

questions.stem: Which best describes how the passage is organized?
options: It presents events in chronological order, from Cixi's arrival at the palace to her death.
It compares Cixi's leadership style with that of other Chinese emperors.
It states a problem with China's government, then proposes multiple solutions.
It describes the Forbidden City's architecture, then explains its cultural significance.
questions.explanation: The passage follows a clear timeline: 1851 (arrival), 1856 (son's birth), 1861 (coup), 1900 (Boxer Rebellion), 1905 (reforms), 1908 (death). Option B is not done in the passage. Option C misidentifies the structure. Option D focuses on a minor detail, not the overall organization.

articles.topic_title: Junko Tabei: first woman to climb Mount Everest
articles.title: Breaking Barriers: Junko Tabei's Mountain Legacy
articles.passage_html: Snow crashed down the mountain slope at 21,325 feet, swallowing tents and climbers in darkness. Junko Tabei felt the crushing weight of an avalanche pin her beneath frozen layers on May 4, 1975. Her Sherpa guides clawed through the snow, dragging her back to safety. Many climbers would have retreated after such a harrowing experience. Tabei did not. Born on September 22, 1939, in Miharu, a small town in Fukushima Prefecture, Japan, Tabei grew up in a society where women faced constant scrutiny for pursuing activities considered masculine. Yet mountains called to her. In 1969, she founded the Ladies Climbing Club of Japan—the first climbing club for women in her country. This bold move challenged traditional expectations and created opportunities for other female climbers. The 1975 Everest expedition tested every limit. Just twelve days after the avalanche nearly killed her, Tabei stood beside her Sherpa guide Ang Tsering at 29,032 feet above sea level. On May 16, 1975, she became the first woman to reach Mount Everest's summit. The achievement demonstrated remarkable perseverance, but Tabei refused to stop there. Her climbing career flourished over the following decades. She conquered Mount Kilimanjaro in Tanzania (19,341 feet) in 1980 and Mount McKinley in Alaska (20,310 feet) in 1988. By 1992, she had scaled the highest peak on each of the seven continents, becoming the first woman to complete the Seven Summits. Each mountain demanded different skills, different preparation, and different courage. Tabei died on October 20, 2016, at age 77 in Kawagoe, Japan, after battling cancer for four years. She had proven that determination could shatter barriers as solid as ice. Her legacy extends beyond altitude records—she showed that pathways once closed can be forced open, inspiring countless women to climb their own mountains, literal or otherwise.

The achievement demonstrated remarkable <vocab>perseverance|continued effort despite difficulties</vocab>, but Tabei refused to stop there.
