questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: Junko Tabei overcame social barriers and physical dangers to become the first woman to climb Mount Everest and the Seven Summits.
Mount Everest is the world's highest mountain and presents extreme dangers including avalanches to all climbers.
Women in Japan during the 1960s and 1970s faced discrimination when trying to participate in outdoor sports.
The Seven Summits challenge requires climbers to scale the highest mountain on each of the seven continents.
questions.explanation: The passage focuses on Tabei's specific achievements and her role in breaking barriers for women climbers, supported by concrete examples throughout. The other options address isolated details or general facts not central to the passage's focus on Tabei's accomplishments.

questions.stem: The author mentions the avalanche on May 4, 1975, in order to
options: explain why most climbers fail to reach the summit of Mount Everest
demonstrate the dangerous conditions Tabei overcame just days before her historic summit
describe the typical weather patterns found at high altitudes on Everest
show that Sherpa guides are essential for all mountain climbing expeditions
questions.explanation: The passage emphasizes that Tabei reached the summit just twelve days after this life-threatening event, highlighting her perseverance. The other options represent facts that may be generally true but are not the author's purpose for including this specific detail.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that Tabei's founding of the Ladies Climbing Club in 1969
options: was supported enthusiastically by the Japanese climbing community
required her to obtain special government permission
represented a challenge to traditional views about women's roles
attracted hundreds of female climbers in its first year
questions.explanation: The passage states that women faced scrutiny for pursuing 'masculine' activities and calls the club's founding a 'bold move' that 'challenged traditional expectations,' supporting this inference. The other options introduce specific details not stated or implied in the passage.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word 'flourished' most nearly means
options: decorated beautifully
became wealthy
thrived and prospered
moved quickly
questions.explanation: The context describes Tabei's climbing career growing successfully over decades with multiple achievements. 'Thrived and prospered' best captures this meaning. While 'flourished' can mean 'decorated' in other contexts, that meaning doesn't fit here.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward Junko Tabei can best be described as
options: admiring
skeptical
neutral
cautionary
questions.explanation: The author uses positive language throughout, highlighting Tabei's perseverance, courage, and legacy, and describes her achievements as 'remarkable' and 'bold.' This indicates clear admiration rather than neutrality, skepticism, or caution.

articles.topic_title: The Trans-Siberian Railway: connecting a continent
articles.title: Iron Rails Across a Frozen Continent
articles.passage_html: In 1891, the first spike was driven into frozen ground near Vladivostok, marking the beginning of an engineering endeavor unlike any before. Tsar Alexander III had ordered construction of a railway to connect Moscow, 5,772 miles away on Russia's western edge, to the Pacific coast. Workers would need to cross eight time zones, sixteen major rivers, and temperature extremes ranging from -40°F to 95°F. The construction presented immediate challenges. At Lake Baikal, the world's deepest freshwater lake plunging 5,387 feet down, engineers devised a temporary solution: a massive ice-breaking ferry that could transport entire trains across the water. This ingenious vessel operated until 1904, when workers finally completed a permanent track circling the southern shore. Each year, crews laid an average of 740 miles of new track, hammering through formidable terrain that included dense forests, rocky mountains, and vast stretches of empty steppe. Twenty-five years after construction began, the Trans-Siberian Railway opened in 1916 as the longest single railway line on Earth. During World War II, its strategic importance became clear when it transported 30 percent of Soviet military supplies eastward, beyond German reach. Today, trains rumble across 3,901 bridges, carrying 200,000 containers of freight and 30 million passengers annually. This steel ribbon remains a vital artery, pumping goods and people between two continents separated by a six-day journey.

Workers would need to cross eight time zones, sixteen major rivers, and temperature extremes ranging from -40°F to 95°F.

The construction presented immediate challenges. At Lake Baikal, the world's deepest freshwater lake plunging 5,387 feet down, engineers devised a temporary solution: a massive ice-breaking ferry that could transport entire trains across the water. This <vocab>ingenious|clever and inventive</vocab> vessel operated until 1904, when workers finally completed a permanent track circling the southern shore. Each year, crews laid an average of 740 miles of new track, hammering through <vocab>formidable|impressively difficult or powerful</vocab> terrain that included dense forests, rocky mountains, and vast stretches of empty steppe.

Twenty-five years after construction began, the Trans-Siberian Railway opened in 1916 as the longest single railway line on Earth. During World War II, its strategic importance became clear when it transported 30 percent of Soviet military supplies eastward, beyond German reach. Today, trains rumble across 3,901 bridges, carrying 200,000 containers of freight and 30 million passengers annually. This steel ribbon remains a <vocab>vital|absolutely necessary</vocab> <vocab>artery|a main route for transportation or communication</vocab>, pumping goods and people between two continents separated by a six-day journey.

vocab.definition: a difficult project requiring serious effort
a main route for transportation or communication

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: The Trans-Siberian Railway was a monumental engineering project that connected Russia's vast territory and continues to serve as a crucial transportation link.
Tsar Alexander III commissioned the Trans-Siberian Railway in 1891 to transport military supplies across Russia.
Workers on the Trans-Siberian Railway faced extreme temperatures and difficult terrain during twenty-five years of construction.
The Trans-Siberian Railway is the longest single railway line in the world, stretching 5,772 miles across eight time zones.
questions.explanation: This answer captures both the historical achievement of building the railway and its ongoing importance, which the passage emphasizes throughout. The other options focus on single details rather than the overall central idea.

questions.stem: The author mentions the ice-breaking ferry at Lake Baikal in order to
options: explain why the railway took longer to complete than originally planned
illustrate the creative problem-solving required during construction
describe the deepest freshwater lake in the world
show how engineers transported passengers across the water
questions.explanation: The passage presents the ferry as an 'ingenious' temporary solution to a difficult obstacle, emphasizing the innovative thinking needed. While Lake Baikal's depth is mentioned, that detail provides context rather than serving as the main purpose of mentioning the ferry.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that the Trans-Siberian Railway's role during World War II was
options: limited because German forces controlled the western sections of track
disappointing since it could not transport enough military equipment quickly
strategically significant because it moved supplies away from enemy territory
surprising given that most military supplies traveled by ship instead
questions.explanation: The passage states the railway transported 30 percent of Soviet supplies 'eastward, beyond German reach,' indicating its strategic value in keeping resources safe from the enemy. This implies significant importance.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "formidable" most nearly means
options: frozen
distant
dangerous
challenging
questions.explanation: The passage uses 'formidable terrain' to describe the difficult landscape workers had to cross, including forests, mountains, and empty plains. 'Challenging' best captures this meaning of impressively difficult.

questions.stem: Which statement best summarizes the relationship between the railway's construction challenges and its current importance?
options: The extreme effort required to build the railway across difficult terrain resulted in a transportation system that remains essential for moving goods and people today.
The railway's construction difficulties made it too expensive to maintain, though it still carries some freight between Europe and Asia.
Because workers faced harsh temperatures during construction, the railway now only operates during summer months when conditions are safer.
The challenges of building across multiple time zones created scheduling problems that continue to limit the railway's usefulness for passengers.
questions.explanation: This answer correctly connects the construction challenges described in the middle section with the railway's vital modern role mentioned at the end, showing how the difficult achievement led to lasting importance. The passage emphasizes current heavy use, contradicting the other options.

articles.topic_title: Pompeii: a city frozen in volcanic ash
articles.title: Pompeii: The City Time Forgot
articles.passage_html: A loaf of bread sits in a stone oven, its crust still perfectly rounded after nearly two thousand years. Outside, painted advertisements cover the walls, and someone's hurried message—scratched into plaster—announces a gladiator match. This is Pompeii, where everyday life stopped abruptly on August 24, 79 AD, when Mount Vesuvius erupted and buried the entire Roman city under 13 to 20 feet of volcanic ash and pumice. The eruption released thermal energy equivalent to approximately 100,000 times the atomic bombs dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki. Pliny the Younger, a seventeen-year-old Roman writer watching from across the Bay of Naples, described a massive cloud rising from the mountain. His letters to the historian Tacitus provide the only surviving eyewitness account of the disaster. Between 11,000 and 15,000 residents lived in Pompeii that day, and excavations have uncovered more than 1,150 bodies in the city and surrounding areas. For approximately 1,500 years, Pompeii remained entombed beneath layers of hardened ash. The city's location became uncertain, its streets and buildings forgotten. Then, in 1748, Spanish engineer Rocque Joaquin de Alcubierre accidentally rediscovered the buried city while digging a well. What he found was not a pile of ruins, but a sanctuary perfectly preserved by volcanic materials. The ash had created a natural time capsule. Colorful wall frescoes—paintings on fresh plaster—retained their vivid reds and golds. Graffiti covered the walls, recording ancient jokes, political slogans, and declarations of love. Even more remarkable were the human remains. As bodies decomposed within the hardened ash, they left hollow spaces. Archaeologists discovered they could pour plaster into these voids, creating casts that captured people's final moments in haunting detail. About 100 such casts now exist, showing mothers clutching children, families huddled together, and individuals frozen mid-stride. Today, Pompeii serves as an invaluable window into ancient Roman civilization. The volcanic ash that destroyed the city also safeguarded its secrets, offering modern researchers a snapshot of daily life from 79 AD. Each excavated street reveals how ordinary people lived, worked, and—on one terrible August day—perished.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: Pompeii's destruction by Mount Vesuvius preserved the ancient city, providing modern researchers with detailed insights into Roman daily life.
Mount Vesuvius erupted with tremendous force in 79 AD, killing thousands of residents in the nearby city of Pompeii.
Spanish engineer Rocque Joaquin de Alcubierre made one of history's greatest archaeological discoveries when he found Pompeii in 1748.
Archaeologists have created plaster casts of Pompeii's victims by filling the spaces their bodies left in hardened volcanic ash.
questions.explanation: The passage focuses on how volcanic ash both destroyed and preserved Pompeii, making it valuable for understanding ancient Roman life. The other options mention important details but miss the dual nature of destruction and preservation that is the passage's central theme.

questions.stem: The author mentions Pliny the Younger in order to
options: explain how historians learned that approximately 15,000 people lived in Pompeii
show that some people survived the eruption by watching it from a safe distance
provide evidence that an eyewitness documented the volcanic eruption as it occurred
prove that the eruption could be seen from cities throughout ancient Italy
questions.explanation: The passage states that Pliny the Younger's letters to Tacitus 'provide the only surviving eyewitness account of the disaster.' Option A is not stated, option B uses passage information (watching from Naples) but draws an incorrect conclusion about the author's purpose, and option D makes an unsupported claim about 'throughout ancient Italy.'

questions.stem: The passage suggests that without the volcanic ash,
options: Pompeii would likely have been destroyed by earthquakes within a few decades
archaeologists would never have known that the city of Pompeii existed
historians would have less detailed knowledge of everyday life in ancient Rome
the bodies of Pompeii's victims would have been preserved in a different way
questions.explanation: The passage emphasizes how the ash preserved everyday objects, frescoes, and details that serve as 'an invaluable window into ancient Roman civilization.' Without this preservation, such detailed knowledge would not exist. Options A and D are not supported by the passage, and option B is incorrect because the passage mentions the city was 'largely forgotten,' not completely unknown.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word 'decomposed' most nearly means
options: separated into different groups
became less important over time
broke down into simpler parts
changed shape under pressure
questions.explanation: The passage explains that bodies 'decomposed within the hardened ash,' leaving hollow spaces where plaster could be poured. This context indicates the bodies broke down and decayed, matching option C. The other options represent alternative meanings of 'decompose' or related words but don't fit this context.

questions.stem: Which best describes how the passage is organized?
options: describing the disaster, explaining the burial period, then discussing the rediscovery and modern significance
comparing ancient Pompeii to modern archaeological sites around the world
presenting a problem with understanding ancient Rome, then offering Pompeii as the solution
following the life of one Pompeii resident from birth through the volcanic eruption
questions.explanation: The passage follows a chronological structure: the eruption (paragraphs 1-2), the burial and rediscovery (paragraph 3), and the preservation and modern value (paragraphs 4-5). Options B and D describe patterns not present in the passage, and option C partially fits but misrepresents the opening focus on the disaster itself.

articles.topic_title: Zheng He's treasure ships: China's voyages to Africa
articles.title: Zheng He's Voyages Proved China's Maritime Power
articles.passage_html: In 1414, a giraffe stepped off a Chinese ship onto the docks of Beijing. The tall creature, with its spotted coat and impossibly long neck, had traveled thousands of miles from Africa aboard one of Admiral Zheng He's massive treasure ships. Emperor Yongle gazed at the animal in wonder, convinced it was a qilin—a mythical beast that appeared only when a wise ruler governed. This moment symbolized more than a diplomatic gift. It demonstrated China's unparalleled maritime power during the early 1400s. Zheng He's seven voyages between 1405 and 1433 proved that China possessed the world's most advanced seafaring capabilities. Consider the scale of his first expedition in 1405: more than 300 ships carrying approximately 28,000 sailors, soldiers, and craftsmen. The largest treasure ships measured roughly 400 feet long and 160 feet wide—the biggest wooden vessels of their era. Each massive ship featured nine masts towering above the deck and required 500 crew members to operate. No other nation could construct or command such a fleet. While European explorers would later gain fame for sailing much smaller ships across the Atlantic, China had already mastered oceanic travel decades earlier. The distances Zheng He covered further illustrate China's maritime dominance. His fleet reached the eastern coast of Africa during the fourth voyage in 1413-1415, visiting present-day Kenya and Somalia. These journeys required sophisticated navigation techniques and provisions to sustain thousands of sailors for months at sea. The ships carried Chinese porcelain, silk, and lacquerware, which crews traded for African ivory, rhinoceros horn, and exotic animals. Such long-distance trade networks demanded not just shipbuilding excellence but also organizational skill and geographical knowledge that surpassed anything Europe possessed at the time. Finally, the magnitude of resources China devoted to these voyages revealed a government confident in its wealth and power. Born in Yunnan Province in 1371, Zheng He commanded these expeditions under Emperor Yongle's orders until his death in 1433, likely during his seventh voyage. The emperor funded this enormous maritime program for nearly three decades, demonstrating China's economic strength. When that giraffe arrived in Beijing, it carried proof of something remarkable. China had projected its power across the Indian Ocean to Africa and back, decades before European ships would attempt similar feats. The treasure fleet's achievements remain evidence of medieval China's technological and organizational superiority at sea.

relating to the sea or sailing

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: Zheng He's voyages between 1405 and 1433 demonstrated China's superior maritime capabilities compared to other nations of that era.
Admiral Zheng He commanded the largest wooden ships ever built and traveled farther than any other explorer in history.
China's treasure ships brought exotic animals from Africa that the emperor believed were mythical creatures bringing good fortune.
Emperor Yongle funded Zheng He's expeditions to establish trade relationships with African leaders and expand China's wealth.
questions.explanation: The passage argues that Zheng He's voyages proved China's unparalleled maritime power through ship size, distance traveled, and resources devoted to the expeditions.

questions.stem: The author mentions the crew size of 500 sailors per treasure ship in order to
options: explain why China needed to recruit sailors from different provinces throughout the empire
emphasize the massive scale and complexity of operating the world's largest wooden ships
contrast the working conditions aboard Chinese ships with those on European vessels
describe the different roles sailors performed during the multi-year voyages to Africa
questions.explanation: The passage uses the 500-person crew requirement to support its argument about the unprecedented scale of China's maritime capabilities.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that European maritime exploration
options: relied on Chinese navigation techniques that Zheng He's sailors had developed during earlier voyages
focused primarily on reaching Africa rather than crossing the Atlantic Ocean to the Americas
occurred later than and with smaller resources compared to China's treasure fleet expeditions
attempted to copy the design of Chinese treasure ships but lacked the engineering knowledge
questions.explanation: The passage states China 'mastered oceanic travel decades earlier' than famous European explorers who sailed 'much smaller ships,' indicating European exploration came later and on a smaller scale.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word 'magnitude' most nearly means
options: uncertainty
distance
expense
scale
questions.explanation: The passage uses 'magnitude' to describe the great size and importance of resources China devoted to the voyages, making 'scale' the best synonym.

questions.stem: Which statement best summarizes the relationship between the giraffe's arrival and China's maritime achievements?
options: The giraffe served as physical evidence that China could successfully transport goods and animals across vast oceanic distances.
The giraffe convinced Emperor Yongle to continue funding Zheng He's expeditions for another two decades after 1414.
The giraffe proved that African leaders valued Chinese porcelain and silk more than their own ivory and exotic animals.
The giraffe demonstrated that Zheng He had discovered sea routes to Africa that European explorers never found.
questions.explanation: The passage frames the giraffe's arrival as symbolic proof of China's maritime power—specifically its ability to travel thousands of miles to Africa and back, which connects to multiple facts about ship size, distance, and trade.

articles.topic_title: The Japanese tea ceremony: 500 years of meaning
articles.title: Five Centuries of Grace in a Tea Bowl
articles.passage_html: A guest kneels before a doorway barely two feet tall. To enter the tea room, she must bow low, leaving pride and status outside. Inside, the tranquil space measures exactly 4.5 tatami mats—roughly the size of a small bedroom. Soft light filters through paper screens. The air smells faintly of incense and fresh bamboo. This is the chashitsu, where Japan's tea ceremony has unfolded for five hundred years. In the 1500s, a man named Sen no Rikyū revolutionized how Japanese people thought about tea. Before Rikyū, wealthy nobles hosted elaborate tea gatherings to display their expensive treasures. Rikyū, who lived from 1522 to 1591, stripped away the excess. He taught that the ceremony should embody simplicity and harmony instead. Every movement held meaning. Every object served a purpose. The tea itself—matcha—arrives as a vibrant green powder. A Buddhist monk named Eisai brought this powdered tea from China to Japan in 1191. During the ceremony, the host scoops matcha into a bowl, then pours water heated to exactly 80 degrees Celsius. Boiling water at 100 degrees would ruin the delicate flavor, making it taste harsh and bitter. Next comes the whisk called a chasen, carved from a single piece of bamboo. Its 80 to 120 thin prongs blur into motion, blending powder and water into frothy liquid. A formal tea gathering, the chaji, demands patience and attention. Guests spend approximately four hours following more than one hundred specific movements. They share a meal called kaiseki, drink multiple servings of tea, and observe precise gestures that have remained unchanged for generations. Today, the Urasenke school in Kyoto, founded by Rikyū's great-grandson in 1622, teaches these traditions to roughly two million students worldwide. The Japanese government recognizes certain tea ceremony utensils as Important Cultural Properties, honoring master craftsmen whose work preserves history. In tea ceremony, a simple bowl becomes a bridge across centuries. The steam rising from matcha carries whispers of ancient wisdom: that beauty lives not in extravagance, but in the grace of a single, mindful moment.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: The Japanese tea ceremony evolved from an elaborate display of wealth into a spiritual practice emphasizing simplicity, with traditions that continue today.
Sen no Rikyū invented the Japanese tea ceremony in the 1500s and taught it to millions of students around the world.
The chashitsu is a small room where Japanese people drink matcha tea that was brought from China by Buddhist monks.
Tea ceremony utensils are considered Important Cultural Properties because they are made by master craftsmen in Kyoto.

articles.topic_title: Tu Youyou: finding malaria's cure in ancient texts
articles.title: Ancient Wisdom Defeats a Modern Killer
articles.passage_html: In 1971, Chinese scientist Tu Youyou held a yellowed manuscript from the year 340 CE in her hands. The text, "A Handbook of Prescriptions for Emergencies" by Ge Hong, described treating fevers with sweet wormwood soaked in cold water. For four years, Tu's research team had tested hundreds of plant extracts against malaria—a disease killing soldiers fighting in the Vietnam War. Nothing worked. Tu Youyou's discovery proves that ancient knowledge can solve modern problems when scientists approach it with fresh thinking. First, traditional texts provided the crucial insight that conventional methods had missed. After reviewing over 2,000 recipes from Chinese medical history, Tu realized Ge Hong's emphasis on cold water meant heat destroyed the plant's power. She abandoned the standard boiling method and extracted the compound artemisinin at low temperatures instead. Second, Tu demonstrated extraordinary courage by testing the unknown substance on herself first in 1972 before giving it to patients. This bold decision accelerated research that would eventually rescue millions. Third, her work produced remarkable results. Between 2000 and 2015, artemisinin-based treatments reduced malaria deaths worldwide by 60 percent, according to the World Health Organization. When Tu Youyou received the Nobel Prize in 2015 at age 84, she became China's first female Nobel laureate in medicine. Her achievement reveals that solving today's catastrophic health challenges sometimes requires looking backward through centuries of wisdom.

articles.topic_title: Galileo's telescope: changing our place in the universe
articles.title: The Telescope That Shook the Heavens
articles.passage_html: On a November night in 1609, Galileo Galilei aimed his handmade telescope at the Moon and gasped. Instead of the smooth, perfect sphere philosophers had described for centuries, he saw jagged mountains and deep craters casting shadows across the lunar surface. Some peaks towered at least four miles high. The heavens, it seemed, were not so different from Earth after all. Galileo had constructed his first telescope months earlier in Padua, Italy, after learning about a Dutch invention. While the original spyglasses magnified objects only three times, Galileo's apparatus achieved twenty-times magnification. He turned this powerful tool skyward, and what he discovered would shatter humanity's understanding of the universe. In January 1610, Galileo focused on Jupiter and spotted four small lights moving around the giant planet. Night after night, he tracked these moons—Io, Europa, Ganymede, and Callisto—as they orbited Jupiter. This observation demolished the ancient belief that everything circled Earth. He also noticed Venus displaying phases like the Moon, appearing as a crescent, then half-circle, then full. These changes only made sense if Venus traveled around the Sun. Galileo published his findings in a slim sixty-page book, "Sidereus Nuncius" (The Starry Messenger), which became an instant sensation across Europe. Yet his discoveries carried a dangerous price. The Roman Catholic Church, viewing his Sun-centered model as heresy, put him on trial in 1633. Found guilty, Galileo spent his final nine years under house arrest. Still, those nights peering through ground glass had already transformed everything. Earth was no longer the universe's center—just one planet among many, spinning through an ocean of stars.

articles.topic_title: Inky the octopus: the great escape from Seattle Aquarium
articles.title: Inky's Daring Escape to the Ocean
articles.passage_html: One morning in April 2016, staff members at the National Aquarium of New Zealand arrived at work to find Inky's tank empty. The common octopus, about the size of a rugby ball, had vanished. Only telltale sucker prints stretched across the floor, leading toward a drainpipe. The clever invertebrate had pulled off one of the most famous escapes in aquarium history. Inky's journey to freedom began months earlier when a local fisherman caught him in a crayfish pot near Napier, a coastal city approximately 210 miles north of Wellington on New Zealand's North Island. The fisherman delivered Inky to the National Aquarium of New Zealand, where the octopus settled into his new home. Yet Inky never forgot the ocean. The escape required both timing and flexibility. Aquarium workers believe Inky squeezed through a gap at the top of his tank measuring just six inches wide. How could an animal the size of a rugby ball fit through such a narrow opening? Common octopuses possess an extraordinary anatomical advantage: they have no bones except for a hard beak. This means Inky could compress his soft body through any space larger than his beak, transforming from a bulky creature into something that could slip through surprisingly small openings. After escaping his enclosure, Inky faced a 164-foot journey across the aquarium floor. He slithered toward a drainpipe that led directly to Hawke's Bay and the Pacific Ocean beyond. The vulnerable octopus moved swiftly, leaving only those sucker prints as evidence of his path. Down the long drainpipe he traveled, propelled by eight arms working in perfect coordination. Inky's escape captivated people worldwide because it demonstrated something scientists had long observed: octopuses possess exceptional problem-solving abilities. These intelligent creatures can navigate mazes, open jars, and apparently, plan elaborate escapes when the opportunity presents itself. Inky's story reminds us that wild animals, no matter how comfortable their surroundings, often retain an instinct to return home. Somewhere in the Pacific Ocean, one clever octopus swims free, having outwitted his keepers and reclaimed the vast ocean as his own.

articles.topic_title: Iceland's geothermal pools: heating a country with volcanoes
articles.title: Iceland's Underground Solution to Cold Winters
articles.passage_html: Steam rises from pipes snaking across Iceland's frozen landscape, carrying water heated by the earth itself. Inside homes throughout Reykjavik, radiators warm living rooms without burning a single fossil fuel. This water arrives hot from deep underground, a gift from the same volcanic forces that shaped this North Atlantic island. Iceland should harness geothermal energy for its primary power source because this approach offers both environmental and economic advantages that few other countries can match. Two compelling reasons demonstrate why this volcanic energy represents the smartest choice for Iceland's future. First, geothermal energy provides reliable, clean power that dramatically reduces pollution. In 2020, geothermal sources supplied 66% of Iceland's primary energy consumption, with 90% of homes heated directly by hot water piped from underground springs. Unlike coal or oil, this method releases no smoke into the atmosphere. The Hellisheiði Power Station, which opened in 2006, generates 303 megawatts of electricity and 400 megawatts of thermal energy, powering thousands of homes while keeping Iceland's air crystal clear. The Blue Lagoon near Grindavik—where turquoise water maintains temperatures between 37-39 degrees Celsius year-round—actually contains wastewater from the nearby Svartsengi power plant, proving that even byproducts can serve useful purposes. Second, Iceland's unique geology makes geothermal energy remarkably efficient. The island sits on the Mid-Atlantic Ridge where two massive tectonic plates meet, creating approximately 30 active volcanic systems. At the Krafla Power Station in northeastern Iceland, workers drill boreholes 2,000-3,000 meters deep to access steam reaching 200-300 degrees Celsius. The Nesjavellir station, located 30 kilometers southeast of Reykjavik, produces 1,800 liters of hot water per second for the capital's heating system. This abundant resource flows continuously, unlike solar panels that depend on sunshine or wind turbines that require steady breezes. Geothermal energy even supports unexpected industries. Iceland's geothermal greenhouses cover approximately 50 hectares, where tomatoes, cucumbers, and peppers flourish despite winter darkness, producing about 2,000 tons of vegetables annually. Reykjavik pioneered this technology in 1930, becoming the world's first city with a geothermal district heating system. While volcanoes might seem dangerous neighbors, Iceland has transformed potential hazards into powerful solutions. The same forces that occasionally shake the ground now warm nearly every home, power cities, and grow winter vegetables under Arctic skies.

articles.topic_title: The night Berliners climbed the Wall (1989)
articles.title: The Night the Wall Came Down
articles.passage_html: At 6:53 PM on November 9, 1989, a government spokesman named Günter Schabowski stepped before cameras in East Berlin. He shuffled his papers and announced that East Germans could now cross into West Berlin. A reporter shouted: "When does this take effect?" Schabowski glanced down, confused. "Immediately," he answered. Those words ignited a spark. Within hours, thousands of East Berliners rushed toward the border checkpoints. They weren't sure if the announcement was real, but they had waited 28 years—since August 13, 1961—for this moment. The Berlin Wall, stretching 96 miles around their city, had imprisoned them in their own homes. Now, perhaps, that barrier would finally open. The largest crowd surged toward the Bornholmer Street crossing. Border guards peered through their stations at the growing mob. They had received no official orders. Sweat dripped down their faces as the crowd pressed closer, chanting and demanding passage. At 11:30 PM, one guard made a decision that changed history. He lifted the gate. East Berliners streamed across the border like water bursting through a dam. On the other side, West Berliners waited with flowers and champagne bottles. Strangers embraced. People wept. Car horns blared in jubilant celebration. Throughout the night, the festivities continued as families separated for nearly three decades finally reunited. But the most powerful image emerged near the Brandenburg Gate. Berliners from both sides climbed onto the Wall itself—that formidable concrete structure that had divided their city. They swung hammers and chisels, chipping away pieces to keep as mementos. Each strike against the concrete echoed through the streets. The Wall that had stood as an impenetrable symbol of division was crumbling, one chip at a time. Within weeks, over 2 million East Germans visited West Berlin to glimpse the world they had been forbidden to see. The night of November 9 proved that even the strongest walls eventually fall when people refuse to accept them.

He lifted the gate.

East Berliners streamed across the border like water bursting through a dam. On the other side, West Berliners waited with flowers and champagne bottles. Strangers embraced. People wept. Car horns blared in <vocab>jubilant|expressing great joy</vocab> celebration. Throughout the night, the festivities continued as families separated for nearly three decades finally reunited.

But the most powerful image emerged near the Brandenburg Gate. Berliners from both sides climbed onto the Wall itself—that <vocab>formidable|inspiring fear or respect through size or power</vocab> concrete structure that had divided their city. They swung hammers and chisels, chipping away pieces to keep as <vocab>mementos|objects kept as reminders</vocab>. Each strike against the concrete echoed through the streets. The Wall that had stood as an <vocab>impenetrable|impossible to pass through</vocab> symbol of division was crumbling, one chip at a time.

Within weeks, over 2 million East Germans visited West Berlin to glimpse the world they had been forbidden to see. The night of November 9 proved that even the strongest walls eventually fall when people refuse to accept them.

vocab.definition: inspiring fear or respect through size or power

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: A confused announcement by a government spokesman led to the opening of the Berlin Wall and the joyful reunion of a divided city.
The Berlin Wall was built in 1961 and stretched 96 miles around the city of Berlin.
Border guards at the Bornholmer Street checkpoint made an important decision on November 9, 1989.
East and West Berliners celebrated with flowers and champagne when the wall finally opened.
questions.explanation: The passage focuses on how Schabowski's announcement triggered the wall's opening and the celebrations that followed, making option A the best summary of the central idea.

questions.stem: The author mentions that the border guard "had received no official orders" in order to
options: explain why East Germans were confused about whether they could cross the border
show that the guard's decision to open the gate was brave and unexpected
prove that Günter Schabowski did not plan his announcement carefully
describe the communication problems in the East German government
questions.explanation: The passage emphasizes this detail to highlight the guard's courageous decision to open the gate despite lacking official permission, making it a turning point in the narrative.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that before November 9, 1989, East Berliners
options: had planned for years to destroy the Berlin Wall with hammers and chisels
frequently visited West Berlin but had to return home each night
were prevented from freely traveling to the western part of their own city
celebrated every year on August 13 to remember when the Wall was built
questions.explanation: The passage states that the Wall "imprisoned them in their own homes" for 28 years and that millions visited West Berlin "for the first time," indicating they were previously prevented from crossing.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "surged" most nearly means
options: walked slowly
gathered peacefully
stood waiting
rushed powerfully
questions.explanation: The word "surged" describes the crowd moving forcefully toward the checkpoint, consistent with the urgent and powerful movement described in the passage.

questions.stem: Which best describes how the passage is organized?
options: describing events in chronological order from the announcement through the celebrations
comparing life in East Berlin with life in West Berlin before 1989
presenting a problem with the Berlin Wall, then explaining how it was solved
stating an opinion about the Wall, then supporting it with historical facts
questions.explanation: The passage follows a clear timeline: Schabowski's 6:53 PM announcement, crowds gathering, the 11:30 PM gate opening, and the celebrations that followed through the night.

articles.topic_title: Why we say OK: the strangest word in English
articles.title: OK: The World's Most Unusual Word
articles.passage_html: When NASA astronauts struggled to save Apollo 13 in 1970, they used one word 402 times to confirm that systems were working. That word wasn't "affirmative" or "confirmed." It was simply "OK." This humble two-letter word, spoken billions of times daily, began as a joke in a Boston newspaper. On March 23, 1839, the Boston Morning Post printed a peculiar abbreviation: "OK," standing for "oll korrect," a deliberate misspelling of "all correct." Newspapers in the 1830s loved creating silly shortened words, much like internet slang today. Most of these playful inventions vanished within weeks. OK, however, survived—and thrived. The word gained momentum during the 1840 presidential campaign. Martin Van Buren's supporters created "OK Clubs" because the president's nickname was "Old Kinderhook," after his New York hometown. Political rallies echoed with chants of "OK!" Soon, Americans across the country recognized the term, though many had forgotten its absurd origins as a misspelling joke. Then came the telegraph. When Samuel Morse's invention connected cities with clicking electrical signals, operators needed quick ways to acknowledge messages. OK became the perfect response—faster to tap out than "received" or "understood." The word zipped along copper wires, spreading from coast to coast throughout the 1840s. For decades, scholars puzzled over OK's true beginnings. Some claimed it came from Greek, others from Native American languages. In 1963, Columbia University professor Allen Walker Read finally solved the mystery by carefully examining old newspapers. His research revealed the Boston Morning Post joke, conclusively ending the debate. Today, OK appears in more than 600 languages worldwide. Linguist John McWhorter calculated that English speakers say it roughly three times daily. The Oxford English Dictionary officially recognized OK in 1961, after 122 years of use. From a newspaper editor's silly prank to a word understood from Tokyo to Toronto, OK achieved what no intentional invention could: becoming humanity's most universal expression of agreement. Sometimes the strangest beginnings lead to the most extraordinary journeys.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: OK evolved from a humorous newspaper joke in 1839 to become one of the most widely recognized words across the world.
The word OK was invented by telegraph operators who needed a quick way to acknowledge messages.
Professor Allen Walker Read discovered that OK came from the Boston Morning Post newspaper.
NASA astronauts used OK 402 times during the Apollo 13 mission to communicate effectively.
questions.explanation: The passage traces OK's journey from its origin as a joke through various stages to its current status as a global word, making this the central idea.

questions.stem: The author mentions the telegraph in order to
options: explain how Samuel Morse invented a new communication system in the 1840s
demonstrate how technology helped spread OK rapidly across America
show that OK was the first word ever transmitted by telegraph
prove that telegraph operators preferred short words over long ones
questions.explanation: The passage explains that telegraph operators used OK because it was quick to send, which helped the word spread widely during the 1840s.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that OK survived when other 1830s slang words disappeared because
options: newspapers continued to print it regularly throughout the decade
President Van Buren made it illegal to use other abbreviated words
it gained political popularity and then proved useful for new technology
Professor Allen Walker Read promoted it through his research articles
questions.explanation: The passage shows OK gained momentum through Van Buren's campaign, then spread further when telegraph operators found it useful—two factors that kept it alive.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "conclusively" most nearly means
options: in a brief manner
with some uncertainty
in an interesting way
in a way that settles a question
questions.explanation: The passage indicates that Read's research "ended the debate," meaning he settled the question definitively about OK's origins.

questions.stem: Which statement best summarizes the relationship between OK's origins and its current status?
options: A word created as a joke without serious purpose became one of humanity's most universal expressions.
A word invented for telegraph communication eventually spread to over 600 languages worldwide.
A word popularized by a presidential campaign was later adopted by NASA astronauts.
A word whose origins were mysterious was finally explained by Professor Allen Walker Read.
questions.explanation: The passage emphasizes the contrast between OK's absurd beginning (a newspaper joke) and its remarkable outcome (global recognition), connecting the opening and closing ideas.

articles.topic_title: The Treaty of Tordesillas: how two nations drew a line through the world
articles.title: Two Kingdoms Divide the Entire World
articles.passage_html: Picture a world map. Now imagine two European kings drawing a single line straight down the Atlantic Ocean, claiming that everything on one side belonged to Spain and everything on the other to Portugal. On June 7, 1494, this audacious scene became reality when these two kingdoms signed the Treaty of Tordesillas in a small Spanish town. The Treaty of Tordesillas epitomized the breathtaking confidence of fifteenth-century Europe. Spain and Portugal agreed to divide all future discoveries between themselves, placing an imaginary boundary 370 leagues—roughly 1,100 miles—west of the Cape Verde Islands off Africa's coast. King Ferdinand and Queen Isabella of Spain negotiated with King John II of Portugal to shift this line farther west than Pope Alexander VI had originally proposed in 1493. Portugal had repudiated the Pope's first attempt because it positioned the line too close to African territories Portugal already controlled. This treaty succeeded in preventing war between two powerful rivals, which represents its greatest achievement. By establishing clear territorial boundaries, Spain and Portugal avoided costly conflicts that could have devastated both kingdoms. When Portuguese explorer Pedro Álvares Cabral landed in Brazil in 1500, Portugal could claim this vast territory without Spanish interference because Brazil fell east of the treaty line. Meanwhile, Spain gained rights to explore and colonize an enormous expanse stretching from present-day California to the southern tip of South America. The treaty provided stability and predictability for three centuries. Furthermore, the agreement demonstrated how diplomacy could resolve international disputes. Rather than sending armies to fight over unknown lands, these monarchs chose negotiation. They extended their agreement in 1529 with the Treaty of Zaragoza, drawing another line through the Eastern Hemisphere to settle arguments over the valuable Spice Islands. Yet the treaty's presumption—that two nations could carve up the entire globe—revealed Europe's disregard for people already inhabiting these lands. England, France, and the Netherlands recognized this arrogance and simply ignored the treaty, establishing their own American colonies. The line through the ocean ultimately proved as imaginary as it always was, unable to contain the ambitions of other nations or acknowledge the rights of millions already living on these so-called "newly discovered" continents.

Picture a world map. Now imagine two European kings drawing a single line straight down the Atlantic Ocean, claiming that everything on one side belonged to Spain and everything on the other to Portugal. On June 7, 1494, this audacious scene became reality when these two kingdoms signed the Treaty of Tordesillas in a small Spanish town.

The Treaty of Tordesillas <vocab>epitomized|perfectly represented</vocab> the breathtaking confidence of fifteenth-century Europe. Spain and Portugal agreed to divide all future discoveries between themselves, placing an imaginary boundary 370 leagues—roughly 1,100 miles—west of the Cape Verde Islands off Africa's coast. King Ferdinand and Queen Isabella of Spain negotiated with King John II of Portugal to shift this line farther west than Pope Alexander VI had originally proposed in 1493. This treaty succeeded in preventing war between two powerful rivals, which represents its greatest achievement. By establishing clear territorial boundaries, Spain and Portugal avoided costly conflicts that could have <vocab>devastated|destroyed or ruined</vocab> both kingdoms. When Portuguese explorer Pedro Álvares Cabral landed in Brazil in 1500, Portugal could claim this vast territory without Spanish interference because Brazil fell east of the treaty line. Meanwhile, Spain gained rights to explore and colonize an enormous <vocab>expanse|wide area</vocab> stretching from present-day California to the southern tip of South America. The treaty provided stability and predictability for three centuries.

Furthermore, the agreement demonstrated how <vocab>diplomacy|peaceful negotiation between nations</vocab> could resolve international disputes. Rather than sending armies to fight over unknown lands, these monarchs chose negotiation. They extended their agreement in 1529 with the Treaty of Zaragoza, drawing another line through the Eastern Hemisphere to settle arguments over the valuable Spice Islands.

Yet the treaty's <vocab>presumption|assumption without evidence</vocab>—that two nations could carve up the entire globe—revealed Europe's disregard for people already inhabiting these lands. England, France, and the Netherlands recognized this arrogance and simply ignored the treaty, establishing their own American colonies. The line through the ocean ultimately proved as imaginary as it always was, unable to contain the ambitions of other nations or acknowledge the rights of millions already living on these so-called "newly discovered" continents.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: The Treaty of Tordesillas successfully prevented Spanish-Portuguese conflict but revealed European arrogance by dividing lands without regard for existing populations.
Spain and Portugal signed an important treaty in 1494 that gave Portugal control of Brazil and Spain control of most of the Americas.
The Treaty of Tordesillas was the most significant diplomatic achievement of the fifteenth century and established lasting peace in Europe.
European kingdoms competed fiercely for control of newly discovered territories, leading to centuries of warfare across the Atlantic.
questions.explanation: The passage argues both that the treaty achieved its diplomatic goal (preventing war) and that it demonstrated problematic presumption (dividing inhabited lands). Option B is a detail but misses the argumentative structure. Option C overstates the treaty's significance. Option D contradicts the passage, which emphasizes the treaty prevented war between Spain and Portugal.

questions.stem: Which detail from the passage BEST supports the idea that Portugal benefited from negotiating changes to the Pope's original proposal?
options: The Pope issued his first division in 1493, showing his support for Spanish exploration.
Portugal gained the right to claim Brazil when Cabral landed there in 1500 because it fell east of the treaty line.
King John II worked together with King Ferdinand and Queen Isabella to create the final agreement.
The treaty placed the dividing line 370 leagues west of the Cape Verde Islands off the coast of Africa.
questions.explanation: This detail directly demonstrates Portugal's concrete benefit from moving the line westward—gaining Brazil. Option A is true but doesn't show Portugal's benefit from changing the line. Option C is partially true but doesn't demonstrate the benefit. Option D states where the line was placed but doesn't explain why this helped Portugal.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that England, France, and the Netherlands viewed the Treaty of Tordesillas as
options: a diplomatic model they should follow when creating their own treaties with Spain and Portugal.
an unfair agreement that violated the religious authority of Pope Alexander VI.
an arrangement between two nations that had no authority over their own colonial ambitions.
a reasonable division of the world that unfortunately needed to be revised for the Eastern Hemisphere.
questions.explanation: The passage states these nations 'simply ignored the treaty' and established their own colonies, suggesting they rejected Spain and Portugal's authority to divide the world. Option A contradicts their ignoring it. Option B invents a religious objection not mentioned. Option D misrepresents their response—they didn't accept it as reasonable.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "expanse" most nearly means
options: opportunity
distance
stretch of land
expensive territory
questions.explanation: The passage uses 'expanse' to describe a vast area 'stretching from present-day California to the southern tip of South America,' making 'stretch of land' the best match. Option A changes the meaning entirely. Option B focuses only on measurement, not territory. Option D confuses 'expanse' with 'expensive.'

questions.stem: Which best describes how the passage is organized?
options: The author states a claim about the treaty's significance, then supports it with two main reasons before acknowledging its limitations.
The author compares the Treaty of Tordesillas with other European treaties to show which was most effective.
The author presents the treaty in chronological order, from the Pope's 1493 proposal through the 1529 extension.
The author describes a problem facing European kingdoms, then explains how the treaty permanently solved this problem.
questions.explanation: The passage follows an argumentative structure: claiming the treaty succeeded, supporting this with two reasons (preventing war, demonstrating diplomacy), then examining its problematic presumption. Option B invents comparisons not present. Option C describes some content but misses the argumentative organization. Option D contradicts the passage, which shows the treaty didn't permanently solve territorial disputes.

articles.topic_title: Mae Jemison: first Black woman in space
articles.title: Mae Jemison Proved Excellence Knows No Boundaries
articles.passage_html: On September 12, 1992, Dr. Mae Jemison fastened her safety harness inside the Space Shuttle Endeavour and felt the engines roar beneath her. As the spacecraft pierced through Earth's atmosphere, she became the first African American woman to travel beyond our planet's boundaries. Her journey represents why determination paired with education creates unstoppable achievers who inspire future generations. Jemison's academic achievements demonstrate that rigorous preparation opens doors once thought locked. After graduating from Stanford University in 1977 with degrees in both chemical engineering and African American studies, she pursued medicine at Cornell University, earning her medical degree in 1981. This dual focus on science and cultural understanding shaped her unique perspective. Before NASA selected her from approximately 2,000 applicants in June 1987, Jemison served as a Peace Corps medical officer in Sierra Leone and Liberia from 1983 to 1985. These experiences taught her to solve complex problems with limited resources—exactly the skills astronauts need when equipment malfunctions 200 miles above Earth. Her space mission produced tangible scientific progress that advanced human knowledge. During the eight-day STS-47 mission, Jemison and her six crewmates circumnavigated Earth 127 times while conducting 44 scientific experiments. These weren't simple observations; the crew tested how weightlessness affects human cells, studied how materials behave in space, and examined ways to improve life both in orbit and on the ground. Each experiment required meticulous attention because mistakes in space can have serious consequences. The data collected during those 127 orbits helped scientists worldwide understand everything from bone density loss to semiconductor manufacturing. Perhaps most importantly, Jemison refused to let her accomplishments end with a single mission. After leaving NASA in March 1993, she founded the Jemison Group, a technology consulting company that same year. In 1994, she established The Earth We Share, an international science camp where students aged 12 to 16 explore scientific concepts through hands-on projects. Rather than simply bask in past glory, Jemison actively works to ensure that young people from all backgrounds see themselves as viable scientists and engineers. When Mae Jemison gazed through Endeavour's windows at our blue planet spinning below, she carried the dreams of countless people who had been told space wasn't for them. Her journey proves that excellence requires no permission—only preparation, courage, and the conviction that knowledge belongs to everyone willing to reach for it.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: Mae Jemison's education and determination, combined with her ongoing efforts to inspire others, demonstrate how one person can break barriers and create lasting change.
Mae Jemison was the first African American woman to travel to space aboard the Space Shuttle Endeavour in 1992.
Scientists who conduct experiments in space must have both medical training and engineering backgrounds to succeed.
The Space Shuttle Endeavour mission was important because it circled Earth 127 times and conducted 44 experiments.
questions.explanation: The passage argues that Jemison's achievements show how determination and education create achievers who inspire others, supporting this with her academic preparation, scientific contributions, and post-NASA educational work.

questions.stem: The author mentions Jemison's Peace Corps service in order to...
options: explain why NASA rejected her first application to the astronaut program
illustrate how her experience solving problems with limited resources prepared her for space challenges
describe the medical experiments she later conducted during her space mission
show that she preferred working in Africa to working in the United States

questions.stem: The passage suggests that Jemison's decision to establish The Earth We Share demonstrates her belief that...
options: space travel should only be available to people with medical degrees
international cooperation is more important than individual achievement
young people need hands-on experience to see themselves as future scientists
science camps are more effective than traditional classroom education
questions.explanation: The passage states Jemison works to ensure young people from all backgrounds 'see themselves as viable scientists and engineers,' and describes The Earth We Share as providing hands-on exploration for students.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word 'conviction' most nearly means...
options: a criminal sentence
a formal accusation
confusion about a topic
firm belief or certainty
questions.explanation: The passage uses 'conviction' to describe the 'strong belief or determination' that knowledge belongs to everyone, which matches the meaning of firm belief or certainty.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward Mae Jemison can best be described as...
options: admiring
skeptical
neutral
cautionary
questions.explanation: The author presents Jemison's accomplishments with clear respect and approval, using phrases like 'unstoppable achievers,' emphasizing her scientific contributions, and highlighting her ongoing inspirational work.

articles.topic_title: The 1854 cholera map that invented epidemiology
articles.title: The Map That Changed Medicine Forever
articles.passage_html: On September 7, 1854, Dr. John Snow persuaded London officials to remove the handle from a neighborhood water pump. That simple action helped end a deadly outbreak that had already claimed more than 500 lives in just ten days. The breakthrough came not from a microscope or medicine, but from a map that would revolutionize how scientists understand disease. Cholera had ravaged London's Soho neighborhood throughout August and September. Victims suffered terribly, and doctors felt helpless. Most physicians believed the disease spread through "miasma"—bad air rising from rotting garbage and sewage. Dr. Snow, however, suspected a different culprit: contaminated water. Snow created an unusual map of the Broad Street area. He marked each cholera death with a black bar at the victim's home address. The visual pattern that emerged proved striking. Nearly all the deaths clustered around one location: the public water pump on Broad Street at the corner of Cambridge Street. The evidence seemed clear, but Snow needed more proof. He discovered a fascinating case involving Susannah Eley, a woman who lived far from Broad Street yet died from cholera. Through careful investigation, Snow learned she had water from the Broad Street pump delivered to her home because she preferred its taste. This peculiar detail strengthened his theory. Later examination revealed the source of contamination: a leaking cesspool just three feet from the pump had allowed sewage from a cholera victim to seep into the well water. Snow's map provided compelling evidence that cholera spread through water, not air. His work challenged the prevailing medical beliefs of his time. Today, epidemiologists—scientists who study how diseases spread through populations—recognize Snow's 1854 cholera map as a founding document of their field. By transforming invisible patterns of illness into visible proof, Snow demonstrated that careful observation and clear presentation of data could solve mysteries that had puzzled doctors for generations. His map didn't just stop one outbreak; it changed how we fight disease forever.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: Dr. John Snow's 1854 map of cholera deaths proved that contaminated water spread the disease and established a new way to study epidemics.
Cholera was a terrible disease in London during the 1850s that killed more than 500 people in the Soho neighborhood.
Most doctors in the 1850s incorrectly believed that diseases spread through bad air rather than through contaminated water.
Creating maps of disease outbreaks is an important tool that modern epidemiologists use to track how illnesses spread.
questions.explanation: The passage focuses on how Snow's map both proved his water contamination theory and founded the field of epidemiology, making option A correct. Options B and C are details that support the main idea, while D is true but focuses only on modern practices rather than Snow's historical contribution.

questions.stem: The author mentions Susannah Eley in order to
options: explain why the Broad Street pump water tasted better than water from other pumps in London
demonstrate that cholera could affect people who lived in wealthy neighborhoods far from Soho
provide evidence that cholera spread through water even to someone who lived far from the outbreak area
show that most London residents preferred to have water delivered to their homes rather than collect it themselves
questions.explanation: The passage states that Eley's case 'strengthened his theory' because she died from cholera after having Broad Street pump water delivered to her distant home. Option A focuses on taste, which is mentioned but not the reason for including her story. Option B makes an assumption about wealth not stated in the passage. Option D generalizes beyond what the passage claims.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that before Snow's work, doctors were unable to stop cholera outbreaks because they
options: did not have access to modern microscopes that could identify cholera bacteria in water samples
lacked effective medicines and treatments that could cure patients who had already contracted the disease
were trying to eliminate bad air instead of identifying and removing the actual source of contamination
refused to believe that poor sanitation and leaking cesspools could cause any serious health problems
questions.explanation: Since doctors believed cholera spread through 'miasma' (bad air), they would have focused on the wrong cause. Snow's water theory led to removing the pump handle, which helped end the outbreak. Option A is historically true but not suggested by this passage. Option B addresses treatment rather than prevention. Option D overstates doctors' attitudes toward sanitation.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "compelling" most nearly means
options: forcing someone to do something against their will
extremely interesting or exciting to watch
powerfully persuasive and convincing
requiring immediate attention or action
questions.explanation: The passage describes Snow's map as providing 'compelling evidence' that convinced others of his water contamination theory. In this context, compelling means convincing or persuasive, making option C correct.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward Dr. John Snow's work can best be described as
options: admiring
skeptical
neutral
cautionary
questions.explanation: The author uses positive language throughout, describing Snow's map as a 'breakthrough' that 'revolutionized' disease study and 'changed how we fight disease forever.' This admiring tone is consistent throughout the passage.

articles.topic_title: Zhang Heng: the first seismograph in 132 AD
articles.title: Why Zhang Heng's Ancient Invention Still Matters
articles.passage_html: A bronze ball dropped from a dragon's mouth, clattering into the waiting jaws of a metal toad below. The sound echoed through the palace in Luoyang, capital of the Eastern Han Dynasty. Officials rushed to examine the device—a massive bronze vessel six feet across, adorned with eight dragons and eight toads. One dragon had released its ball, pointing west. But no one in the city had felt any tremors. Had the machine malfunctioned? Days later, a messenger arrived with startling news: an earthquake had struck Longxi, roughly 400 miles to the west, on the exact day the dragon released its ball. The year was 138 AD, and Zhang Heng's seismoscope had just proven its worth. This remarkable achievement demonstrates that Zhang Heng deserves recognition as one of history's greatest scientific minds. His invention, combined with his other contributions to science, established principles of observation and measurement that were centuries ahead of their time. First, Zhang Heng's seismoscope represented an unprecedented leap in earthquake detection. Created in 132 AD and called the "Houfeng Didong Yi"—meaning "instrument for measuring the seasonal winds and the movements of the Earth"—the device used a sophisticated internal pendulum mechanism to detect distant earthquakes. When seismic waves reached the bronze vessel, the pendulum would swing and trigger one of eight dragons to release its ball, revealing the earthquake's direction. This wasn't merely a curiosity. In ancient China, where emperors needed to send aid quickly to afflicted regions, knowing an earthquake's location without waiting for messengers could save countless lives. The 138 AD detection proved the instrument's reliability, marking the first time humans could scientifically monitor earthquakes beyond what they could physically feel. Second, Zhang Heng's broader scientific accomplishments reveal a mind committed to understanding the natural world through precise measurement. Born in 78 AD in Nanyang, he served Emperor Shun as chief astronomer and court mathematician. He constructed an improved armillary sphere and a water-powered rotating celestial globe. His star catalog identified 2,500 stars and named 124 constellations, dramatically expanding astronomical knowledge. He even calculated pi as approximately 3.1622—impressively close to the actual value. These achievements weren't isolated experiments; they formed a coherent approach to studying the universe through careful observation and mathematical analysis. Zhang Heng's legacy extends beyond any single invention. He demonstrated that natural phenomena follow patterns we can measure, predict, and understand—a principle that remains the foundation of modern science.

The seismoscope was the most important invention in ancient China because it could detect earthquakes from hundreds of miles away.

articles.topic_title: The Dragon Boat Festival: a poet's sacrifice that became a tradition
articles.title: A Poet's Sacrifice Launches a Festival
articles.passage_html: On the fifth day of the fifth lunar month in 278 BCE, poet and minister Qu Yuan waded into the cold waters of the Miluo River in present-day Hunan Province and never emerged. His beloved state of Chu had just fallen to the conquering Qin army, and Qu Yuan chose death over witnessing his homeland's destruction. Qu Yuan had served as a trusted minister during China's Warring States period, a turbulent era spanning from approximately 340 BCE to 278 BCE. When his political enemies convinced the king to banish him, Qu Yuan spent years wandering the countryside, composing poetry that expressed his anguish over his country's fate. After Chu's defeat, the loyal poet decided he could no longer bear the sorrow. Local fishermen, hearing of Qu Yuan's drowning, immediately propelled their boats across the river, desperately searching for his body. The rhythmic splashing of their oars echoed across the water as they raced to reach him. To protect the poet's remains from hungry fish, the villagers threw rice dumplings into the river—an act of respect that would transform into a lasting tradition. Today, the Dragon Boat Festival, also called Duanwu Festival, honors Qu Yuan's memory each year on the fifth day of the fifth month of the Chinese lunar calendar, typically falling in June. Modern dragon boat racing crews replicate those ancient fishermen's urgent search, with 20 paddlers, one drummer, and one steerer working in perfect coordination. The boats themselves—ranging from 40 to 100 feet in length—feature carved dragon heads at the bow and dragon tails at the stern, creating a spectacle of color and movement on the water. The festival's traditional food, zongzi, directly connects to that fateful day. These pyramid-shaped dumplings contain glutinous rice wrapped in bamboo or reed leaves and tied with string. What began as an improvised offering to honor one man has spread across the globe. Since the International Dragon Boat Federation's establishment in 1991, competitions now take place in more than 90 countries worldwide. A minister's tragic choice became a celebration that unites communities across centuries and continents, proving that sometimes the most enduring traditions spring from moments of profound loss.

articles.topic_title: Why polar bears have black skin under white fur
articles.title: The Hidden Secret Beneath Polar Bear Fur
articles.passage_html: A polar bear emerges from an icy Arctic plunge, water streaming from its coat. To an observer, the bear appears perfectly white against the frozen landscape. Yet beneath that snowy exterior lies a surprising secret: jet-black skin. Polar bears' black skin serves as nature's most effective heating system in one of Earth's harshest environments. This dark coloration exists because of melanin, the same pigment that colors human skin. While the bear's fur appears white, each individual hair shaft is actually hollow and transparent, containing no pigment whatsoever. These clear hairs work like tiny prisms, scattering and reflecting visible light the same way snow crystals do. The result? Fur that looks white while hiding dark skin underneath. The black skin provides a crucial survival advantage through heat absorption. When sunlight penetrates the transparent fur, the black skin absorbs those rays, warming the bear in temperatures that plummet to minus 40 degrees Fahrenheit. Scientists can observe this dark skin most clearly on polar bears' noses, lips, and footpads, which remain exposed and hairless. This absorption system transforms the bear into a walking solar panel, capturing precious warmth in an environment where every degree matters. The fur itself contributes another layer of protection through remarkable insulation. Because the hairs are hollow, they trap air between the skin and the outside world. Researchers discovered that this creates an R-value similar to double-pane windows—the same technology humans use to keep homes warm. Each square inch of skin contains approximately 9,700 hairs, forming a dense barrier against Arctic winds. The combination of heat-absorbing black skin and heat-trapping hollow fur creates a two-part temperature regulation system. This sophisticated adaptation didn't appear overnight. Polar bears evolved from brown bears approximately 600,000 years ago, gradually developing these specialized features as they moved into Arctic regions. The black skin and transparent fur represent evolutionary solutions to extreme cold, allowing these bears to thrive where few other large mammals can survive. The next time you see a photograph of a polar bear, remember: what appears simple on the surface often conceals remarkable complexity beneath. That white coat and black skin combination represents millions of years of adaptation, proving that survival in Earth's frozen frontier demands more than meets the eye.

articles.topic_title: The Kenyan teen who invented a cow predator alarm
articles.title: How Flashing Lights Saved Lions and Livestock
articles.passage_html: Nine cows dead. Richard Turere counted each loss, watching his family's herd shrink as lions from Nairobi National Park crept through the darkness. For an eleven-year-old Maasai boy in Kitengela, Kenya, these attacks meant more than financial hardship—they threatened his community's way of life. Richard knew the traditional response: Maasai herders killed lions to protect their animals. Yet he believed a better solution existed. Richard's ingenious invention deserves recognition as one of the most effective wildlife conservation tools created by a young person. His Lion Lights system demonstrates that simple technology, applied thoughtfully, can resolve conflicts between humans and endangered animals. Two key reasons prove why this invention matters: it protects both livestock and lions, and it shows how local knowledge combined with basic science creates lasting change. First, Richard's system addresses both sides of a dangerous problem. Before 2012, his family lost livestock regularly to lion attacks. Frustrated herders throughout the region killed lions in retaliation, pushing these magnificent predators closer to extinction. Richard observed that lions feared moving lights, mistaking them for human patrols. He connected flashing LED bulbs to a solar panel and an old car battery, creating the illusion of someone walking around the livestock enclosure with a flashlight. After installation, his family experienced zero lion attacks. By protecting cows without harming lions, Richard transformed a cycle of violence into peaceful coexistence. Second, Richard's approach proves that young people from affected communities can generate practical solutions to environmental challenges. He didn't study wildlife biology at a university or work for an international conservation organization. Instead, he applied his understanding of both lions and available materials. His success inspired rapid expansion of the technology—by 2013, Lion Lights protected livestock at seven homesteads around Nairobi National Park. This local innovation accomplished what outside experts had struggled to achieve. Richard's journey extended far beyond Kenya's borders. In February 2013, he presented his invention at a TED conference in Long Beach, California, becoming one of the youngest speakers in the program's history. He earned a scholarship to Brookhouse International School, one of Kenya's most prestigious educational institutions. Yet perhaps his greatest achievement remains those flashing lights, still protecting animals on both sides of the fence—proof that brilliant ideas can emerge from anywhere, solving problems that affect the entire planet.

The Lion Lights system taught lions to avoid all human settlements permanently, solving the conflict throughout Kenya.
Richard's TED talk convinced international organizations to create new laws protecting lions in Nairobi National Park.
questions.explanation: The passage connects multiple facts: herders traditionally killed lions in retaliation for attacks, but Richard's system stopped attacks on livestock, which reduced lion killings—this cause-and-effect relationship aided conservation.

articles.topic_title: Sacagawea: the teen guide of Lewis and Clark
articles.title: The Teenage Guide Who Crossed a Continent
articles.passage_html: Snow crunched beneath moccasins as a sixteen-year-old girl adjusted the cradleboard strapped to her back, her infant son sleeping against the January cold. Sacagawea stood outside Fort Mandan in present-day North Dakota, preparing to join thirty-three men on a journey that would stretch 8,000 miles across an unknown wilderness. Born around 1788 in the Lemhi River Valley of Idaho, she belonged to the Agaidika tribe of the Shoshone people. The mountains, rivers, and forests of her homeland had shaped her childhood until a Hidatsa raiding party captured her at age twelve in 1800, dragging her hundreds of miles east to their villages near modern Bismarck, North Dakota. Now, in November 1805, she joined the Lewis and Clark Expedition alongside her French-Canadian husband, Toussaint Charbonneau, who had been hired as an interpreter. Just months earlier, on February 11, 1805, she had given birth to her son Jean Baptiste Charbonneau. Throughout the arduous trek ahead, she would carry him on her back across prairies where buffalo herds thundered past, through mountain passes where rocks scraped her hands raw, and along rivers whose rapids roared like angry beasts. The expedition's most crucial moment came in August 1805, when the team reached Shoshone territory in the Rocky Mountains. Sacagawea recognized the landscape—these were her people. When she met with the Shoshone chief to negotiate for horses the expedition desperately needed, she discovered something astonishing: the chief, Cameahwait, was her own brother. This remarkable reunion helped secure the horses that allowed the expedition to cross the treacherous mountain terrain. On November 24, 1805, as Pacific Ocean waves crashed against distant shores, the expedition members voted on where to build their winter fort. Sacagawea cast her vote alongside the men—an exceptional act that made her one of the first women to vote in American history. She had traveled from captivity to becoming an essential member of one of America's greatest explorations, proving that courage and knowledge recognize no age or gender.

The expedition's most <vocab>crucial|extremely important</vocab> moment came in August 1805, when the team reached Shoshone territory in the Rocky Mountains.
