When she met with the Shoshone chief to <vocab>negotiate|discuss to reach an agreement</vocab> for horses the expedition desperately needed, she discovered something <vocab>astonishing|extremely surprising</vocab>: the chief, Cameahwait, was her own brother. This <vocab>remarkable|extraordinary and worthy of attention</vocab> reunion helped secure the horses that allowed the expedition to cross the treacherous mountain terrain.

On November 24, 1805, as Pacific Ocean waves crashed against distant shores, the expedition members voted on where to build their winter fort. Sacagawea cast her vote alongside the men—an <vocab>exceptional|very unusual or rare</vocab> act that made her one of the first women to vote in American history. She had traveled from captivity to becoming an essential member of one of America's greatest explorations, proving that courage and knowledge recognize no age or gender.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?

The Lewis and Clark Expedition covered 8,000 miles and required help from Native American tribes to cross the Rocky Mountains.
Sacagawea was captured as a child and later reunited with her brother during an important expedition to the Pacific Ocean.
Young women in early American history faced many challenges but sometimes gained opportunities to participate in important events.
questions.explanation: The passage focuses on Sacagawea's journey from captivity to becoming an essential expedition member, emphasizing her contributions despite her youth. Options B and C are too narrow, focusing on single aspects, while D is too general and not specifically about Sacagawea.

questions.stem: The author mentions Sacagawea's reunion with her brother in order to...
options: explain why the Shoshone people decided to help all explorers traveling through their territory
demonstrate how her personal connections helped the expedition succeed at a critical moment
show that family members who are separated in childhood can eventually find each other again
illustrate the common practice of Native American chiefs meeting with expedition members

Option A adds information not in the text, C focuses on the reunion itself rather than its importance to the expedition, and D generalizes beyond what the passage states.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that Sacagawea's childhood experiences...
options: taught her to speak multiple languages that helped her communicate with all tribes the expedition encountered
made her fearful of traveling long distances away from established settlements
provided her with knowledge and skills that proved valuable during the expedition
were similar to those of most young women living in North Dakota during that time period
questions.explanation: The passage describes her Shoshone upbringing where 'mountains, rivers, and forests...shaped her childhood' and later shows her recognizing the landscape and negotiating with her people, implying her early experiences gave her useful knowledge. Option A overstates what's mentioned, B contradicts her actions, and D makes an unsupported generalization.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "negotiate" most nearly means...

to exchange goods in a marketplace
to surrender in a conflict
to discuss terms of an agreement
questions.explanation: The passage describes negotiating 'for horses the expedition desperately needed,' indicating a discussion to reach an agreement for obtaining horses. While negotiate can relate to traversing terrain (A), the context here is clearly about the transaction with her brother's tribe.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward Sacagawea can best be described as...
options: admiring
neutral
skeptical
cautionary
questions.explanation: The author uses phrases like 'proving that courage and knowledge recognize no age or gender' and emphasizes her remarkable achievements despite difficult circumstances, showing admiration for Sacagawea's contributions. The passage is not neutral (B), doubtful (C), or warning-oriented (D).

articles.topic_title: The 18-year-old who discovered Lascaux cave (1940)
articles.title: A Dog, a Hole, and Ancient Masterpieces
articles.passage_html: Marcel Ravidat's dog Robot vanished down a hole on September 12, 1940, near Montignac in southwestern France. The 18-year-old mechanic's apprentice peered into the darkness, wondering where his companion had gone. What he discovered would soon captivate the world. Ravidat returned that same day with three teenagers: Jacques Marsal, age 15, Georges Agnel, age 16, and Simon Coencas, also 15. The boys excavated earth to widen the opening, then descended approximately 15 meters underground. Their homemade lamp flickered across the walls, revealing something extraordinary. Ancient artists had transformed the cave chambers into a prehistoric gallery. The walls displayed more than 600 painted animals and nearly 1,400 engravings, created approximately 17,000 years ago during the Upper Paleolithic period. The artisans who painted these images used natural pigments: ochre for reds and yellows, iron oxide for browns, and charcoal for blacks. They depicted 364 horses and 90 stags among other creatures, their forms racing across the stone surfaces. The animals appeared so animated that viewers could almost hear hoofbeats echoing through the chambers. Four days after their discovery, on September 16, the boys reported their find to Léon Laval, their former schoolteacher. Word spread quickly. By late September, French archaeologist Abbé Henri Breuil arrived to examine the paintings. He confirmed their authenticity—these were truly prehistoric masterpieces. The discovery transformed understanding of early human artistic abilities. Lascaux cave opened to the public in July 1948, welcoming approximately 1,200 visitors daily. However, this popularity created problems. Human breath released carbon dioxide and humidity that damaged the ancient paintings. In 1963, authorities closed the cave to protect it. Today, Robot's curiosity and four teenagers' courage to explore underground remain connected to one of archaeology's greatest treasures—a 17,000-year-old testament to human creativity that nearly stayed hidden forever.

questions.stem: Which would be the BEST title for this passage?

The History of Cave Paintings in France
Why Ancient Artists Created Underground Masterpieces

articles.topic_title: Mantis shrimp eyes: colors humans cannot imagine
articles.title: The Rainbow World of Mantis Shrimp Eyes
articles.passage_html: Marine biologist Roy Caldwell peered into the aquarium tank at the University of California, watching a peacock mantis shrimp named Tyson scan the water. Tyson's eyes—each the size of a match head—swiveled independently, one tracking a passing fish while the other examined a shell. Caldwell noticed something odd: the eyes moved constantly, up and down, side to side, as if gathering information humans could never comprehend. Researchers discovered that those tiny eyes held an extraordinary secret. Unlike humans, who possess three types of color-detecting cells called photoreceptor cells, mantis shrimp eyes contain sixteen different types. Each eye also has three separate regions called pseudopupils, allowing the creature to perceive depth using just one eye—a trick no human could perform. The eyes detect ultraviolet light invisible to us, along with visible colors and polarized light across a spectrum extending far beyond our range. Scientists estimate that mantis shrimp can distinguish twelve different color channels, including ultraviolet wavelengths between 300 and 400 nanometers. The turning point came in 2014 when scientists made a stunning discovery. Mantis shrimp eyes could detect circular polarized light, a feat no other animal on Earth is known to achieve. This revelation opened new possibilities. Researchers at the University of Queensland found that the species Odontodactylus scyllarus could distinguish between cancer cells and healthy cells based on how they reflected polarized light. Medical teams began studying these eyes for potential imaging applications that might help doctors detect disease earlier. Each compound eye contains approximately 10,000 tiny structures called ommatidia, packed into intricate rows. These specialized cells work together, processing light in ways scientists are still trying to understand. The eyes move independently, each covering a 70-degree range of vision, creating an immense field of view. Imagine perceiving colors that have no names because no human language needs words for them. The mantis shrimp experiences reality through a visual system so different from ours that we can barely grasp its edges. These tiny ocean hunters remind us that the world contains wonders our own eyes will never witness—entire dimensions of light and color existing just beyond our reach.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: Mantis shrimp possess an extraordinary visual system far more complex than human vision, with abilities that scientists are studying for medical applications.
Marine biologist Roy Caldwell studied mantis shrimp at the University of California and discovered they have eyes that move independently.
Mantis shrimp can see more colors than humans because they live in ocean environments where detecting many colors helps them survive.
Scientists made an important discovery in 2014 about how animals see polarized light in different ways.
questions.explanation: The passage focuses on the remarkable complexity of mantis shrimp vision compared to humans and its scientific applications. Option B mentions only one detail from the opening. Option C adds information about survival not in the passage (Type A distractor). Option D is too narrow and vague (Type B distractor).

questions.stem: The author mentions pseudopupils in order to...
options: explain how mantis shrimp detect ultraviolet light wavelengths between 300 and 400 nanometers.
demonstrate that mantis shrimp eyes can perform a function that human eyes cannot.
describe the approximately 10,000 ommatidia structures packed into each mantis shrimp eye.
show how mantis shrimp move their eyes independently to cover a 70-degree range of vision.
questions.explanation: The passage states pseudopupils allow depth perception with one eye, 'a trick no human could perform.' Options A, C, and D mention other true facts from the passage but misattribute them to pseudopupils (Type C distractors using passage words incorrectly).

questions.stem: The passage suggests that mantis shrimp vision...
options: helps mantis shrimp survive better than most ocean predators because they can see prey from farther away.
allows mantis shrimp to communicate with each other using color patterns humans cannot detect.
processes visual information in ways that scientists have not yet fully understood.
evolved over millions of years as ocean environments became more competitive and dangerous.
questions.explanation: The passage states scientists are 'still trying to understand' how the eyes work. Options A, B, and D describe plausible evolutionary or biological facts but are not mentioned in the passage (Type A distractors—true in real world but not stated).

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "intricate" most nearly means...
options: beautiful and colorful.
difficult to see clearly.
moving in different directions.
complex and detailed.
questions.explanation: The passage uses 'intricate' to describe the complex arrangement of ommatidia cells. Option A suggests aesthetic qualities not implied. Option B relates to visibility, not complexity (Type B—partially related). Option C connects to eye movement mentioned elsewhere but not the meaning here (Type C—uses passage concepts incorrectly).

questions.stem: Which best describes how the passage is organized?
options: introducing an observation, then explaining the science behind it and its applications.
describing events in chronological order from earliest discoveries to most recent findings.
comparing human vision and mantis shrimp vision to show which system is more effective.
stating a claim about animal eyes, then supporting it with examples from multiple species.
questions.explanation: The passage opens with Caldwell observing Tyson, then explains the eye structure, and discusses medical applications. Option B overstates chronological order (only 2014 is mentioned). Option C suggests comparison for effectiveness, not just difference (Type B—partially true). Option D is incorrect as only one species is discussed (Type C—uses passage elements incorrectly).

articles.topic_title: Why we shake hands: an ancient gesture of trust
articles.title: The Ancient Handshake: A Gesture of Peace
articles.passage_html: On January 1, 1907, President Theodore Roosevelt stood in the White House for hours, gripping palm after palm until his right hand ached. By evening's end, he had shaken hands with 8,510 people—a world record that still stands. His hand must have throbbed, but Roosevelt understood something important: the handshake represented American democracy itself. Yet this simple gesture traces back thousands of years before Roosevelt's marathon greeting. Deep in the ruins of an Assyrian palace at Nimrud, in modern-day Iraq, archaeologists discovered a stone carving from the 9th century BCE. It depicts King Shalmaneser III grasping hands with a Babylonian ruler—one of the oldest known images of a handshake. Even then, the gesture carried meaning. Ancient warriors had a practical reason for shaking hands. Greek and Roman soldiers extended their right hands—the sword hand—to prove they carried no weapons. The Greek poet Homer described this act of trust in The Iliad, written around 750 BCE. Warriors who could have been enemies instead clasped hands, demonstrating peaceful intentions through touch. The turning point for handshakes came centuries later, when different cultures began using the gesture to challenge rigid social rules. In 17th-century England, a religious group called the Quakers rejected bowing and hat-removing because these customs showed too much inequality between people. They chose the handshake instead—a greeting between equals. President Thomas Jefferson embraced this principle in 1801 when he replaced the formal bow with the handshake as the official White House greeting. His decision established the practice as a symbol of democratic equality in the United States, where no person should bow before another. Scientists have discovered that handshakes communicate more than respect. Research from the University of California, Berkeley revealed that humans transfer chemical signals through handshakes lasting about three seconds. Our hands literally share information with each touch. Yet handshakes carry risks too. During the 2009 H1N1 influenza pandemic, the World Health Organization recommended avoiding handshakes after studies showed hand-to-hand contact transfers 10 to 20 percent of respiratory viruses. Today, this ancient gesture balances between connection and caution. From Assyrian kings to American presidents, the handshake remains what it has always been: a deliberate choice to trust another person, even when doing so requires courage.

By evening's end, he had shaken hands with 8,510 people—a world record that still stands. Yet this simple gesture traces back thousands of years before Roosevelt's marathon greeting. It depicts King Shalmaneser III grasping hands with a Babylonian ruler—one of the oldest known images of a handshake. Even then, the gesture carried meaning.

Ancient warriors had a <vocab>practical|useful and sensible</vocab> reason for shaking hands. Greek and Roman soldiers extended their right hands—the sword hand—to prove they carried no weapons. The Greek poet Homer described this act of trust in The Iliad, written around 750 BCE. Warriors who could have been enemies instead clasped hands, <vocab>demonstrating|showing clearly</vocab> peaceful intentions through touch.

The turning point for handshakes came centuries later, when different cultures began using the gesture to challenge <vocab>rigid|strict and unchanging</vocab> social rules. In 17th-century England, a religious group called the Quakers rejected bowing and hat-removing because these customs showed too much inequality between people. They chose the handshake instead—a greeting between equals.

President Thomas Jefferson embraced this <vocab>principle|basic rule or belief</vocab> in 1801 when he replaced the formal bow with the handshake as the official White House greeting. His decision <vocab>established|set up firmly</vocab> the practice as a symbol of democratic equality in the United States, where no person should bow before another.

Yet handshakes carry risks too. During the 2009 H1N1 influenza <vocab>pandemic|disease spreading across many countries</vocab>, the World Health Organization recommended avoiding handshakes after studies showed hand-to-hand contact transfers 10 to 20 percent of respiratory viruses.

Today, this ancient gesture balances between connection and caution. From Assyrian kings to American presidents, the handshake remains what it has always been: a <vocab>deliberate|done on purpose with careful thought</vocab> choice to trust another person, even when doing so requires courage.

vocab.definition: done on purpose with careful thought

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: The handshake has served as a symbol of trust and equality across different cultures and time periods.
President Theodore Roosevelt set a world record by shaking 8,510 hands in one day.
Ancient warriors invented the handshake to show they were not carrying weapons.
The handshake became popular in America because Thomas Jefferson preferred it to bowing.
questions.explanation: The passage traces the handshake through multiple cultures and eras, emphasizing its consistent meaning as a gesture of trust and equality, making option A the best summary of the central idea.

questions.stem: The author mentions the Quakers in order to
options: explain why handshakes became popular in religious communities throughout Europe
illustrate how the handshake was adopted to challenge social inequality
describe the origins of the handshake in 17th-century England
show that bowing was considered disrespectful in most cultures
questions.explanation: The passage explains that Quakers chose handshakes because bowing showed too much inequality, illustrating how the gesture challenged rigid social hierarchies.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that ancient warriors who shook hands
options: were following orders from their military commanders
preferred this greeting to other forms of welcome
understood the symbolic value of the gesture beyond its practical purpose
had learned the practice from Assyrian and Babylonian rulers
questions.explanation: The passage describes the practical weapon-checking purpose but also notes warriors used it as 'a sign of trust and agreement,' suggesting they recognized deeper symbolic meaning.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word 'deliberate' most nearly means
options: slow and cautious
difficult to perform
intentional and thoughtful

questions.explanation: The passage uses 'deliberate' to describe a conscious choice to trust someone, meaning intentional and thoughtful rather than hasty or accidental.

questions.stem: Which statement best summarizes the relationship between the handshake's historical meaning and modern scientific understanding?
options: Both ancient and modern evidence show the handshake serves as a form of communication beyond words.
Scientific research has proven that ancient warriors were correct about the handshake preventing disease.
Modern science contradicts the historical belief that handshakes create trust between people.

questions.explanation: The passage connects ancient symbolic communication (showing trust, proving no weapons) with modern findings about chemical signal transfer, both demonstrating communication beyond verbal exchange.

articles.topic_title: Venetia Burney: the 11-year-old who named Pluto
articles.title: The Girl Who Named a Distant World
articles.passage_html: The morning sunlight slanted through the breakfast room windows as eleven-year-old Venetia Burney listened to her grandfather read the newspaper aloud. The date was March 14, 1930, and the headlines buzzed with excitement about a mysterious new planet discovered beyond Neptune. Venetia's mind drifted to her mythology lessons—stories of gods and heroes, underworlds and heavens. Then, like a spark jumping across darkness, an idea struck her. "Why not call it Pluto?" she suggested, her voice breaking the quiet clink of teacups at their Oxford breakfast table. Venetia's mother paused mid-sip. Her grandfather, Falconer Madan, a retired librarian from Oxford University's venerable Bodleian Library, set down his paper. The girl explained her reasoning: Pluto, the Roman god of the underworld, seemed fitting for a cold, dark world lurking so far from the Sun's warming rays. What Venetia couldn't have known was that her grandfather possessed connections that would carry her idea across the Atlantic Ocean. Madan knew Herbert Hall Turner, a professional astronomer, and he wasted no time sharing his granddaughter's ingenuous suggestion. Meanwhile, at Lowell Observatory in Flagstaff, Arizona—where astronomers had first spotted the celestial wanderer on February 18, 1930—scientists were inundated with naming suggestions from around the world. Names poured in like autumn leaves: Minerva, Cronus, Zeus, even Constance. But Pluto resonated. The name carried weight, mystery, and appropriateness all at once. On May 1, 1930, Lowell Observatory's official announcement echoed through scientific circles and newspaper offices: the ninth planet would be called Pluto. Venetia's grandfather rewarded her with a five-pound note—equivalent to several weeks' allowance—a tangible token of her unprecedented achievement. Venetia Burney grew up to become a teacher and accountant in Epsom, England, living a quiet life far removed from astronomical fame. Yet her childhood breakfast conversation left an indelible mark on human exploration. In 2006, when NASA launched the New Horizons spacecraft toward the distant world she'd named seventy-six years earlier, engineers honored her by naming the student dust counter instrument after her. Sometimes the most distant worlds are touched by the simplest moments—a young girl, a breakfast table, and the perfect word spoken aloud.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: An eleven-year-old girl's breakfast suggestion became the name of a planet, showing how simple moments can have lasting scientific impact.
Scientists at Lowell Observatory struggled to find an appropriate name for the newly discovered planet until they received help from England.
Venetia Burney's grandfather used his connections at Oxford University to help astronomers name the solar system's ninth planet.
The discovery of Pluto in 1930 represented one of the most important astronomical achievements of the twentieth century.
questions.explanation: The passage focuses on how Venetia's casual breakfast suggestion led to naming Pluto, emphasizing the connection between an ordinary moment and extraordinary outcome. The other options are either too narrow (focusing only on the grandfather or the observatory) or introduce ideas not central to the passage (the discovery's importance).

questions.stem: The author mentions that Venetia chose the name Pluto in order to...
options: demonstrate her extensive knowledge of Roman mythology from school.
show how her breakfast conversation connected mythology with astronomy in an appropriate way.
prove that children could contribute meaningfully to professional scientific discussions.
honor the Roman god who ruled over darkness and shadows in ancient stories.
questions.explanation: The passage explicitly describes how Venetia's mythology knowledge connected with the planet's cold, dark characteristics, making the breakfast suggestion appropriate. Option A overstates her expertise (the passage mentions 'lessons' but not 'extensive knowledge'), option C introduces a broader purpose not stated, and option D focuses only on the god rather than the connection-making process emphasized in the passage.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that Venetia's suggestion succeeded primarily because...
options: scientists preferred names from Roman mythology over Greek mythology for newly discovered planets.
her grandfather's connections allowed the idea to reach professional astronomers who found it fitting.
the Lowell Observatory had already been considering the name Pluto before receiving her suggestion.
eleven-year-old children had fresher, more creative perspectives than professional scientists could develop.
questions.explanation: The passage emphasizes both her grandfather's connections (he 'wasted no time' contacting Turner) and the name's appropriateness ('carried weight, mystery, and appropriateness'). Option A makes an unsupported generalization, option C contradicts the passage (scientists were receiving many suggestions), and option D overgeneralizes beyond what the passage implies.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "resonated" most nearly means...
options: echoed loudly
vibrated rapidly
appealed strongly
repeated frequently
questions.explanation: In context, 'resonated' describes how the name Pluto struck a chord with the scientists—it appealed to them because it 'carried weight, mystery, and appropriateness.' While 'resonated' can literally mean 'echoed' or 'vibrated,' the passage uses it figuratively to mean 'had a strong positive effect.'

questions.stem: Which best describes how the passage is organized?
options: presenting a specific moment, then explaining how that moment led to a lasting outcome
comparing Venetia's suggestion with other proposed names for the newly discovered planet
describing events in strict chronological order from the planet's discovery to the spacecraft launch
stating a problem astronomers faced, then showing how a young girl provided the solution
questions.explanation: The passage opens with the breakfast scene (specific moment), then traces how that suggestion traveled to astronomers, was selected, and ultimately honored decades later (lasting outcome). While chronology plays a role, the structure emphasizes the connection between the initial moment and its long-term significance rather than strict timeline sequencing. Options B and D don't accurately reflect the passage's emphasis.

articles.topic_title: Sybil Ludington: the 16-year-old who rode farther than Paul Revere
articles.title: The Midnight Ride History Nearly Forgot
articles.passage_html: Hoofbeats shattered the silence as Sybil Ludington galloped through the darkness, clutching a wooden stick to knock on farmhouse doors. Rain pelted her face while her horse thundered along treacherous mud-slicked paths. The sixteen-year-old had no time to rest—British soldiers were burning Danbury, Connecticut, just miles away, and her father's militia needed to assemble immediately. On the night of April 26, 1777, Sybil faced a daunting mission. Her father, Colonel Henry Ludington, commanded the 7th Regiment of the Dutchess County Militia, but his soldiers had scattered to their homes across the countryside. Someone needed to ride through Putnam County, New York, alerting every militiaman to report for duty. That someone was Sybil—the eldest daughter who knew the region's winding roads better than any messenger her father could send. The danger was real. British General William Tryon's forces had already destroyed 1,600 tents and 5,000 pairs of shoes stored in Danbury's warehouses—supplies the Continental Army desperately needed. Worse still, Sybil's route wound through areas where Loyalists lived, families who remained allegiant to the British crown. These sympathizers might capture her or alert enemy patrols. Armed with only her stick to prod her horse forward and rap against doors, Sybil pressed onward through approximately 40 miles of hostile territory. By dawn on April 27, her perseverance had paid off. Nearly 400 militia members stood assembled at the Ludington farm, ready to march. Sybil had ridden more than three times farther than Paul Revere, whose famous midnight ride two years earlier had covered just 12.5 miles before British soldiers captured him. Yet for nearly two centuries, Sybil's story remained largely unknown. Not until 1961 did sculptor Anna Hyatt Huntington create a bronze statue of Sybil on horseback in Carmel, New York. In 1975, the United States Postal Service finally issued a commemorative stamp honoring her courage as part of the Contributors to the Cause series. Sybil Ludington's forty-mile journey through rain and darkness proves that heroism doesn't always thunder loudly through history books—sometimes it gallops quietly through the night, waiting generations to be heard.

articles.topic_title: Lindbergh's solo flight across the Atlantic (1927)
articles.title: Alone Above the Atlantic: Lindbergh's Daring Flight
articles.passage_html: Charles Lindbergh yanked open the side window of his cramped cockpit, letting frigid air blast his face. For the tenth time that night, sleep threatened to overwhelm him. Flying just ten feet above the black Atlantic waves, the 25-year-old pilot fought to keep his eyes open. He had been airborne for nearly twenty hours, and dawn still seemed impossibly far away. Lindbergh's journey had begun at 7:52 a.m. on May 20, 1927, when his aircraft, the Spirit of St. Louis, lifted off from Roosevelt Field in New York. The custom-built Ryan monoplane carried 450 gallons of gasoline—so much fuel that it blocked his forward view entirely. To see ahead, Lindbergh had to peer through a periscope or crane his neck to look out the side windows. The $10,580 aircraft was attempting something no one had accomplished alone: a nonstop flight across the Atlantic Ocean to Paris, France. The turning point came somewhere over the dark water during that endless night. Lindbergh's body screamed for rest, but his mind understood the stakes. Six aviators had already died trying to win the $25,000 Orteig Prize, offered since 1919 to the first person to complete this route. He couldn't afford a single moment of sleep. So he descended closer to the waves, letting the spray and roar of wind shock him awake. As dawn broke on May 21, Lindbergh spotted the coast of Ireland. Relief surged through him—his navigation had worked. Thirty-three and a half hours after takeoff, covering approximately 3,600 miles, the Spirit of St. Louis touched down at Le Bourget Field near Paris at 10:22 p.m. An estimated 150,000 people swarmed onto the airfield, their cheers echoing across the runway as they rushed toward the solitary pilot who had conquered the Atlantic. Lindbergh's flight proved that oceans were no longer barriers but bridges. In one sleepless night above the waves, aviation—and the world—had changed forever.

articles.topic_title: Greenland shark: the vertebrate that lives 400 years
articles.title: The Shark That Outlives Centuries
articles.passage_html: In a research laboratory in 2016, marine biologist Julius Nielsen peered through a microscope at a tiny lens extracted from a shark's eye. The tissue sample, no larger than a peppercorn, held a secret locked inside its layers. By using radiocarbon dating on the eye lens nuclei, Nielsen and his team could determine how old this particular Greenland shark had been when fishermen accidentally caught it. The answer stunned the scientific community: 392 years old. This creature had been swimming through Arctic waters since 1624, when King James I ruled England and the Pilgrims had barely settled in America. The discovery represented a turning point in our understanding of longevity. Scientists had examined 28 female Greenland sharks in total, publishing their findings in the journal *Science*. The research revealed that these slow-moving giants can live up to 400 years, making them the longest-living vertebrate animals on Earth. Unlike salmon or sea turtles, which migrate across oceans at impressive speeds, Greenland sharks cruise through the frigid depths at just 0.76 meters per second—roughly 1.7 miles per hour. They glide through waters as cold as -1 degrees Celsius, surviving in an environment that would kill most creatures. Everything about these sharks unfolds in extreme slow motion. They grow less than one centimeter each year, taking more than a century to reach their full length of up to seven meters. Most astounding of all, they don't reach sexual maturity until approximately 150 years old—the longest maturation period of any vertebrate. A Greenland shark born today wouldn't be ready to reproduce until the year 2174. What allows them to survive so long in such hostile conditions? Their flesh contains high concentrations of a chemical called trimethylamine oxide, which acts as natural antifreeze. This compound prevents ice crystals from forming in their bodies as they swim through the North Atlantic and Arctic Oceans, sometimes descending to depths of 2,200 meters. These ancient swimmers remind us that time moves differently in the ocean's dark depths. While human civilizations rise and fall within a single Greenland shark's lifetime, these creatures continue their silent vigil, witnesses to centuries we can barely imagine.

articles.topic_title: Wood frogs: the animals that freeze solid each winter
articles.title: The Frog That Freezes Solid and Lives
articles.passage_html: The wood frog lies motionless beneath a blanket of dried leaves. Its eyes cloud over with ice. Its heart beats once, twice, then stops. No breath stirs its throat. To any observer, the small brown amphibian appears dead—yet it is merely waiting. Each autumn, as temperatures plunge across North America, wood frogs prepare for an ordeal that would kill most creatures. When October or November arrives, depending on their location, these remarkable animals enter a frozen state that lasts until spring. Scientists at the University of Alaska Fairbanks have studied this phenomenon since the 1980s, trying to understand how any vertebrate could survive such extreme conditions. The transformation happens swiftly. Within twenty-four hours of freezing weather, ice crystals begin forming throughout the frog's body. Up to seventy percent of the water in its tissues turns solid. The ice spreads between cells and around organs, but never pierces the cells themselves—a crucial distinction. Meanwhile, the frog's heart stops beating completely. Its lungs cease expanding. By any medical definition, the animal has shut down. Yet wood frogs possess a hidden defense. As temperatures drop, their bodies flood their cells with glucose—a simple sugar—at concentrations up to ten times higher than normal. This concentrated glucose acts as a natural antifreeze, preventing ice crystals from rupturing vital organs. The sugar protects what matters most while ice encases everything else. These frogs can endure temperatures as low as minus eighteen degrees Celsius for weeks. Their range stretches from the Appalachian Mountains northward to Alaska and Canada, making them the only frog species found beyond the Arctic Circle. In such harsh environments, the ability to freeze represents not a death sentence but a survival strategy. When March or April finally arrives, warmth triggers an equally dramatic reversal. Ice melts. The heart stutters back to life. Lungs inflate. Within hours, the frog hops away, ready to breed in shallow ponds. What seemed impossible—returning from a frozen, lifeless state—becomes an annual cycle. Perhaps the wood frog's greatest lesson lies not in how it freezes, but in what freezing allows. By surrendering to winter rather than fleeing it, this small amphibian claims territory its competitors cannot reach. Sometimes survival demands not resistance, but acceptance of change—even when that change turns you to ice.

questions.stem: According to the passage, which of the following is true about ice formation in wood frogs?

questions.stem: The passage suggests that wood frogs' ability to freeze solid gives them an advantage by allowing them to

options: Glucose prevents ice crystals from damaging vital organs while allowing most of the frog's body to freeze safely.

Option B contradicts the fact that the heart stops completely. Options C and D describe plausible biological processes but are not mentioned in the passage.

articles.topic_title: Marie Curie: two elements, and the price she paid
articles.title: The Glowing Discovery That Killed Marie Curie
articles.passage_html: In a drafty converted shed in Paris, Marie Curie stirred a massive iron pot filled with bubbling pitchblende ore, her arms aching from hours of labor. The air hung thick with chemical fumes that stung her nostrils. Between 1898 and 1902, she and her husband Pierre processed eight tons of this black, sticky mineral—enough to fill a small truck—just to extract one gram of a mysterious glowing substance. The shed had no proper ventilation, and in winter, Marie's hands turned purple from cold as she worked. Yet inside that gram of white powder lay a discovery that would revolutionize science: radium, the second radioactive element she identified. Marie had already discovered polonium in July 1898, naming it after her homeland Poland. Five months later, in December 1898, she and Pierre announced radium's existence. These elements possessed an inexplicable quality—they continuously emitted energy without any visible source. This property, called radioactivity, contradicted everything scientists believed about matter at the time. In 1903, Marie became the first woman to win a Nobel Prize, sharing the Physics award with Pierre and Henri Becquerel. Eight years later, she won again in Chemistry, becoming the first person to claim Nobel Prizes in two different sciences. But Marie's greatest discovery came with an invisible danger. She routinely carried test tubes of radioactive materials in her dress pockets, where they warmed her fingers with their mysterious glow. She stored samples in her desk drawer like ordinary supplies. The soft blue-green light they emitted seemed magical, not menacing. Scientists in the early 1900s didn't understand that radiation could ravage human tissue. The price of ignorance became clear decades later. Marie's laboratory notebooks from the 1890s remain so radioactive today that France's National Library stores them in lead-lined boxes. Anyone who examines them must wear protective clothing. Marie herself died on July 4, 1934, at age 66 from aplastic anemia—a blood disease caused by prolonged radiation exposure. Her relentless dedication to science had slowly poisoned her. During World War I, Marie channeled her expertise into saving lives, developing 20 mobile X-ray units she called "petites Curies" and driving them to battlefields herself. Even as radiation weakened her body, her resolve never wavered. The elements she discovered transformed medicine, industry, and physics—but they also consumed their discoverer, atom by atom.

She stored samples in her desk drawer like ordinary supplies. The soft blue-green light they emitted seemed magical, not <vocab>menacing|threatening or dangerous</vocab>. Scientists in the early 1900s didn't understand that radiation could <vocab>ravage|severely damage or destroy</vocab> human tissue.

The price of ignorance became clear decades later. Marie's laboratory notebooks from the 1890s remain so radioactive today that France's National Library stores them in lead-lined boxes. Anyone who examines them must wear protective clothing. Marie herself died on July 4, 1934, at age 66 from aplastic anemia—a blood disease caused by prolonged radiation exposure. Her <vocab>relentless|continuing without stopping</vocab> dedication to science had slowly poisoned her.

During World War I, Marie channeled her expertise into saving lives, developing 20 mobile X-ray units she called "petites Curies" and driving them to battlefields herself. Even as radiation weakened her body, her <vocab>resolve|firm determination</vocab> never wavered. The elements she discovered transformed medicine, industry, and physics—but they also consumed their discoverer, atom by atom.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: Marie Curie's groundbreaking discovery of radioactive elements brought her scientific glory but ultimately caused her death from radiation exposure.
Marie Curie became the first woman to win a Nobel Prize by discovering polonium and radium in a converted shed in Paris.
Scientists in the early 1900s did not understand that radioactive materials could be dangerous to human health.
Marie Curie worked harder than any other scientist of her time, processing eight tons of ore to extract one gram of radium.
questions.explanation: The passage describes both Curie's achievements (discovering two elements, winning Nobel Prizes) and the tragic consequence (her death from radiation exposure), making option A the central idea that captures both aspects.

questions.stem: The author mentions the lead-lined boxes in order to
options: explain how modern libraries preserve all historical scientific documents
illustrate that Curie's work remains dangerous nearly a century after her death
describe the methods scientists use to store all radioactive materials today
show that Marie Curie's notebooks were more valuable than other scientists' records
questions.explanation: The passage states Curie's notebooks are 'still so radioactive' and require lead-lined storage, emphasizing the lasting danger of her work. Option B correctly identifies this purpose, while A and C make overly broad claims not supported by the passage, and D misinterprets the meaning.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that Marie Curie's lack of protective equipment while working
options: demonstrated her careless attitude toward scientific safety procedures
was typical of other scientists who studied radioactive materials during that era
resulted from the fact that radiation's dangers were not yet known in her time
proved that she valued scientific discovery more than her own wellbeing
questions.explanation: The passage explicitly states scientists 'didn't understand that radiation exposure was dangerous' in the early 1900s, supporting option C. Options A and D make judgments about Curie's character not supported by the text, while B makes claims about other scientists not mentioned in the passage.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "ravage" most nearly means
options: completely confuse
quickly heal
slowly change
seriously harm
questions.explanation: The passage uses 'ravage' in the context of radiation damaging human tissue, and later describes how radiation caused Curie's fatal blood disease. This context indicates 'ravage' means to seriously harm or destroy, making option D correct.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward Marie Curie can best be described as
options: admiring but cautionary about the consequences of her work
critical of her failure to protect herself from radiation
neutral and focused only on presenting historical facts
skeptical about whether her discoveries were worth the sacrifice
questions.explanation: The author presents Curie's achievements with respect (first woman Nobel laureate, drove X-ray units to battlefields) while emphasizing the tragic cost of her discoveries (death from radiation, contaminated notebooks). This balanced perspective is admiring yet cautionary, not critical or skeptical.

articles.topic_title: Frank Epperson: the 11-year-old who invented the Popsicle
articles.title: How a Forgotten Drink Created a Frozen Empire
articles.passage_html: On a freezing San Francisco evening in 1905, eleven-year-old Frank Epperson stirred powdered soda into a cup of water on his porch. The sweet drink was a popular concoction in those days, when families mixed flavored powders with water to create fizzy beverages. Frank left his cup outside with the wooden stirring stick still in it, completely forgetting about his experiment. By morning, the cold had transformed his mistake into something entirely new: a frozen treat on a stick. Frank named his accidental creation the "Epsicle," cleverly combining his surname with the word "icicle." Yet this young inventor didn't capitalize on his discovery right away. Instead, he waited eighteen years before sharing his frozen treat with the world. In 1922, Epperson finally began selling his Epsicles at an amusement park in Alameda, California. Visitors couldn't resist the sweet, icy novelty, and the treats quickly became a sensation. The name "Epsicle" wouldn't last long, however. Frank's own children began calling the frozen treats "Pop's sicles" after their father. The nickname stuck, and by the time Epperson applied for a patent in 1923, he had officially changed the name to "Popsicle." This shift reflected how the invention had become a family favorite before it became an American staple. Epperson's venture proved remarkably successful. By 1928, just six years after he began selling them commercially, Epperson had earned royalties on more than 60 million Popsicles. These weren't just impressive numbers—they represented a complete transformation of how Americans enjoyed frozen desserts. Yet in 1925, Epperson made a surprising decision: he sold the rights to his patent to the Joe Lowe Company of New York. The reasons for this sale remain unclear, but it meant that Epperson would no longer control the product he had accidentally created two decades earlier. Today, billions of Popsicles are sold annually, and the basic concept remains unchanged from that cold night in 1905. Frank Epperson's story demonstrates how innovation sometimes emerges not from careful planning but from simple mistakes. His childhood forgetfulness, combined with San Francisco's winter chill, accidentally launched a frozen treat that would endure for more than a century. Sometimes the best discoveries happen when we're not even trying to make them.

By morning, the cold had transformed his mistake into something entirely new: a frozen treat on a stick.

Epperson's <vocab>venture|business undertaking</vocab> proved remarkably successful. By 1928, just six years after he began selling them commercially, Epperson had earned royalties on more than 60 million Popsicles. These weren't just impressive numbers—they represented a complete transformation of how Americans enjoyed frozen desserts. Today, billions of Popsicles are sold annually, and the basic concept remains unchanged from that cold night in 1905. questions.stem: Which would be the BEST title for this passage?
options: From Childhood Mistake to Frozen Treat Empire
How Popsicles Became America's Favorite Dessert
The Science Behind Freezing Drinks on Sticks
Why Frank Epperson Sold His Patent Too Early
questions.explanation: The passage focuses on how Frank Epperson's accidental childhood invention became a commercial success, making option A the most accurate title. Option B makes an unsupported claim about Popsicles being America's favorite dessert. Option C suggests scientific focus that isn't present. Option D emphasizes the sale, which is only one part of the story.

questions.stem: The author mentions Frank Epperson's children in order to
options: explain why Epperson waited so long to patent his invention
show how they helped him sell Popsicles at the amusement park
describe how the product's name changed from Epsicle to Popsicle
illustrate that frozen treats were becoming popular with young people
questions.explanation: The passage states that Epperson's children called the treats 'Pop's sicles,' which led to the name change to Popsicle. Option A uses passage elements incorrectly—the children aren't connected to the patent delay. Option B invents details not in the passage. Option D is partially true but not the author's purpose for mentioning the children.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that Frank Epperson's success with Popsicles was
options: dependent on his scientific knowledge of freezing temperatures
carefully planned from the moment of his childhood discovery
greater than he initially expected when he began selling them
limited because he sold the patent rights too quickly
questions.explanation: The 18-year delay and the rapid success (60 million sold by 1928) suggest the outcome exceeded early expectations. Option A contradicts the accidental nature of the invention. Option B directly contradicts the passage's emphasis on the accident and long delay. Option D makes a judgment not supported by the passage—we don't know if the sale was premature.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "capitalize" most nearly means
options: write in uppercase letters
invest money in a company
recognize an important moment
take advantage of an opportunity
questions.explanation: In context, 'capitalize' refers to Epperson not immediately using his invention for commercial gain, which means taking advantage of an opportunity. Options A and B are other definitions of capitalize but don't fit this context. Option C is close but not as precise as option D.

questions.stem: Which statement best summarizes the relationship between timing and Frank Epperson's invention?
options: A cold night enabled the accidental invention, but commercial success took nearly two decades to achieve
Epperson's decision to wait 18 years allowed him to perfect the Popsicle recipe before selling it
The 1905 invention succeeded immediately because frozen treats were already popular in California
Cold weather was less important than Epperson's business skills in creating the Popsicle empire
questions.explanation: The passage shows both the immediate role of cold weather in 1905 and the 18-year gap before commercial sales in 1922, making option A correct. Option B invents a reason for the delay not mentioned in the passage. Option C contradicts the passage—success came later, not immediately. Option D minimizes the cold weather's essential role in the invention itself.

articles.topic_title: Cai Lun: the inventor of paper
articles.title: Why Cai Lun Deserves Our Recognition
articles.passage_html: Imagine carrying your homework to school in bundles of heavy bamboo strips tied together with rope. Each strip weighs as much as a brick, and your assignment fills an entire cart. This was reality for Chinese scholars before 105 CE, when a court official named Cai Lun demonstrated an invention that would transform civilization. Cai Lun deserves recognition as one of history's most influential inventors because his creation of paper solved a critical problem, spread across the world, and continues to impact our lives today. First, Cai Lun's paper addressed a serious obstacle facing scholars and government officials throughout China. Before his breakthrough, people wrote on two materials: bamboo strips and silk cloth. Bamboo was so cumbersome that a single book could require a wagon for transport. Silk offered a lighter alternative, but remained too expensive for ordinary citizens or even most scholars to afford. While serving as an official in Emperor He's imperial court during the Eastern Han Dynasty, Cai Lun recognized this predicament. His solution mixed tree bark, hemp waste, old rags, and fishnets into a pulp, which workers pounded and spread thin to dry into flexible sheets. Emperor He was so impressed that he promoted Cai Lun to Shang Fang Si, placing him in charge of manufacturing instruments and weapons for the palace. Second, papermaking technology became one of China's most significant exports, traveling thousands of miles along trade routes. The technique remained a Chinese secret for centuries until 751 CE, when the Battle of Talas changed everything. Chinese papermakers captured during the battle revealed their methods to the Islamic world, and the knowledge dispersed rapidly westward. Today, Leiyang in Hunan Province honors Cai Lun's birthplace with a museum celebrating his contribution to human progress. Finally, despite living in an age of computers and tablets, we remain dependent on Cai Lun's invention. Books, newspapers, cardboard boxes, paper money, and school notebooks all trace their origins to those first sheets dried in 105 CE. The official who died in 121 CE after becoming entangled in palace political conflicts could never have envisioned his creation's lasting influence. When you write on paper today, you're using technology nearly two thousand years old—a testament to one inventor's elegant solution to an ancient problem.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: Cai Lun deserves recognition for inventing paper, which solved critical problems and continues to influence the world.
Before paper was invented, Chinese scholars struggled with heavy bamboo strips and expensive silk cloth.
Papermaking spread from China to the Islamic world after the Battle of Talas in 751 CE.
Cai Lun served Emperor He in the Eastern Han Dynasty and was later promoted to an important position.
questions.explanation: The passage argues that Cai Lun deserves recognition for three reasons: solving a problem, spreading globally, and having lasting impact. The other options are supporting details rather than the main claim.

questions.stem: Which detail from the passage BEST supports the idea that paper was a more practical writing material than previous options?
options: Cai Lun mixed tree bark, hemp waste, old rags, and fishnets to create his invention.
Bamboo was so heavy that a single book could require a wagon for transport, while silk was too expensive for most people.
Emperor He promoted Cai Lun to the position of Shang Fang Si after seeing his invention.
The city of Leiyang in Hunan Province contains a museum dedicated to Cai Lun.
questions.explanation: This detail directly shows the impracticality of both previous materials—bamboo's extreme weight and silk's high cost—demonstrating why paper was needed. The other details discuss different aspects of the invention.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that papermaking knowledge was valuable because
options: Emperor He immediately gave Cai Lun a promotion after seeing the invention.
the Islamic world wanted to capture Chinese papermakers during battles.
China kept the technique secret for hundreds of years before it spread to other regions.
Cai Lun used expensive materials like silk to create the first paper.
questions.explanation: The fact that the technique remained secret for centuries until being revealed through captured papermakers suggests it was considered valuable knowledge worth protecting. Option A is true but doesn't address value, B reverses cause and effect, and D contradicts the passage.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word 'dispersed' most nearly means
options: disappeared completely
became more concentrated
spread to different places
returned to the original location
questions.explanation: The passage describes how papermaking knowledge 'dispersed rapidly westward' after the Battle of Talas, meaning it spread to different regions. The context of knowledge traveling along trade routes confirms this meaning.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward Cai Lun can best be described as
options: admiring of his lasting contribution to civilization
skeptical about whether he truly invented paper
neutral toward his role in Chinese history
critical of his involvement in palace politics
questions.explanation: The author argues that Cai Lun 'deserves recognition' and calls his solution 'elegant,' describing him as 'one of history's most influential inventors.' This demonstrates clear admiration rather than skepticism, neutrality, or criticism.

articles.topic_title: The Eden Project: a giant biodome in an old clay pit
articles.title: A Rainforest Inside a Clay Pit
articles.passage_html: Steam rises from emerald leaves the size of dinner plates. Water trickles down moss-covered rocks. The air feels thick and warm, like stepping into a bathroom after a hot shower. Yet this isn't the Amazon jungle—it's Cornwall, England, inside the world's largest greenhouse. The Eden Project opened to the public on March 17, 2001, transforming an abandoned china clay pit near St. Austell into something extraordinary. The site's two massive bubble-shaped structures, called biomes, look like giant soap bubbles frozen in time. The Rainforest Biome towers 55 meters tall—high enough to enclose the Tower of London inside it. It sprawls across 1.56 hectares, housing roughly 1,000 plant species from tropical regions spanning the Amazon, West Africa, and Malaysia. Before construction could begin in 1998, workers faced an enormous challenge: removing 1.8 million tonnes of earth from the 60-meter-deep pit. Tim Smit, a former archaeologist and record producer, conceived this ambitious idea and partnered with architect Nicholas Grimshaw to bring his vision to life. Together, they created something that cost £141 million to build, funded by the Millennium Commission, the European Regional Development Fund, and other sources. The biomes' construction required ingenious engineering. Instead of traditional glass, builders used 625 hexagonal panels made from ETFE, a transparent material weighing less than 1% of what glass would weigh. This allowed the structures to curve and bend without collapsing. Inside the Rainforest Biome, temperatures hover between 18 and 35 degrees Celsius year-round, mimicking true tropical conditions. The smaller Mediterranean Biome covers 0.65 hectares and showcases plants from sun-baked regions including South Africa, California, and the Mediterranean coast. Walk through these immense domes today, and you'll encounter banana plants, olive groves, and towering palms—all thriving in a former quarry. What was once a scar on the landscape now pulses with life, proving that devastated land can bloom again.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: The Eden Project transformed an abandoned clay pit into massive biodomes that recreate different climate zones and demonstrate ecological restoration.

ETFE is a superior building material compared to glass because it weighs significantly less and allows for curved structures.
Cornwall, England, has become a popular destination for tourists interested in tropical plants and Mediterranean vegetation.
questions.explanation: The passage describes the Eden Project's transformation of a clay pit into biodomes while emphasizing both the engineering achievement and ecological restoration theme, which is the central focus throughout.

questions.stem: The author mentions the Tower of London in order to...
options: suggest that the Eden Project could be built in other historic locations throughout England.
help readers understand the impressive height of the Rainforest Biome through a familiar comparison.

questions.explanation: The passage states the biome is 'tall enough to enclose the Tower of London inside it,' using this well-known landmark as a comparison to help readers visualize the structure's enormous height.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that the Eden Project's location in a former clay pit was...
options: originally intended to make the project more affordable than building in a developed area.
chosen because Cornwall's natural climate closely matches tropical and Mediterranean conditions.
part of demonstrating that damaged landscapes could be restored into thriving ecosystems.
required by the Millennium Commission as a condition for providing construction funding.
questions.explanation: The final sentence emphasizes that 'what was once a scar on the landscape now pulses with life, proving that devastated land can bloom again,' suggesting restoration was a key purpose.

options: discovered accidentally
criticized publicly
examined carefully
developed originally
questions.explanation: The passage describes how Tim Smit 'conceived this ambitious idea,' meaning he originally came up with or developed the concept for the Eden Project.

questions.stem: Which best describes how the passage is organized?
options: introducing the Eden Project through sensory description, then explaining its construction and purpose
comparing the Rainforest Biome and Mediterranean Biome to show their similarities and differences
presenting a chronological history of Cornwall's clay mining industry and its eventual decline
stating a problem with abandoned industrial sites, then proposing solutions used worldwide

articles.topic_title: The Mirabal Sisters: standing up to a dictator
articles.title: The Butterflies Who Defied a Dictator
articles.passage_html: The car wound through the Dominican Republic mountains on November 25, 1960, carrying three sisters toward a prison visit. Patria, Minerva, and María Teresa Mirabal never imagined this journey would transform them into international symbols of courage. Secret police blocked their path, dragged them from the vehicle, and murdered them before pushing the car off a cliff to fake an accident. The Mirabal sisters grew up during the regime of Rafael Trujillo, who controlled the Dominican Republic from 1930 to 1961. Trujillo demanded complete obedience from every citizen. He imprisoned, tortured, or killed anyone who challenged his power. Most Dominicans feared him too much to resist. Minerva Mirabal refused to be silent. In 1949, she publicly rejected Trujillo's romantic advances at a party—a brazen act that could have cost her life immediately. Instead of retreating in fear, Minerva joined her sisters Patria and María Teresa in an underground resistance movement called the Fourteenth of June Movement. The women worked secretly to overthrow the dictator's government. Their code name became "Las Mariposas"—The Butterflies. The sisters' husbands also joined the fight and were imprisoned as political prisoners at Puerto Plata prison, located approximately 100 miles from the family home. When secret police murdered the three sisters that November day, they intended to silence the resistance. The assassination produced the opposite effect. News of their deaths ignited outrage throughout the country. Citizens who had remained quiet now demanded change. Six months later, on May 30, 1961, assassins shot and killed Trujillo, ending his 31-year dictatorship. The fourth sister, Dedé, survived and devoted her life to sharing her sisters' story until her death in 2014 at age 88. In 1999, the United Nations designated November 25 as the International Day for the Elimination of Violence Against Women, choosing the date of the sisters' murders to honor their sacrifice. The Butterflies proved that even a dictator's power cannot crush the human spirit.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: The Mirabal sisters challenged a powerful dictator through their resistance work, and their murders ultimately helped end his rule.
Rafael Trujillo controlled the Dominican Republic through fear and violence for more than three decades.
The United Nations honors brave women who fight for justice around the world every year.
Underground resistance movements work secretly to overthrow governments they believe are unfair.
questions.explanation: The passage focuses on how the Mirabal sisters opposed Trujillo and how their deaths led to change, making option A correct. Option B is true but describes only the context, not the central idea. Option C is partially true but too broad. Option D uses passage concepts but misses the specific story of the sisters.

questions.stem: The author mentions Minerva's refusal of Trujillo's advances at the party in order to
options: explain why all four sisters decided to join the underground resistance movement together
demonstrate that Minerva showed courage even before joining the organized resistance
show that Trujillo treated women in the Dominican Republic with great disrespect
prove that the dictator always punished people immediately when they opposed him
questions.explanation: The passage presents this 1949 incident as an early example of Minerva's defiance before describing the resistance work, showing her courage developed early. Option A is false because Dedé didn't join. Option C may be true but isn't the author's purpose for including this detail. Option D contradicts the passage, which notes Minerva wasn't killed immediately.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that the secret police's attempt to make the sisters' deaths look like an accident
options: succeeded because most citizens believed the car crash was real for many years
was unnecessary since Trujillo had legal authority to execute political prisoners
failed to convince people, which contributed to growing opposition against Trujillo
prevented the United Nations from learning about the murders until 1999
questions.explanation: The passage states news of the deaths caused outrage and led to Trujillo's assassination six months later, suggesting people didn't believe the fake accident story. Option A contradicts this evidence. Option B may be true but isn't suggested by the passage. Option D misuses passage facts—the UN learned about it long before 1999.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "ignited" most nearly means
options: set on fire
calmed down
carefully planned
sparked suddenly
questions.explanation: The passage describes how the murders ignited outrage, meaning they sparked or triggered it suddenly. While 'ignited' literally means 'set on fire,' in this context it's used figuratively to mean 'sparked.' Options B and C present opposite or unrelated meanings.

questions.stem: Which best describes how the passage is organized?
options: describing a dramatic event, then explaining the background and consequences
comparing the four sisters' different contributions to the resistance movement
presenting Trujillo's crimes in order from least serious to most serious
arguing that underground movements are the most effective way to challenge dictators
questions.explanation: The passage opens with the sisters' murder, then moves back to explain the dictatorship and resistance work, and finally discusses the aftermath—a clear event-background-consequences structure. Option B is wrong because the passage doesn't compare all four sisters. Option C misrepresents the organization. Option D describes argumentative structure, but this is informational writing.

articles.topic_title: The Rosetta Stone: cracking ancient Egyptian writing
articles.title: The Stone That Unlocked Ancient Egypt
articles.passage_html: The black granite slab weighed as much as a small car—760 kilograms of polished stone covered with mysterious symbols. French soldiers digging near the Egyptian town of Rosetta in July 1799 had no idea they'd stumbled upon the key to understanding a lost language. The massive stone puzzled everyone who examined it. Three different types of writing covered its surface: ancient Egyptian hieroglyphics at the top, Demotic script in the middle, and ancient Greek at the bottom. Scholars quickly realized these weren't three different messages but the same decree written three times. The proclamation honored thirteen-year-old King Ptolemy V and celebrated his coronation anniversary in 196 BCE. Because educated people could still read ancient Greek, researchers finally possessed a tool to decipher hieroglyphics—those elegant picture-symbols that had remained silent for centuries. Yet cracking the code proved far more difficult than anyone anticipated. When British forces defeated Napoleon's army in 1801, they seized the stone and transported it to London. For decades, the 114-centimeter-tall tablet sat in the British Museum while scholars struggled to unlock its secrets. English scientist Thomas Young made crucial progress in 1814 when he proved that cartouches—distinctive oval shapes surrounding certain hieroglyphs—contained phonetic spellings of royal names like Ptolemy. Instead of representing only ideas or objects, some hieroglyphs actually represented sounds. French scholar Jean-François Champollion built upon Young's work with extraordinary dedication. He possessed one crucial advantage: knowledge of Coptic, a language descended from ancient Egyptian and still spoken in some Christian churches in Egypt. By comparing Coptic words with the hieroglyphic symbols, Champollion gradually unraveled the ancient writing system. In 1822, after twenty-three years of collaborative study by various researchers, he finally succeeded. The breakthrough transformed our understanding of ancient Egypt. Suddenly, temple walls, tomb inscriptions, and papyrus scrolls could speak again. The voices of pharaohs, priests, and ordinary Egyptians—silent for nearly two thousand years—echoed across time. A single stone, discovered by accident during a military campaign, had opened a window into an entire civilization.

articles.topic_title: The Christmas Truce of 1914: when soldiers stopped fighting
articles.title: When Enemies Became Friends on Christmas
articles.passage_html: The flicker of candlelight danced along the muddy trenches near Ypres, Belgium, as darkness fell on December 24, 1914. British soldiers, crouched behind sandbags, stared in disbelief. Across No Man's Land—the deadly strip of earth between opposing armies—German troops were decorating their trenches with small Christmas trees called tannenbaums. The glow of candles pierced the winter night, and then something extraordinary happened: voices rose in song. The melody of "Stille Nacht" (Silent Night) drifted through the cold air from the German trenches. British soldiers, positioned just fifty to one hundred yards away, recognized the familiar tune. They responded, their voices carrying English Christmas carols across the scarred battlefield. For months, these men had exchanged only bullets and artillery shells. Now they traded songs. When Christmas morning arrived, something unprecedented occurred. Soldiers began climbing out of their trenches and walking into No Man's Land, the place where countless men had fallen. They met in the middle, extending hands instead of weapons. Lieutenant Geoffrey Heinekey of the Royal Field Artillery shook hands with German soldiers and swapped a cigar for a cigarette. Men exchanged tobacco, food, and buttons clipped from their own uniforms as keepsakes. The most astonishing sight came next: football matches. British and German troops kicked makeshift balls across the frozen ground, laughing and shouting. One documented game near Ypres ended 3-2, with the Germans claiming victory. The Christmas Truce spread along approximately two-thirds of the thirty-mile British section of the Western Front, affecting thousands of soldiers. Yet this remarkable peace was fleeting. In most locations, the truce lasted just one day, though a few areas remained quiet for several days. Military commanders on both sides grew indignant and issued strict orders preventing similar truces in future years. The war continued for four more brutal years. That single day in 1914 revealed something profound: even in humanity's darkest moments, the desire for peace and connection can break through hatred. The Christmas Truce stands as proof that enemies can choose understanding over violence, even when the world around them demands otherwise.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: During World War I's Christmas Truce of 1914, opposing soldiers temporarily set aside warfare to celebrate together, demonstrating humanity's capacity for peace even amid conflict.
British and German soldiers sang Christmas carols and played football matches throughout World War I to maintain morale.

questions.explanation: The passage describes how soldiers from opposing sides temporarily stopped fighting on Christmas 1914 and shows this event's significance about human connection, making option A correct. Option B incorrectly suggests this happened throughout the war. Option C contradicts the fact that commanders were displeased. Option D invents a motive not mentioned in the passage.

Option A focuses on a detail (score) rather than purpose. Option C reverses cause and effect—singing came first. Option D overgeneralizes beyond what the passage states.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that the Christmas Truce
options: was carefully planned by military leaders weeks before Christmas Day arrived

options: running away quickly
completely forgotten
difficult to understand
brief and temporary

questions.stem: Which statement best summarizes the relationship between the soldiers' actions and their commanders' responses?
options: Soldiers created an unauthorized moment of peace that commanders later worked to prevent from happening again.
Commanders encouraged the Christmas Truce to continue for several days and praised soldiers for their initiative.
Soldiers followed direct orders from commanders to celebrate Christmas before returning to scheduled combat.
Commanders supported the initial truce but later changed their minds when the war continued too long.
questions.explanation: The passage shows soldiers initiating the truce (singing, climbing out, playing football) while commanders were 'displeased' and 'issued orders to prevent similar events,' making option A correct. Option B contradicts the commanders' displeasure. Option C suggests soldiers followed orders, which contradicts the spontaneous nature. Option D invents a timeline not supported by the text.

articles.topic_title: CRISPR: how we learned to edit DNA
articles.title: CRISPR: The Revolutionary Tool Transforming Medicine
articles.passage_html: In a laboratory in 1987, Japanese scientist Yoshizumi Ishino squinted at a peculiar pattern glowing on his computer screen. While studying the DNA of E. coli bacteria, he had stumbled upon something strange: identical sequences of genetic code repeating over and over, like a stuttering sentence. Ishino had no idea what these sequences did, but he had discovered the foundation of what would become humanity's most powerful tool for editing the blueprint of life itself. For over a decade, scientists puzzled over these mysterious repeating patterns. Then Francisco Mojica, working in salt marshes near Alicante, Spain, made a pivotal discovery. In 2000, after years of studying microbes that thrived in salty water, Mojica realized these sequences weren't random at all. They formed a bacterial immune system—a genetic memory bank that recorded past viral attacks. The bacteria could recognize returning viruses and defend themselves, snipping the invaders' DNA like vigilant guards cutting an enemy's rope. Yet the true power of this system remained locked until 2012, when Jennifer Doudna and Emmanuelle Charpentier published their landmark paper in the journal Science. They had transformed bacterial defenses into precision editing tools. Their CRISPR-Cas9 system works like molecular scissors: a protein called Cas9 follows a strand of RNA to locate a specific 20-letter genetic code, then cuts exactly at that spot. This technology proved approximately 100 times faster and far cheaper than previous methods. In 2020, Doudna and Charpentier received the Nobel Prize in Chemistry, becoming the first two women to share this honor without male co-winners. CRISPR's potential to eliminate disease makes it worth embracing, despite valid concerns. First, the technology offers genuine hope for curing genetic disorders. In 2017, researchers at Oregon Health and Science University repaired a heart disease mutation in 42 out of 58 human embryos—success rates that seemed improbable just years earlier. Second, CRISPR's speed and affordability mean treatments won't remain exclusive to the wealthy. Previous gene-editing methods cost millions and took years; CRISPR accomplishes the same work in months at a fraction of the price. The journey from Ishino's mysterious sequences to Nobel Prize-winning technology proves that scientific progress often begins with simple curiosity. As CRISPR continues evolving, we must navigate ethical questions carefully—but we should not let fear prevent us from pursuing its life-saving promise.

articles.topic_title: How honeybees vote on a new home
articles.title: Honeybees Vote for Their New Home
articles.passage_html: A swarm of 10,000 honeybees hangs from a tree branch, humming with energy. Hundreds of scout bees launch into the air, fanning out across the landscape. Their mission: find the perfect home for their colony. This search isn't decided by a single queen bee giving orders. Instead, honeybees rely on a democratic process that may surpass human voting systems in fairness and efficiency. The honeybee method for choosing a new home deserves recognition as one of nature's most sophisticated decision-making systems. Two key reasons support this claim: the bees gather detailed information through repeated exploration, and they correct their mistakes by switching preferences when better options emerge. First, scout bees don't rush their evaluation. When a colony needs a new nest, several hundred scouts fly outward, searching within a radius of about 5 kilometers. Each scout examines potential sites using specific criteria: a cavity volume of approximately 40 liters, an entrance hole measuring 12.5 square centimeters, and a height of at least 3 meters above ground. Martin Lindauer, studying at the University of Munich in the 1950s, first observed how scouts share their findings through waggle dances. The angle of the dance relative to the sun indicates direction. The duration of the waggle portion reveals distance. More importantly, the vigor and length of the dance communicate quality—a scout who discovers an exceptional site performs longer, more energetic movements. Second, honeybees demonstrate remarkable flexibility in their voting. Thomas Seeley, a biologist at Cornell University, documented that scouts visit multiple locations advertised by their sisters. If a scout encounters a superior site, she abandons her original choice and begins dancing for the better option. This willingness to change positions prevents the swarm from settling for a mediocre home. The process continues for one to three days until approximately 15 scouts dance for the same location. Once a quorum of 10 to 20 scouts gathers at a single site, the debate ends. Worker bees raise their body temperature to 35 degrees Celsius, and the entire swarm prepares for flight. This tiny insect's approach reveals an important truth: the best decisions emerge not from a single authority, but from gathering multiple perspectives and remaining open to better alternatives. Perhaps humans could learn something from watching bees deliberate their future.
