articles.topic_title: Marie Tharp: how a woman mapped the ocean floor
articles.title: Marie Tharp: Mapping the Hidden Ocean Floor
articles.passage_html: In a laboratory at Columbia University in 1952, Marie Tharp hunched over sheets of graph paper scattered across her desk. Her pencil traced patterns through thousands of sonar readings—numbers that represented the ocean floor's topography. Most people expected the seafloor to be flat and featureless, like a giant bathtub. But as Tharp connected the dots, something unexpected emerged: a deep V-shaped crack running down the center of the Mid-Atlantic Ridge. Tharp's journey to this moment began in Ypsilanti, Michigan, where she was born on July 30, 1920. By 1944, she had earned a master's degree in petroleum geology from the University of Michigan—an unusual achievement for a woman at that time. Four years later, she joined Columbia University's Lamont Geological Observatory, working alongside geologist Bruce Heezen. He traveled on research vessels, collecting ocean depth measurements using sonar technology. Tharp stayed behind in the lab. Women weren't allowed on research ships, a rule that seemed designed to keep her landlocked. Yet this restriction didn't stop her. While Heezen and his team sailed across the Atlantic, Tharp transformed their data into something tangible. She plotted each measurement by hand, building a picture of a world no one had ever seen. That V-shaped rift she discovered in 1952 wasn't just a crack—it was evidence that Earth's continents actually drifted apart over time. The seafloor was splitting, creating new ocean crust. Scientists had debated continental drift for decades. Tharp's rift valley proved they were right. Between 1957 and 1977, Tharp and Heezen created the first complete map of the entire ocean floor. Her hand-drawn illustrations revealed underwater mountains, valleys, and ridges across approximately 361 million square kilometers. These weren't simple sketches—they were meticulous scientific documents that changed how we understand our planet. In 1995, the Library of Congress recognized Tharp as one of the four greatest cartographers of the 20th century. The woman who wasn't allowed on ships had illuminated more of Earth's surface than perhaps anyone before her. Sometimes the most important discoveries happen not at sea, but in the quiet persistence of someone who refuses to accept limitations.
articles.passage_raw:
In a laboratory at Columbia University in 1952, Marie Tharp hunched over sheets of graph paper scattered across her desk. Her pencil traced patterns through thousands of sonar readings—numbers that represented the ocean floor's <vocab>topography|shape and features of the land surface</vocab>. Most people expected the seafloor to be flat and featureless, like a giant bathtub. But as Tharp connected the dots, something unexpected emerged: a deep V-shaped crack running down the center of the Mid-Atlantic Ridge.

Tharp's journey to this moment began in Ypsilanti, Michigan, where she was born on July 30, 1920. By 1944, she had earned a master's degree in petroleum geology from the University of Michigan—an unusual achievement for a woman at that time. Four years later, she joined Columbia University's Lamont Geological Observatory, working alongside geologist Bruce Heezen. He traveled on research vessels, collecting ocean depth measurements using sonar technology. Tharp stayed behind in the lab. Women weren't allowed on research ships, a rule that seemed designed to keep her landlocked.

Yet this <vocab>restriction|rule that limits what someone can do</vocab> didn't stop her. While Heezen and his team sailed across the Atlantic, Tharp transformed their data into something <vocab>tangible|real and able to be touched</vocab>. She plotted each measurement by hand, building a picture of a world no one had ever seen. That V-shaped rift she discovered in 1952 wasn't just a crack—it was evidence that Earth's continents actually drifted apart over time. The seafloor was splitting, creating new ocean crust. Scientists had <vocab>debated|argued about different sides of an issue</vocab> continental drift for decades. Tharp's rift valley proved they were right.

Between 1957 and 1977, Tharp and Heezen created the first complete map of the entire ocean floor. Her hand-drawn illustrations revealed underwater mountains, valleys, and ridges across approximately 361 million square kilometers. These weren't simple sketches—they were <vocab>meticulous|very careful and precise</vocab> scientific documents that changed how we understand our planet.

In 1995, the Library of Congress recognized Tharp as one of the four greatest cartographers of the 20th century. The woman who wasn't allowed on ships had <vocab>illuminated|lit up or made clear</vocab> more of Earth's surface than perhaps anyone before her. Sometimes the most important discoveries happen not at sea, but in the quiet persistence of someone who refuses to accept limitations.

articles.topic_title: Bedouin navigation: crossing a desert by the stars
articles.title: Ancient Star Maps of the Desert Bedouin
articles.passage_html: A lone traveler stands in the Arabian Desert as darkness falls, surrounded by sand stretching endlessly in every direction. No compass. No map. Yet this Bedouin guide knows exactly which direction to walk, simply by gazing upward at the glittering sky above. For thousands of years, Bedouin navigators mastered the art of crossing vast deserts by reading the stars, and their methods prove that traditional knowledge can rival modern technology. This ancient system deserves recognition as one of history's most sophisticated navigation techniques. First, the Bedouin created an extraordinarily detailed celestial map that rivals anything produced with telescopes. These desert travelers identified and named at least 160 individual stars and constellations—far more than most modern stargazers could recognize. They divided the night sky into 28 sections called manazil al-qamar, or lunar mansions, tracking which star group the moon passed through during its monthly journey. When traveling at night, they located Polaris, the North Star, to determine true north, a practice they perfected over 1,500 years ago. The constellation Ursa Major, known to us as the Big Dipper, became their clock. By observing how it rotated around Polaris throughout the night, Bedouin guides could estimate the hour and decide when their caravans should rest or continue moving. Second, the Bedouin developed a multifaceted navigation system that combined multiple natural signals for remarkable accuracy. Stars provided their primary compass, but these resourceful travelers didn't rely on a single method. They observed the shamal, a powerful wind that blows from the northwest across Arabia at speeds of 30-40 kilometers per hour, using it to confirm their direction. The star Suhail, also called Canopus and the second brightest star visible from Earth, appears on the southern horizon each August. Its arrival signaled that cooler weather approached, helping tribes plan their seasonal movements. When violent sandstorms blocked the stars from view, navigators didn't panic. Instead, they felt the direction of sand dune ridges with their hands and feet, knowing that dunes in the Arabian Desert typically run parallel from northeast to southwest. This elaborate knowledge wasn't written in books. For approximately 3,000 years, Bedouin families passed these navigation secrets orally from parents to children, only documenting them in Arabic texts during the 8th and 9th centuries. Today, as GPS satellites orbit overhead, we should remember that humans once held entire star maps in their memories, transforming the night sky into a celestial highway across barren landscapes. The Bedouin proved that careful observation and accumulated wisdom could guide travelers safely through some of Earth's harshest environments. Their legacy challenges us to consider: what other valuable knowledge might disappear if we forget to honor the expertise of traditional cultures?

articles.topic_title: The lyrebird: nature's greatest mimic
articles.title: The Lyrebird: Australia's Master of Sound
articles.passage_html: Deep in the forests of southeastern Australia, a bird spreads its tail feathers into a shimmering fan that reaches 55 centimeters wide. The superb lyrebird tilts forward, letting the silvery plumes cascade over its body like a flowing waterfall. Then it opens its beak and produces sounds that seem impossible: a camera shutter clicking, a car alarm wailing, a chainsaw roaring through wood. The lyrebird's repertoire extends far beyond typical birdsong. Males can accurately mimic over 20 different bird species, switching between calls with remarkable ease. Yet their most surprising talent involves copying mechanical noises from the human world. At the Adelaide Zoo in South Australia during the 1960s, a lyrebird named Chook became famous for imitating construction sounds. Workers building new zoo facilities heard their own hammers and saws replicated by the bird, note for perfect note. This extraordinary ability stems from the lyrebird's unique anatomy. While most songbirds possess a syrinx—the vocal organ that produces sound—with just one pair of muscles, the lyrebird's syrinx contains three pairs. These additional muscles allow the bird to create more complex sounds and control them with greater accuracy. Scientists first described this remarkable species, Menura novaehollandiae, in 1800, though it took decades to understand how its voice truly worked. Male lyrebirds don't just sing randomly through the forest. During breeding season from June to August, they construct special performance stages on the forest floor. Each male clears a display mound up to one meter in diameter, removing leaves and debris to create a clean arena. There, he performs his elaborate singing and mimicking routines, hoping to attract a female. Females show little interest in theatrics themselves. After mating, they build dome-shaped nests from sticks, moss, and ferns, placing them on ledges or in tree ferns. Each female lays only one egg per season, investing all her energy into raising a single chick. Meanwhile, Albert's lyrebird, the second lyrebird species, lives in an even more restricted habitat—just 176 kilometers of coastal Queensland rainforest. These forest performers remind us that nature's creativity often surpasses human imagination. A bird that can transform itself into a chainsaw or a camera challenges our understanding of what voices can achieve.

articles.topic_title: Eunice Newton Foote: who discovered the greenhouse effect (and was forgotten)
articles.title: The Scientist History Nearly Erased
articles.passage_html: In August 1856, a hushed audience filled the meeting hall of the American Association for the Advancement of Science in Albany, New York. Professor Joseph Henry stepped to the podium to read aloud a scientific paper—but the words belonged to someone else. Eunice Newton Foote, an American scientist, had written a revolutionary paper about heat and gases, yet she could not present it herself. Why? Because women were forbidden from presenting their own scientific work at the time. Foote's paper, titled "Circumstances Affecting the Heat of the Sun's Rays," revealed something remarkable. Using an air pump, four glass cylinders, and four thermometers, she had conducted experiments proving that carbon dioxide gas trapped more heat than ordinary air when exposed to sunlight. She placed the cylinders in bright sunshine and watched the thermometers rise, recording which gases absorbed the most warmth. Her discovery explained a fundamental principle of how Earth's atmosphere works—what we now call the greenhouse effect. The American Journal of Science and Arts published her findings in November 1856, creating a permanent public record of her achievement. Yet Foote's accomplishment was nearly lost to history. Three years later, in 1859, Irish scientist John Tyndall conducted similar research independently, without knowledge of Foote's work. Because Tyndall was a man working in Europe's scientific centers, he received credit for discovering the greenhouse effect. Foote's name vanished from textbooks and scientific discussions. This omission reflected the pervasive discrimination women scientists faced in the nineteenth century. Foote deserves recognition as the true pioneer of climate science. Born in Connecticut in 1819, she attended Troy Female Seminary in New York, one of the first American schools offering science education to women. She persevered through barriers that would have stopped many others, conducting careful experiments that advanced human understanding. Her work remained buried until 2011, when retired petroleum geologist Ray Sorenson discovered her paper while researching climate science history—155 years after she published it. Science belongs to everyone who asks questions and seeks answers through careful observation. When we erase scientists like Eunice Foote from history, we lose not just their stories but also inspiration for future innovators. Her thermometers and glass cylinders revealed a truth about our planet that shapes climate science today. Remembering her name corrects a historical injustice and reminds us that genius can emerge anywhere—if we give it the chance.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: Eunice Newton Foote discovered the greenhouse effect before John Tyndall, but discrimination against women scientists caused her achievement to be forgotten until recently.
Women scientists in the nineteenth century conducted important experiments using simple equipment like thermometers and glass cylinders.
The greenhouse effect was discovered independently by two scientists working in different countries during the 1850s.
Scientific discoveries about climate change have been made by both men and women throughout history.
questions.explanation: The passage argues that Foote deserves recognition as the true pioneer, explaining how her discovery came first but was erased due to gender discrimination. Options 2 and 4 are too broad (Type B: correct premise, wrong scope), while option 3 misses the injustice argument central to the passage (Type C: uses passage words but misses the point).

questions.stem: The author mentions Professor Joseph Henry in order to
options: explain why Foote's discovery became well-known in the scientific community
illustrate the barriers that prevented women from presenting their own scientific work
show that male scientists supported women's participation in science during the 1850s
demonstrate that the Smithsonian Institution conducted important climate research
questions.explanation: Henry read Foote's paper because she was forbidden from presenting it herself, illustrating gender discrimination. Option 1 contradicts the passage—her work was forgotten (Type B: wrong conclusion). Option 3 is partially true but misses that Henry's role highlighted restrictions, not support (Type B). Option 4 uses passage words but misrepresents the point (Type C).

questions.stem: The passage suggests that if Foote had been allowed to present her own work in 1856,
options: John Tyndall would have learned about her research and built upon her findings
she would have conducted additional experiments on other greenhouse gases
she might have received proper credit as the discoverer of the greenhouse effect
the American Journal of Science and Arts would not have published her paper
questions.explanation: The passage argues her achievements were erased due to discrimination, implying proper recognition might have followed proper presentation. Option 1 is possible but not suggested (Type A: plausible but not in passage). Option 4 contradicts the passage—publication happened despite presentation restrictions (Type B). Option 2 invents details (Type A).

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "persevered" most nearly means
options: complained loudly
changed direction
worked quickly
continued steadily
questions.explanation: The passage describes Foote continuing her work despite barriers, which means continuing steadily despite difficulty. The context emphasizes persistence through obstacles, not speed (option 3), complaint (option 1), or changing course (option 2).

questions.stem: Which best describes how the passage is organized?
options: It presents an injustice, explains the historical context, then argues for correcting it
It compares two scientists' discoveries and evaluates which was more important
It describes events in chronological order from 1819 to 2011
It lists several reasons why women scientists faced discrimination in the nineteenth century
questions.explanation: The passage opens with Foote's inability to present (injustice), explains her discovery and erasure (context), then argues she deserves recognition (correction). Option 2 misrepresents the focus—it's not comparing importance but correcting erasure (Type C). Option 3 is partially true but misses the argumentative structure (Type B). Option 4 focuses on one element, not overall organization (Type B).

articles.topic_title: The Lascaux cave paintings: 40,000 years of art
articles.title: The Ancient Artists of Lascaux Cave
articles.passage_html: Four teenagers scrambled through a narrow hole near Montignac, France, on September 12, 1940. Marcel Ravidat, Jacques Marsal, Georges Agnel, and Simon Coencas had followed their dog into the opening, expecting to find an old tunnel. Instead, their flashlight beams swept across something impossible: enormous painted bulls stretching across stone walls, their bodies outlined in black pigment that seemed to move in the flickering light. The boys had discovered the Lascaux cave, one of the most important archaeological sites ever found. Scientists later used radiocarbon dating to determine that prehistoric artists created these paintings approximately 17,000 years ago during the Upper Paleolithic period. The cave contains nearly 6,000 figures, including 900 painted animals such as horses, stags, bison, and aurochs—extinct wild cattle that once roamed Europe. The main chamber, called the Hall of the Bulls, measures 66 feet long and features four massive black bulls. The largest bull stretches 17 feet across the wall, longer than most modern cars. How did these ancient artists create such detailed images? They mixed natural pigments from their surroundings. Iron oxide produced red colors, while manganese oxide created black. They combined these minerals with charcoal, animal fat, or clay to form paint that could adhere to stone surfaces. The artists likely worked by firelight, painting images of animals they hunted and observed daily. After authorities opened Lascaux to visitors in 1948, approximately 1,200 people toured the cave each day. However, this popularity created a serious problem. Carbon dioxide and humidity from human breath began damaging the ancient paintings. In 1963, authorities made the difficult decision to close the cave permanently to protect the artwork. UNESCO designated Lascaux as a World Heritage Site in 1983, recognizing its profound importance to understanding early human culture. Today, visitors can experience Lascaux II, an exact reproduction opened in 1983 just 200 meters from the original cave. This replica allows people worldwide to witness the remarkable skill of artists who lived 17,000 years ago—humans who felt the same urge we do today to create beauty and leave their mark on the world.

describe the most difficult chamber for tourists to reach
questions.explanation: The passage provides specific measurements (66 feet long chamber, 17-foot bull) to show how large and impressive the paintings are. The cave wasn't named after bulls, no comparison to modern methods appears, and tourist access isn't discussed in that section.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that closing Lascaux to the public in 1963
options: was an unpopular decision that reduced tourism revenue
happened too late to prevent all damage to the paintings
required balancing public access against artwork preservation
occurred because the cave became too crowded with visitors
questions.explanation: The passage describes it as a 'difficult decision' made after damage occurred, showing authorities had to weigh competing priorities. While crowding contributed to damage, the closure was about preservation, not crowd control. Revenue and timing aren't discussed.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word 'adhere' most nearly means
options: blend
transform
spread
attach
questions.explanation: The passage explains that artists mixed materials to form paint that could stick to stone surfaces. 'Attach' best captures this meaning of staying firmly in place on the wall.

questions.stem: Which best describes how the passage is organized?
options: describing events in chronological order from discovery to present day
comparing ancient painting techniques with modern art methods
presenting a problem about cave damage, then explaining the solution
arguing that Lascaux should reopen to public visitors
questions.explanation: The passage follows a timeline: 1940 discovery, dating to 17,000 years ago, 1948 opening, 1963 closure, 1983 World Heritage designation and replica opening. The other structures don't match the passage's organization.

articles.topic_title: Tardigrades: microscopic animals that survive in space
articles.title: The Tiny Creatures That Conquered Space
articles.passage_html: In September 2007, thousands of eight-legged animals no bigger than poppy seeds tumbled through the void of space. The European Space Agency had launched them aboard the FOTON-M3 mission, exposing them to freezing temperatures, deadly radiation, and a vacuum that would kill most organisms within seconds. Yet after ten days orbiting Earth, these microscopic travelers returned home alive. Scientists call them tardigrades, and they might be the toughest creatures on our planet. Tardigrades measure between 0.3 and 0.5 millimeters in length, so tiny that you would need a microscope to observe their plump, segmented bodies. Despite their diminutive size, these animals possess eight legs with four to eight claws on each foot, allowing them to lumber across surfaces like miniature bears. In fact, their nickname "water bears" comes from their slow, deliberate movements. More than 1,300 species inhabit environments across all seven continents, from tropical rainforests to the frozen expanse of Antarctica. What makes tardigrades nearly indestructible? The secret lies in a survival technique called cryptobiosis. When conditions become too harsh, tardigrades curl into a dried husk called a tun. Their bodies expel water until less than 3 percent remains, and their metabolism slows to 0.01 percent of normal levels—barely functioning at all. In this suspended state, they can endure temperatures as low as -272 degrees Celsius, just one degree above absolute zero, and as high as 150 degrees Celsius. Even more remarkably, they can survive without food or water for more than 30 years. The durability of tardigrades extends beyond Earth. In 2019, the Israeli spacecraft Beresheet crashed on the Moon carrying thousands of dehydrated tardigrades. Scientists believe some may have survived the impact, making these resilient organisms the first Earth creatures to potentially colonize another world—even if unintentionally. Tardigrades challenge our understanding of life's limits. If these microscopic animals can withstand the harshest conditions imaginable, what other forms of life might exist in the universe's most unexpected places?

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: Tardigrades are microscopic animals with remarkable survival abilities that allow them to endure extreme conditions including space.
The European Space Agency discovered that tardigrades can survive in space during the FOTON-M3 mission in 2007.
Tardigrades use cryptobiosis to slow their metabolism and can live for 30 years without food or water.
Scientists have found over 1,300 species of tardigrades living on all seven continents including Antarctica.
questions.explanation: Option A captures the main idea by addressing both what tardigrades are and their remarkable survival abilities. Options B, C, and D are all supporting details but do not encompass the passage's central focus.

questions.stem: The author mentions the Beresheet spacecraft in order to
options: prove that tardigrades can survive on other planets permanently
illustrate how tardigrades' durability extends to potential colonization beyond Earth
explain why Israel chose tardigrades for their Moon mission
demonstrate that tardigrades need water to survive lunar conditions
questions.explanation: The passage uses the Beresheet example to show tardigrades' extreme durability extending to space colonization. Option A overstates the claim (the passage says 'may have survived'), option C is not mentioned, and option D contradicts the text about dehydration.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that tardigrades' ability to survive extreme conditions
options: means they originally evolved on another planet before coming to Earth
makes them useful subjects for studying how organisms adapt to climate change
could help scientists understand the possibility of life in harsh environments beyond Earth
proves that all microscopic organisms can survive in space with proper preparation
questions.explanation: The passage's concluding question about 'what other forms of life might exist in the universe's most unexpected places' implies tardigrades help us understand possibilities for extraterrestrial life. Options A and D make unsupported claims, and B is not mentioned.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word 'endure' most nearly means
options: accept without complaint
survive through difficulty
last for a long time
strengthen against damage
questions.explanation: In context, 'endure' refers to tardigrades surviving extremely harsh temperature conditions. While 'endure' can mean options A or C in other contexts, option B best fits the passage's meaning about survival capability.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward tardigrades can best be described as
options: admiring and fascinated
skeptical and questioning
concerned and cautionary
neutral and detached
questions.explanation: The author uses phrases like 'toughest creatures on our planet' and 'even more remarkably' showing admiration and fascination. The tone is positive and engaged rather than skeptical, concerned, or neutral.

articles.topic_title: Chocolate: from sacred drink to candy bar
articles.title: From Sacred Brew to Five-Cent Treat
articles.passage_html: In the bustling Aztec marketplace, a trader could exchange 100 smooth, oval cacao beans for a single fat turkey. The beans clinked softly as they passed from hand to hand, more precious than gold. These weren't ordinary seeds—they held the power to create a bitter, frothy drink reserved for emperors and warriors. The Aztecs had inherited their love of chocolate from earlier civilizations, including the ancient Olmec, who had been grinding cacao beans and sipping chocolate drinks as far back as 1500 BCE. When Spanish conquistador Hernán Cortés visited the court of Aztec emperor Moctezuma II in the early 1500s, he witnessed servants pouring the dark, foaming beverage from great heights to create its signature froth. Cortés recognized opportunity. In 1528, he carried cacao beans across the Atlantic to Spain, where Europeans eventually sweetened the beverage with sugar and heated it until steam rose from ornate cups. By 1657, wealthy Londoners crowded into England's first chocolate house, paying steep prices to savor this luxurious imported drink. The transformation from liquid to solid required ingenuity. For centuries, chocolate remained exclusively a beverage, its thick paste difficult to shape. Then, in 1847, British company J.S. Fry and Sons discovered they could blend cocoa powder, sugar, and melted cacao butter into a smooth mixture that hardened into bars. This breakthrough made chocolate portable and convenient, no longer requiring hot water and careful preparation. Milton Hershey revolutionized chocolate again in 1905 when his Pennsylvania factory began manufacturing milk chocolate bars that cost just five cents. Suddenly, children could buy their own treats, and chocolate shifted from aristocratic luxury to everyday pleasure. Today, the journey from tree to treat relies on immense West African farms—the Ivory Coast alone produces roughly 2 million metric tons of cacao annually, about 40% of the world's supply. Each cacao tree yields merely 20 to 30 pods per year, and workers must split open these football-sized pods to extract the 20 to 50 precious beans hidden inside. What began as sacred currency for ancient emperors now fills supermarket shelves worldwide. That simple chocolate bar in your hand connects you to 3,500 years of history, from Olmec priests to modern farmers, all devoted to the remarkable cacao bean.

questions.stem: Which would be the BEST title for this passage?
options: The Evolution of Chocolate Through Civilizations
How Cacao Trees Produce Chocolate Pods
Why the Aztecs Used Cacao as Money
Milton Hershey's Impact on American Candy
questions.explanation: The passage traces chocolate's transformation from ancient sacred drink to modern affordable candy, covering multiple civilizations and time periods. The other options focus on only one aspect mentioned in the passage.

questions.stem: The author mentions J.S. Fry and Sons in order to...

describe the first time chocolate was sold in stores

compare British chocolate to American chocolate

questions.stem: The passage suggests that before 1847, chocolate was...
options: unknown outside of Mexico and Central America
too expensive for most European families to afford
impossible to enjoy without special preparation
considered healthier than it is today
questions.explanation: The passage indicates chocolate remained exclusively a beverage requiring hot water and careful preparation until J.S. Fry and Sons created the first solid bar in 1847. While it was expensive, the passage emphasizes the preparation challenge as the key limitation.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "manufacturing" most nearly means...
options: designing
purchasing
advertising
producing
questions.explanation: The passage uses 'manufacturing' to describe Hershey's factory making chocolate bars in large quantities. 'Producing' captures this meaning of creating goods on a large scale.

questions.stem: Which best describes how the passage is organized?

comparing how different cultures prepared chocolate drinks
stating a problem with chocolate production, then explaining solutions
introducing cacao trees, then describing how they grow
questions.explanation: The passage follows a clear timeline: Olmec (1500 BCE), Aztecs, Spanish conquistadors (1528), English chocolate houses (1657), solid bars (1847), and Hershey (1905), ending with modern production. It does not focus on comparing cultures or solving problems.

articles.topic_title: Wang Zhenyi: the Qing Dynasty astronomer
articles.title: Wang Zhenyi: Scholar Who Lit the Night Sky
articles.passage_html: A glowing lamp flickered across a courtyard in Qing Dynasty China as a young woman positioned a crystal sphere to dangle above a round table. Wang Zhenyi adjusted the objects carefully, watching shadows creep across the makeshift Earth below. This homemade experiment would help her unlock one of nature's mysteries: why the moon occasionally vanishes into darkness during a lunar eclipse. Born in 1768 in Anhui Province, Wang Zhenyi grew up surrounded by knowledge. Her grandfather, a former magistrate, had assembled more than 75 bookcases bursting with books in his personal library. The dusty pages became Wang's classroom. Before turning sixteen, she traveled with her father and grandfather through five provinces, journeying as far north as Jilin Province. These trips exposed her to different landscapes, peoples, and ideas that would shape her scientific thinking. Wang mastered complex mathematical concepts, including the Pythagorean theorem and principles from the ancient text Computational Methods in Nine Chapters. Yet she refused to keep this knowledge locked away in scholarly circles. She wrote at least thirteen books on astronomy and mathematics, deliberately crafting them in simple language so farmers, merchants, and other ordinary citizens could grasp difficult scientific ideas. Her famous article "Dispute of Longitude and Stars" challenged widespread confusion about the heavens, explaining with clear logic that Earth curves into a sphere rather than stretching flat like a pancake. Her courtyard demonstration revealed brilliant ingenuity. The lamp represented the sun's blazing light, the crystal sphere stood in for the moon, and the round table became Earth itself. By shifting these objects, Wang could show exactly how Earth's shadow falls across the moon's surface during an eclipse—a phenomenon that had baffled people for generations. Wang married at twenty-five but continued writing and researching until her death just two years later in 1797. She lived only twenty-nine years, yet her legacy challenges us still: knowledge belongs not to the privileged few, but to anyone curious enough to seek it. In candlelit courtyards and dusty libraries, wisdom waits for those brave enough to question the darkness.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: Wang Zhenyi used creative experiments and clear writing to make complex astronomy and mathematics accessible to ordinary people during the Qing Dynasty.
Wang Zhenyi traveled through five provinces in China before age sixteen, learning about different cultures and scientific ideas.
Scientists in ancient China had to overcome many obstacles to share their discoveries with educated scholars.
Women during the Qing Dynasty rarely received education but sometimes managed to study mathematics and science.

questions.stem: The author mentions Wang Zhenyi's grandfather's book collection in order to
options: prove that wealthy families always supported women's education in the Qing Dynasty
illustrate the rich learning environment that shaped Wang's early intellectual development
explain why Wang eventually became a magistrate like her grandfather
show that books were extremely expensive and rare during this historical period
questions.explanation: The passage describes the 75 bookcases as the environment where Wang 'grew up surrounded by knowledge,' directly connecting the library to her educational foundation.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that Wang Zhenyi's courtyard experiment was significant because it
options: proved that ancient Chinese astronomy was more advanced than European science
required expensive equipment that only wealthy scholars could afford to purchase
demonstrated complex scientific concepts using simple, everyday objects that anyone could understand
was the first time anyone in China had successfully explained what causes lunar eclipses

questions.stem: In the passage, the word 'baffled' most nearly means
options: entertained
frightened
angered
puzzled

questions.stem: Which statement best summarizes the relationship between Wang Zhenyi's travels and her scientific work?
options: Her journeys through five provinces exposed her to diverse perspectives that influenced how she approached scientific thinking.
She collected data about lunar eclipses from different regions to prove that Earth was round.
Her grandfather required her to travel before allowing her to study mathematics and astronomy.
She traveled primarily to find rare books that would help her write her thirteen scientific texts.
questions.explanation: The passage states her travels 'exposed her to different landscapes, peoples, and ideas that would shape her scientific thinking,' connecting journeys to intellectual development.

articles.topic_title: The Edenton Tea Party: women who boycotted British tea
articles.title: When Women's Voices Shook an Empire
articles.passage_html: Fifty-one women gathered in Elizabeth King's home on October 25, 1774, their skirts rustling as they pressed into the parlor. Outside the windows of the Edenton, North Carolina residence, autumn leaves drifted to the ground. Inside, Penelope Barker held a document that would cross the Atlantic Ocean and shake the confidence of British leaders. Barker, a wealthy 51-year-old plantation owner, had organized something unprecedented in the American colonies: a formal political action led entirely by women. The petition she presented was clear and defiant. The women pledged to boycott British tea and cloth, supporting resolutions made by North Carolina's First Provincial Congress in New Bern two months earlier. One by one, all 51 women signed their names, creating an official record of their allegiance to the colonial resistance movement. Unlike the Boston Tea Party of 1773, where protesters dumped tea into the harbor under cover of darkness, the Edenton women's action was deliberate and documented. They wanted their names known. Their petition traveled across the ocean and appeared in the Morning Chronicle and London Advertiser on January 16, 1775. British readers were stunned. Women involving themselves in politics? The idea seemed audacious to a society that relegated women to household duties alone. British newspapers responded with mockery. A political cartoon published in London in March 1775 depicted the Edenton women as neglectful mothers, abandoning their children to discuss politics. The ridicule revealed British anxiety: if even colonial women opposed British rule, the empire's authority was crumbling. The Edenton Tea Party demonstrated that resistance to British control had spread beyond angry merchants and political leaders. When women who managed households and plantations refused to purchase British goods, they struck at the empire's economic foundation. Their signed petition proved that courage and political conviction could not be restricted by gender, and their example inspired colonists throughout America to recognize that independence required everyone's participation.

The <vocab>ridicule|mocking laughter or criticism</vocab> revealed British anxiety: if even colonial women opposed British rule, the empire's authority was crumbling.

The Edenton Tea Party demonstrated that resistance to British control had spread beyond angry merchants and political leaders. When women who managed households and plantations refused to purchase British goods, they struck at the empire's economic foundation. Their signed petition proved that courage and political conviction could not be restricted by gender, and their example inspired colonists throughout America to recognize that independence required everyone's participation.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: The Edenton Tea Party was a significant act of political resistance by women that challenged both British authority and traditional gender roles.
Penelope Barker was a wealthy woman who organized a meeting in North Carolina in 1774.
British newspapers published articles and cartoons about colonial women who refused to buy tea.
The Edenton Tea Party was different from the Boston Tea Party because it involved a signed document.
questions.explanation: The passage focuses on how the women's organized boycott was both politically significant and challenged expectations about women's roles, which is captured in option A. The other options mention details but miss the central idea about the event's broader importance.

questions.stem: The author mentions the British political cartoon in order to...
options: show that the Edenton women became famous throughout Europe for their political actions.
illustrate that British leaders felt threatened by the spread of colonial resistance to all segments of society.
prove that British newspapers were more interested in American politics than colonial newspapers were.
explain why the Edenton Tea Party was more effective than the Boston Tea Party.
questions.explanation: The passage states the cartoon's ridicule 'revealed British anxiety' and that 'if even colonial women opposed British rule, the empire's authority was crumbling,' showing British concern about widespread resistance. Option A is partially true but overstates the scope, option C rearranges passage ideas incorrectly, and option D is not supported.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that the Edenton women chose to sign a formal petition rather than take anonymous action because they...
options: wanted to prove they were more civilized than the Boston Tea Party participants.
hoped to receive recognition from the First Provincial Congress in New Bern.
believed their open identification would demonstrate the legitimacy and seriousness of their political commitment.
needed to create legal documentation to protect themselves from British prosecution.
questions.explanation: The passage emphasizes that unlike the secret Boston Tea Party, the Edenton action 'was deliberate and documented' and 'They wanted their names known,' suggesting they sought to establish their action as legitimate and serious. The other options introduce ideas not suggested in the passage.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "relegated" most nearly means...
options: promoted
assigned
transported
confined
questions.explanation: In context, 'relegated women to household duties' means women were restricted or confined to those roles. While 'assigned' is close, 'confined' better captures the limiting nature described. The passage discusses how British society restricted women's roles, making 'confined' the most accurate synonym.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the Edenton women can best be described as...
options: admiring
skeptical
neutral
cautionary
questions.explanation: The author uses positive language like 'courage,' describes their action as striking 'at the empire's economic foundation,' and concludes that they proved 'courage and political conviction could not be restricted by gender,' revealing an admiring tone rather than a neutral, skeptical, or cautionary one.

articles.topic_title: Apollo 13: Failure is not an option
articles.title: Apollo 13: Mission in the Dark
articles.passage_html: A dull bang echoed through the spacecraft. Warning lights flashed across the control panel like angry fireflies. Jack Swigert's voice crackled through the static: "Houston, we've had a problem." It was April 13, 1970, and Apollo 13 sat 200,000 miles from Earth—a metal capsule drifting through the black void of space with three astronauts whose oxygen was leaking into the darkness. Two days earlier, on April 11, astronauts Jim Lovell, Jack Swigert, and Fred Haise had launched from Kennedy Space Center in Florida at 2:13 PM EST. Their destination: the moon. Their reality, 56 hours into the mission: an exploded oxygen tank in the service module that had transformed their spacecraft into a crippled tin can. Oxygen, electricity, light, water—all vanishing. The command module Odyssey was dying. The crew scrambled into the lunar module Aquarius, designed to hold two astronauts for two days, not three men for four. In Houston, Texas, Flight Director Gene Kranz gathered his team. They faced an impossible puzzle: how to bring three men home using systems never designed for this purpose. The astronauts manually fired the lunar module's descent engine—a precarious maneuver that adjusted their trajectory back toward Earth. Inside Aquarius, temperatures plummeted to 38 degrees Fahrenheit. The men watched their breath form icy clouds in the thin air. Condensation dripped from the metal walls like tears. Carbon dioxide presented another lethal threat. The lunar module's air filters couldn't handle three people breathing for days. Engineers at Mission Control designed a makeshift solution using plastic bags, cardboard, and tape. The astronauts followed instructions, building a square filter to fit a round hole. It worked. On April 17, 1970, after 5 days, 22 hours, and 54 minutes, Apollo 13 splashed into the Pacific Ocean. All three astronauts emerged alive. The mission never reached the moon, yet it proved something more formidable: human creativity doesn't require a perfect plan—just the refusal to quit when everything goes wrong.

The astronauts followed instructions, building a square filter to fit a round hole. It worked.

On April 17, 1970, after 5 days, 22 hours, and 54 minutes, Apollo 13 splashed into the Pacific Ocean. All three astronauts emerged alive. The mission never reached the moon, yet it proved something more <vocab>formidable|impressively powerful</vocab>: human creativity doesn't require a perfect plan—just the refusal to quit when everything goes wrong.

the curved path of an object through space

questions.stem: Which would be the BEST title for this passage?

The Journey to the Moon and Back
Why Space Exploration Is Dangerous
Gene Kranz and Mission Control

questions.stem: The author mentions the temperature inside Aquarius in order to...

illustrate the harsh conditions the crew endured
show how cold space is compared to Earth

questions.stem: The passage suggests that the lunar module Aquarius was...

equipped with unlimited oxygen supplies
used in a way its designers never intended
designed specifically as a backup lifeboat

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "makeshift" most nearly means...
options: professional
permanent
improvised
complicated

questions.stem: Which statement best summarizes the relationship between the Mission Control team and the astronauts?

The astronauts worked independently while Mission Control monitored their progress
Mission Control commanded the astronauts to follow predetermined emergency procedures

articles.topic_title: How dolphins see with sound
articles.title: Dolphins See the World Through Sound
articles.passage_html: A dolphin glides through murky ocean water where sunlight barely penetrates. The creature cannot see its prey hidden in the darkness, yet it swoops down with perfect accuracy and captures a fish buried 18 inches beneath the sandy ocean floor. How does the dolphin accomplish this remarkable feat? The answer lies in its ability to use sound as a sophisticated detection system. Dolphins navigate and hunt using echolocation, a biological sonar system that allows them to "see" their surroundings through sound waves. The process begins when a dolphin produces rapid clicking sounds at frequencies between 40,000 and 130,000 hertz—far beyond the 20,000 hertz upper limit of human hearing. These ultrasonic clicks originate deep within the dolphin's head and travel forward through a rounded, fatty organ called the melon. This specialized structure focuses the outgoing sound waves into a concentrated beam that travels through the water like an invisible flashlight. When the sound beam strikes an object—whether a fish, rock, or boat—it bounces back toward the dolphin. The returning echo travels through specialized fat channels in the dolphin's lower jaw, which conduct the sound waves to the inner ear. The dolphin's brain then analyzes these echoes to determine the object's location, size, shape, and even its composition. Scientists at the University of Hawaii's Kewalo Basin Marine Mammal Laboratory discovered that blindfolded dolphins could successfully identify different shapes and materials using only echolocation. The dolphins could determine whether an object was made of metal, plastic, or rubber by analyzing the quality of the returning echo in less than one second. The precision of this system is astonishing. A dolphin can detect a single steel ball bearing just 2.5 inches in diameter from 380 feet away in clear water. When closing in on prey or investigating an object closely, dolphins accelerate their clicking rate to an incredible 1,200 clicks per second, creating a nearly continuous stream of sound. This rapid-fire clicking allows them to build an increasingly detailed acoustic picture of their target. Researchers describe echolocation as giving dolphins a three-dimensional map of their environment that updates constantly as they swim. While humans rely primarily on vision to understand the world, dolphins have evolved an equally powerful sense that transforms sound into sight. This adaptation enables them to thrive in conditions where eyes alone would fail, proving that nature has many ways to solve the challenge of sensing the world.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: Dolphins use echolocation, a sophisticated sound-based system, to navigate and hunt by sending out sound waves and analyzing the returning echoes.
Dolphins can hear sounds at much higher frequencies than humans can, making them superior to other ocean animals.
Scientists at the University of Hawaii have conducted important research about how dolphins communicate with each other.
The melon is the most important organ in a dolphin's body because it helps the animal survive in the ocean.
questions.explanation: The passage focuses on explaining how dolphins use echolocation as a complete detection system, not just on their hearing ability, scientific research, or a single organ.

questions.stem: The author mentions the dolphin's lower jaw in order to
options: explain that dolphins have stronger jaws than most marine animals
describe how returning sound waves travel to the dolphin's inner ear
show that dolphins use their jaws to produce echolocation clicks
demonstrate that the jaw is more important than the melon for echolocation
questions.explanation: The passage states that the lower jaw contains specialized fat channels that conduct returning sound waves to the inner ear, explaining this step in the echolocation process.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that dolphins would have the MOST difficulty using echolocation to
options: locate prey hiding in underwater caves with many rock formations
find fish swimming in the open ocean during daylight hours
determine what material an object is made from at close range
distinguish between objects of similar size and shape made of identical materials
questions.explanation: Since dolphins identify objects by analyzing differences in returning echoes based on composition, size, and shape, objects that are similar in all these characteristics would produce nearly identical echoes and be hardest to distinguish.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "stream" most nearly means
options: a small river with flowing water
to move smoothly and continuously
a group of people moving together
a steady flow of something
questions.explanation: In context, "stream" describes the continuous flow of sound clicks the dolphin produces, making 'steady flow' the most accurate meaning in this passage.

questions.stem: The author's description of the blindfolded dolphin experiments emphasizes the
options: remarkable ability of dolphins to identify objects without using vision at all
superior intelligence of dolphins compared to other marine mammals
importance of the University of Hawaii in marine biology research
difficulty scientists face when trying to understand dolphin behavior
questions.explanation: The blindfolded experiments demonstrate that dolphins can successfully identify shapes and materials using echolocation alone, proving they don't need vision—this directly supports the passage's main point about echolocation as an alternative to sight.

articles.topic_title: The desert frogs that wait years for rain
articles.title: Buried Alive: The Frogs That Wait Underground
articles.passage_html: The Australian desert looks lifeless. Red sand stretches in every direction. No rain has fallen for three years. Yet six feet beneath the surface, a water-holding frog sits perfectly still inside a cocoon made from its own shed skin. Its heart beats once every few minutes. It breathes but does not move. It waits. This remarkable amphibian has perfected an almost magical survival trick. When drought strikes central Australia, water-holding frogs dig themselves underground using their powerful back legs. Then they shed layer after layer of skin, which hardens around their bodies like plastic wrap. This waterproof barrier prevents moisture from escaping. Inside this shelter, the frogs can survive for up to five years—no food, no water, barely breathing. Their bodies slow down to approximately one-twentieth of their normal speed. Indigenous Australians in the Western Desert understood this extraordinary ability. During severe droughts, they carefully dug up the buried frogs and gently squeezed them. Clean water stored in the frog's bladder and body tissues trickled out—enough to drink and stay alive. Halfway across the world in Arizona's Sonoran Desert, the spadefoot toad faces a similar challenge. It spends ten months of each year entombed, waiting for the summer monsoons between July and September. When rain finally pounds the desert floor, the transformation begins. This is the turning point these creatures have waited for all year. Spadefoot toads burst from the sand using specialized hard bumps on their hind feet, which work like shovels to dig upward at several inches per minute. Males begin calling immediately—a sound like a bleating sheep. Females arrive at temporary pools that have formed in depressions. Each female releases between 300 and 500 eggs. Now a race against time begins. The desert sun beats down. The shallow pools shrink daily through evaporation. Tadpoles must hatch, grow legs, lose their tails, and become froglets in as little as nine days. Any that develop too slowly will die when their pools dry up. The survivors dig down into the cooling sand, their bodies already preparing for another ten-month wait. These desert frogs teach us that life finds ways to persist in the harshest places. Patience, it turns out, can be a survival strategy—even if that patience lasts for years underground.

Yet six feet beneath the surface, a water-holding frog sits perfectly still inside a <vocab>cocoon|protective outer covering</vocab> made from its own shed skin. When drought strikes central Australia, water-holding frogs dig themselves underground using their powerful back legs. Then they shed layer after layer of skin, which hardens around their bodies like plastic wrap. This waterproof barrier prevents moisture from escaping. Inside this shelter, the frogs can survive for up to five years—no food, no water, barely breathing. Their bodies slow down to approximately one-twentieth of their normal speed.

Indigenous Australians in the Western Desert understood this extraordinary ability. During severe droughts, they carefully dug up the buried frogs and gently squeezed them. Clean water stored in the frog's bladder and body tissues trickled out—enough to drink and stay alive.

It spends ten months of each year <vocab>entombed|sealed underground</vocab>, waiting for the summer monsoons between July and September. When rain finally pounds the desert floor, the transformation begins. This is the turning point these creatures have waited for all year.

Each female releases between 300 and 500 eggs.

Now a race against time begins. The desert sun beats down. The shallow pools shrink daily through <vocab>evaporation|when liquid water turns into invisible water vapor</vocab>. The survivors dig down into the cooling sand, their bodies already preparing for another ten-month wait.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: Desert frogs survive extreme conditions by entering dormant states underground and racing to reproduce when rain finally arrives.
The water-holding frog can remain buried underground for up to five years without food or water.
Indigenous Australians used water-holding frogs as a source of drinking water during droughts.
Desert animals have developed many different adaptations to survive in hot, dry climates.
questions.explanation: The passage focuses on how both water-holding frogs and spadefoot toads survive through dormancy and rapid reproduction cycles. The other options are either too narrow (focusing on single details) or too broad (mentioning desert animals generally).

questions.stem: The author mentions Indigenous Australians in order to
options: explain how ancient peoples survived in the desert by finding underground water sources
demonstrate that humans have long understood the water-holding frog's survival abilities
show that water-holding frogs were more important than spadefoot toads
prove that desert frogs can survive being handled by humans
questions.explanation: The passage states Indigenous Australians 'understood this extraordinary ability' and used it during droughts, showing human awareness of the frog's capabilities. Option A is partially true but focuses on the wrong subject. Options C and D misrepresent the passage's purpose.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that spadefoot toad tadpoles that develop slowly
options: will grow into larger and healthier adult frogs than their faster siblings
have a better chance of surviving the harsh desert climate
are more likely to die before completing their transformation into froglets
will simply wait underground until the next rainy season arrives
questions.explanation: The passage states that 'any that develop too slowly will die when their pools dry up,' making option C correct. The other options contradict the passage's emphasis on the need for rapid development.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "entombed" most nearly means
options: decorated
protected
buried
frightened
questions.explanation: The passage uses 'entombed' to describe toads sealed underground, making 'buried' the best synonym. While 'protected' might seem plausible, 'entombed' specifically refers to being enclosed underground, not just sheltered.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward desert frogs can best be described as
options: admiring
skeptical
cautionary
critical
questions.explanation: The author uses phrases like 'remarkable,' 'extraordinary ability,' and 'almost magical survival trick,' showing admiration for these creatures. The passage celebrates rather than questions or criticizes the frogs' abilities.

articles.topic_title: The Donner Party: trapped in the Sierra Nevadas
articles.title: Frozen in the Mountains: The Donner Party Disaster
articles.passage_html: Snow pelted the canvas wagon covers as 87 travelers stared upward at the towering Sierra Nevada peaks in late October 1846. Brothers George and Jacob Donner, along with James F. Reed, had led these families westward toward California since May. Now, at nearly 6,000 feet elevation near what is now Donner Lake, their journey had reached a catastrophic halt. The problem began weeks earlier when the group chose the Hastings Cutoff, an untested shortcut that promised faster travel. Instead, the detour added three to four weeks to their journey—a delay that would prove deadly. By the time heavy snowstorms trapped them in the mountains, winter had seized the Sierra Nevadas in an iron grip. The pioneers constructed rough shelters at two camps: one beside Truckee Lake (now Donner Lake) and another six miles away at Alder Creek. Wind howled through gaps in the crude log cabins. Food supplies dwindled as snowdrifts climbed higher around their shelters, burying any hope of continuing forward or turning back. On December 16, 1846, seventeen desperate people nicknamed the "Forlorn Hope" strapped on handmade snowshoes and attempted to walk out of the frozen wilderness to find rescue. Their feet crunched through icy crust as they embarked on what would become a harrowing trek through the mountains. Months dragged by. Hunger gnawed at the survivors trapped in both camps. George Donner, the 62-year-old leader, suffered from an infected hand wound that refused to heal. Without proper medicine or nutrition, his condition deteriorated. He died in late March 1847, never seeing the California he'd promised his family. When the first rescue party finally reached the camps on February 19, 1847, they discovered emaciated survivors who had endured approximately four months of isolation. Of the original 87 members who had begun the journey with such optimism, only 48 people lived to continue westward. The Donner Party's tragedy reveals how a single decision—choosing an unproven shortcut—can transform an adventure into a fight for survival. Their story remains carved into California's history, a stark reminder that the path forward isn't always the fastest one.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: The Donner Party's decision to take an untested shortcut led to their entrapment in the Sierra Nevada mountains, resulting in a deadly winter ordeal.
The Donner Party consisted of 87 travelers who wanted to reach California by taking the fastest route possible.
George Donner was a brave leader who tried his best to save all the members of his wagon train during the winter.
Snowshoes were an important invention that helped the Forlorn Hope group escape from the mountains.
questions.explanation: The passage emphasizes how the choice of the Hastings Cutoff shortcut caused the fatal delay and subsequent trapping of the party, making this the central idea. The other options focus on supporting details rather than the main message.

questions.stem: The author mentions the two separate camps in order to
options: explain why the rescue party had difficulty finding all the survivors at once.
illustrate the scattered and vulnerable conditions the trapped pioneers faced.
compare the different survival strategies used at Truckee Lake versus Alder Creek.
show that the group had split up because of disagreements between the leaders.
questions.explanation: The passage describes the crude shelters at two locations six miles apart to emphasize the harsh, dispersed conditions. While option A might be true, it's not stated in the passage. Options C and D introduce details not mentioned in the text.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that the Hastings Cutoff was
options: a well-known route that had been used successfully by many previous wagon trains.
recommended by experienced mountain guides who knew the Sierra Nevada region.
a dangerous gamble that the Donner Party leaders took without sufficient information.
designed specifically to help travelers avoid the heavy winter snows in the mountains.
questions.explanation: The passage states the cutoff was 'untested' and added weeks to the journey, suggesting it was a risky choice made without adequate knowledge. The other options contradict information in the passage or introduce unsupported claims.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "deteriorated" most nearly means
options: improved gradually
remained the same
changed direction
became worse
questions.explanation: The passage describes George Donner's infected hand wound that 'refused to heal' and led to his death, indicating his condition worsened or deteriorated over time.

questions.stem: Which statement best summarizes the relationship between the timing of the Donner Party's arrival in the mountains and their survival?
options: The three to four week delay caused by the Hastings Cutoff meant the group arrived too late to cross before winter storms trapped them.
The Donner Party arrived in the mountains at the perfect time but made poor decisions about where to build their camps.
George Donner's injury in late March 1847 caused the group to arrive in the Sierra Nevadas later than they had planned.
The rescue party's delay until February meant that most members of the Donner Party did not survive the winter.
questions.explanation: The passage connects the delay from the shortcut with their late October arrival and subsequent trapping by winter storms. Option B contradicts the passage, C reverses cause and effect, and D misrepresents the survival rate (48 of 87 survived).

articles.topic_title: The Code of Hammurabi: world's first written laws
articles.title: When Stone Spoke: Hammurabi's Revolutionary Laws
articles.passage_html: The Babylonian stonemason stepped back from the black diorite pillar, his hands aching from months of carving. Standing 7 feet 4 inches tall, the stone gleamed in the Mesopotamian sun. At its top, he had chiseled an image of King Hammurabi receiving instructions from Shamash, the god of justice, who sat upon a throne. Below stretched 282 laws—each one carved in meticulous wedge-shaped cuneiform script. The year was approximately 1754 BCE, and the stonemason had just completed something the world had never seen: a comprehensive written law code. Before Hammurabi ruled the Babylonian Empire from 1792 to 1750 BCE, justice often depended on who you knew or how much you could pay. Disputes were settled by powerful individuals whose decisions could be arbitrary and unpredictable. Hammurabi's innovation changed everything. By displaying laws publicly on stone, he created a system where everyone—at least in theory—could know the rules. The laws themselves covered the minutiae of daily life in ancient Mesopotamia. They addressed trade agreements, property ownership, marriage, divorce, and wages for field laborers and craftsmen. Yet they also revealed the stark inequalities of Babylonian society. The code divided people into three rigid social classes: property owners at the top, freed persons in the middle, and slaves at the bottom. Punishments varied dramatically based on which group you belonged to. Some laws established the famous principle of "an eye for an eye"—proportional punishment that matched the crime. Law 196 declared: "If a man put out the eye of another man, his eye shall be put out." Law 229 held builders accountable for their work: if a house collapsed and killed its owner, the builder faced execution. These weren't gentle rules, but they were explicit—no guesswork required. The massive stone pillar stood in Babylon for over a thousand years before being carried off to Susa, in present-day Iran. French archaeologists rediscovered it there in 1901. Today, visitors to the Louvre Museum in Paris can stand before this ancient monument, reading laws that once governed an empire. Hammurabi's code wasn't perfect—it institutionalized inequality and prescribed harsh punishments. Yet it represented a revolutionary idea: that justice should be visible, consistent, and based on written rules rather than the whims of the powerful.

The Babylonian stonemason stepped back from the black diorite pillar, his hands aching from months of carving. Standing 7 feet 4 inches tall, the stone gleamed in the Mesopotamian sun. At its top, he had chiseled an image of King Hammurabi receiving instructions from Shamash, the god of justice, who sat upon a throne. Below stretched 282 laws—each one carved in <vocab>meticulous|extremely careful and precise</vocab> wedge-shaped cuneiform script. The year was approximately 1754 BCE, and the stonemason had just completed something the world had never seen: a comprehensive written law code.

Before Hammurabi ruled the Babylonian Empire from 1792 to 1750 BCE, justice often depended on who you knew or how much you could pay. Disputes were settled by powerful individuals whose decisions could be <vocab>arbitrary|based on random choice rather than reason</vocab> and unpredictable. Hammurabi's innovation changed everything. By displaying laws publicly on stone, he created a system where everyone—at least in theory—could know the rules.

The laws themselves covered the <vocab>minutiae|small, precise details</vocab> of daily life in ancient Mesopotamia. Yet they also revealed the <vocab>stark|very plain or obvious</vocab> inequalities of Babylonian society. Law 196 declared: "If a man put out the eye of another man, his eye shall be put out." Law 229 held builders <vocab>accountable|responsible for one's actions</vocab> for their work: if a house collapsed and killed its owner, the builder faced execution. These weren't gentle rules, but they were <vocab>explicit|stated clearly and directly</vocab>—no guesswork required.

The massive stone pillar stood in Babylon for over a thousand years before being carried off to Susa, in present-day Iran. French archaeologists rediscovered it there in 1901. Today, visitors to the Louvre Museum in Paris can stand before this ancient monument, reading laws that once governed an empire. Hammurabi's code wasn't perfect—it institutionalized inequality and prescribed harsh punishments. Yet it represented a revolutionary idea: that justice should be visible, consistent, and based on written rules rather than the whims of the powerful.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: Hammurabi's code was revolutionary because it created a public, written system of laws that made justice more predictable than before.
The Code of Hammurabi was carved on a black diorite pillar that stands in the Louvre Museum today.
Ancient Babylonian society was divided into three social classes with different punishments for each group.
Hammurabi ruled the Babylonian Empire from 1792 to 1750 BCE and created 282 laws.
questions.explanation: The passage focuses on how Hammurabi's written law code transformed justice from arbitrary decisions to a predictable, public system—this is the turning point and main theme throughout.

questions.stem: The author mentions the stonemason in the opening paragraph in order to
options: explain how cuneiform writing was invented in ancient Mesopotamia
emphasize the physical effort and craftsmanship required to create the law code
show that builders in ancient Babylon faced harsh punishments for their mistakes
prove that King Hammurabi personally carved the laws into stone
questions.explanation: The description of the stonemason's aching hands and months of work emphasizes the meticulous craftsmanship and effort behind creating this monument, making the achievement more concrete and impressive.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that before Hammurabi's code,
options: there were no laws at all in Mesopotamian society
wealthy and powerful people had more influence over legal outcomes
all crimes were punished by the principle of "an eye for an eye"
builders were never held responsible when their houses collapsed
questions.explanation: The passage states that before the code, justice 'depended on who you knew or how much you could pay' and that decisions were 'arbitrary and unpredictable,' suggesting the wealthy and powerful had more influence.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "explicit" most nearly means
options: violent or graphic
ancient or outdated
fair and equal
clear and definite
questions.explanation: The passage uses 'explicit' followed by 'no guesswork required,' indicating the laws were clear and definite in their meaning, leaving nothing ambiguous.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward Hammurabi's code can best be described as
options: appreciative of its historical importance despite its flaws
critical of its harsh and unequal treatment of people
enthusiastic about its perfect system of justice
skeptical that it actually improved Babylonian society
questions.explanation: The author acknowledges the code's problems (inequality, harsh punishments) but emphasizes its 'revolutionary' nature and importance in making justice more predictable and visible—showing appreciation despite recognizing flaws.

articles.topic_title: The night Orson Welles' radio play made America panic
articles.title: The Night Martians Invaded America's Radios
articles.passage_html: Police switchboards lit up like Christmas trees across New Jersey. Frantic voices flooded telephone lines, demanding to know where the poisonous gas clouds were spreading. Outside, families clutched wet towels over their faces and rushed into the streets. Yet no alien spacecraft hovered overhead. No Martians stalked through the darkness. The invasion existed only in the crackling voices pouring from living room radios on October 30, 1938. At 8:00 PM Eastern Time that Sunday evening, a twenty-three-year-old director named Orson Welles launched his latest production on the Columbia Broadcasting System. His Mercury Theatre on the Air presented a radio adaptation of H.G. Wells' science fiction novel "The War of the Worlds." But Welles transformed the story into something far more convincing than ordinary drama. Instead of traditional storytelling, the show mimicked news bulletins interrupting a music program. The simulated broadcasts reported that Martians had landed in Grover's Mill, New Jersey, wielding heat rays and poisonous gas against terrified Americans. Actors portrayed reporters, scientists, and government officials delivering increasingly dire updates. The performances sounded so authentic that listeners who tuned in late—missing the opening announcement about the fictional drama—believed they were hearing actual news coverage. Approximately six million people listened that evening. According to a Princeton University professor named Hadley Cantril, who studied the broadcast's impact, roughly 1.2 million believed the Martian invasion was real. Newspapers reported thousands fleeing their homes. The program included three announcements clarifying its fictional nature: one at the beginning, another forty minutes into the show, and a final one at the conclusion. Many listeners, however, never heard these warnings. The following day, October 31, Welles faced reporters at a press conference. The young director expressed astonishment at the panic and apologized to frightened listeners. His innovative broadcast technique had revealed something unsettling about modern communication: when information arrives through trusted technology, people may accept it without question. That Halloween eve, America learned that the most terrifying invasions sometimes begin not with spaceships, but with voices we believe we can trust.

Police switchboards lit up like Christmas trees across New Jersey. Frantic voices flooded telephone lines, demanding to know where the poisonous gas clouds were spreading. Outside, families clutched wet towels over their faces and rushed into the streets. Yet no alien spacecraft hovered overhead. No Martians stalked through the darkness. The invasion existed only in the crackling voices pouring from living room radios on October 30, 1938.

At 8:00 PM Eastern Time that Sunday evening, a twenty-three-year-old director named Orson Welles launched his latest production on the Columbia Broadcasting System. His Mercury Theatre on the Air presented a radio <vocab>adaptation|version changed to fit a different form</vocab> of H.G. Wells' science fiction novel "The War of the Worlds." But Welles transformed the story into something far more <vocab>convincing|believable or persuasive</vocab> than ordinary drama. Instead of traditional storytelling, the show mimicked news bulletins interrupting a music program.

The <vocab>simulated|imitated or pretended</vocab> broadcasts reported that Martians had landed in Grover's Mill, New Jersey, wielding heat rays and poisonous gas against terrified Americans. Actors portrayed reporters, scientists, and government officials delivering increasingly dire updates. The performances sounded so <vocab>authentic|real or genuine</vocab> that listeners who tuned in late—missing the opening announcement about the fictional drama—believed they were hearing actual news coverage.

Approximately six million people listened that evening. According to a Princeton University professor named Hadley Cantril, who studied the broadcast's impact, roughly 1.2 million believed the Martian invasion was real. Newspapers reported thousands fleeing their homes. The program included three announcements clarifying its fictional nature: one at the beginning, another forty minutes into the show, and a final one at the conclusion. Many listeners, however, never heard these warnings.

The following day, October 31, Welles faced reporters at a press conference. The young director expressed <vocab>astonishment|great surprise</vocab> at the panic and apologized to frightened listeners. His <vocab>innovative|new and creative</vocab> broadcast technique had revealed something unsettling about modern communication: when information arrives through trusted technology, people may accept it without question. That Halloween eve, America learned that the most terrifying invasions sometimes begin not with spaceships, but with voices we believe we can trust.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: Orson Welles' realistic radio drama in 1938 caused widespread panic because many listeners believed a fictional Martian invasion was real news.
Radio broadcasts in the 1930s were the primary source of entertainment and news for American families.
H.G. Wells wrote a science fiction novel about Martians that became popular in America during the 1930s.

questions.explanation: The passage focuses on how Welles' realistic broadcast format caused listeners to believe a fictional invasion was real, leading to panic. The other options mention true facts but miss the main point about the broadcast's impact.

listened to the entire program from beginning to end without interruption
tuned in after the program started and missed the opening announcement
were unfamiliar with Orson Welles' previous work on the Mercury Theatre

ancient
genuine
loud

questions.stem: Which statement best summarizes the relationship between the broadcast's format and the public's reaction?
options: The simulated news bulletin format made the fictional story seem like real news coverage, causing many to believe an actual invasion was occurring.
The use of famous actors in the broadcast convinced audiences that the government had approved the emergency warnings.
The length of the broadcast prevented listeners from hearing all three announcements about its fictional nature.
