articles.topic_title: Katherine Johnson: the math that sent astronauts to the moon
articles.title: Katherine Johnson: The Mind Behind the Mission
articles.passage_html: John Glenn sat strapped into his spacecraft, ready to become the first American to orbit Earth. But before Friendship 7 could launch in February 1962, Glenn made an unusual demand. Despite having access to NASA's most advanced electronic computers, he insisted that "the girl" check the numbers first. That girl was Katherine Johnson, a mathematician whose calculations would determine whether Glenn lived or died in space. Katherine Johnson deserves recognition as one of America's most influential scientists because her mathematical work made human space exploration possible. Without her precise calculations, astronauts could not have safely traveled beyond Earth's atmosphere and returned home. First, Johnson's work was indispensable for the earliest American space missions. In 1961, she calculated the trajectory for Alan Shepard's Freedom 7 mission, which launched him as the first American into space on May 5. When computers produced the flight path for Glenn's orbital mission the following year, NASA engineers trusted the machines—but Glenn did not. He specifically requested that Johnson personally verify every calculation by hand. Her verification gave Glenn the confidence to climb aboard, and his successful three-orbit flight transformed America's space program. Second, Johnson's mathematical expertise reached its peak during the Apollo 11 mission. She calculated the precise launch window and trajectory that successfully landed Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin on the moon on July 20, 1969. These calculations required extraordinary accuracy: even a tiny error would have sent the spacecraft millions of miles off course or stranded the astronauts in space. Johnson's equations accounted for the Earth's rotation, the moon's movement, and countless other variables. Born in White Sulphur Springs, West Virginia, on August 26, 1918, Johnson demonstrated exceptional mathematical ability from early childhood. She joined NASA's predecessor organization, NACA, in 1953 at the Langley Research Center in Hampton, Virginia. During her 33-year career before retiring in 1986, she authored or co-authored 26 research reports. In 2015, President Barack Obama awarded her the Presidential Medal of Freedom, the nation's highest civilian honor. Johnson's story, featured in the 2016 film "Hidden Figures," reveals a profound truth: behind every monumental achievement in space exploration stood a mathematician with a pencil, a slide rule, and an extraordinary mind. Her legacy reminds us that reaching the stars required more than rockets and courage—it demanded absolute precision in mathematics.

John Glenn refused to fly into space until Katherine Johnson checked the computer calculations for his mission.

questions.stem: Which statement best summarizes the relationship between Johnson's calculations and the success of space missions?
options: Her mathematical precision enabled astronauts to launch, navigate, and return safely from space.
Her work proved that human mathematicians were superior to electronic computers in all situations.

questions.explanation: The passage describes Johnson's work across multiple missions (Shepard, Glenn, Apollo 11) as enabling safe space travel through precise calculations. Option B overstates the human-computer comparison; Option C is too narrow; Option D introduces an unsupported topic.

articles.topic_title: The Terracotta Army: 8,000 clay soldiers, 2,000 years
articles.title: The Army That Slept for 2,000 Years
articles.passage_html: The shovel struck something hard. In March 1974, farmers near Xi'an, China, were digging a well when they unearthed fragments of baked clay—a head here, an arm there. They had no idea they'd just discovered one of the greatest archaeological finds in history: an underground army of 8,000 life-sized clay soldiers that had stood guard in darkness for more than two millennia. This vast clay force was created to protect Qin Shi Huang, who became China's first emperor in 221 BCE. When he died in 210 BCE, he was buried with an army unlike any other. Approximately 700,000 workers spent 38 years constructing his tomb complex, which included three main pits covering about 20,000 square meters—roughly the size of three football fields. Inside stood thousands of terracotta warriors, each weighing 400 precise pounds and standing between 5.7 and 6.5 feet tall. What makes these soldiers truly remarkable is their individuality. Each warrior possesses unique facial features and hairstyles, as if the artists had sculpted an actual army rather than mass-produced copies. They were arranged in battle formation facing east, armed with real bronze weapons: swords, spears, and crossbows. These bronze blades remained sharp after spending more than 2,000 years underground—a testament to ancient Chinese craftsmanship. The warriors once dazzled in brilliant colors. Originally painted in red, green, blue, and purple pigments, they must have looked like a vivid parade frozen in time. Tragically, when archaeologists excavated the figures, exposure to air caused most of the paint to flake away within minutes. The army that once gleamed now stands in shades of gray and brown. Today, the 130 chariots pulled by 520 horses and 150 cavalry horses remain partially concealed in the earth. Emperor Qin Shi Huang's actual tomb lies beneath a pyramid-shaped mound 1.5 kilometers away, still unexcavated. What secrets might it hold? The clay army reminds us that even emperors cannot command time—but through art, they can whisper across centuries.

options: introducing a discovery, then explaining what was found and its significance
comparing ancient Chinese warriors with their clay representations

articles.topic_title: The Wright Brothers' 12-second flight
articles.title: Twelve Seconds That Changed the World
articles.passage_html: The frigid wind whipped across the sand dunes at Kill Devil Hills, North Carolina, on the morning of December 17, 1903. Orville Wright lay flat on the lower wing of a strange contraption made of spruce wood and muslin fabric, his hands gripping the controls. Five men from a nearby lifesaving station—John T. Daniels, Adam Etheridge, Will Dough, W.C. Brinkley, and Johnny Moore—huddled against the cold, watching skeptically. The Wright brothers had spent years studying flight, building gliders, and testing wings in the wind tunnel they constructed in their Dayton, Ohio bicycle shop. They had chosen this desolate North Carolina beach because the U.S. Weather Bureau reported consistent winds averaging 13 miles per hour—perfect for their experiments. Three days earlier, Wilbur had won a coin toss for the first attempt but crashed after just 3.5 seconds. Now it was Orville's turn. The engine, which Orville and Wilbur had designed with their mechanic Charlie Taylor, rattled to life. Its 12 horsepower seemed puny for such an ambitious goal. The entire aircraft, called the Wright Flyer, weighed just 605 pounds without a pilot and stretched 40 feet 4 inches from wingtip to wingtip. Could this fragile machine actually fly? At 10:35 a.m., the Flyer lurched forward along its wooden rail. The wings caught the air. Suddenly, impossibly, the machine lifted off the ground. For 12 seconds, Orville soared through the air, covering 120 feet—a distance shorter than the wingspan of a modern Boeing 747. When the Flyer touched down, those five witnesses had seen something no human had witnessed before: controlled, powered flight. The brothers made three more flights that day, with Wilbur piloting the longest one: 852 feet in 59 seconds. Yet newspapers largely ignored the achievement. The world didn't immediately grasp what those 12 seconds meant. The Wright brothers had solved a puzzle that had frustrated inventors for centuries, proving that humans could master the sky. That brief flight over a North Carolina beach would eventually shrink our planet, connecting distant continents in hours rather than weeks.

considered dangerous by the five witnesses who watched

requiring determination to accomplish

options: admiring
skeptical
cautionary
neutral
questions.explanation: The author uses positive language like 'impossibly,' describes the achievement as solving 'a puzzle that had frustrated inventors for centuries,' and emphasizes its world-changing significance, showing clear admiration rather than skepticism, caution, or neutrality.

articles.topic_title: Boyan Slat: the Dutch teen who designed an ocean cleanup machine
articles.title: The Teenage Inventor Cleaning the Ocean
articles.passage_html: A floating barrier stretches across the Pacific Ocean like a giant orange snake, its curved arms extending 600 meters across the waves. Below the surface, a three-meter skirt hangs in the water, gently corralling plastic debris while allowing fish and other sea creatures to swim safely beneath. This is System 001, the revolutionary machine that began as a frustrated teenager's dream. In 2011, sixteen-year-old Boyan Slat surfaced from a diving trip near Greece and pulled off his mask in disgust. The water around him contained more plastic bags than fish. The problem gnawed at him during his flight home to Delft, Netherlands. Most people would have shrugged and forgotten. Slat grabbed a notebook and began sketching. By October 2012, the eighteen-year-old stood on a TEDx stage in his hometown, explaining his audacious plan: a passive collection system that would use ocean currents to trap floating plastic. His words rippled across the internet. The video attracted more than 2 million views, and suddenly the world was watching. In 2013, Slat founded The Ocean Cleanup, a non-profit organization dedicated to turning his drawings into reality. The target was formidable: the Great Pacific Garbage Patch, a swirling mass of approximately 1.8 trillion plastic pieces covering an area twice the size of Texas, floating between Hawaii and California. In 2014, a crowdfunding campaign raised $2.2 million from 38,000 people across 160 countries. That same year, Slat received the United Nations' Champion of the Earth award at age twenty, becoming one of the youngest people ever honored. September 2018 brought the first test. System 001 deployed into the Pacific, its boom bobbing across the waves. Initial attempts failed—the plastic escaped, and equipment broke. But in October 2019, an improved version successfully snagged plastic from the garbage patch. The barrier had worked. Today, Slat's invention continues extracting tons of ocean plastic, proving that one teenager's frustration beneath the Greek waves could transform into a solution spanning thousands of miles. Sometimes the biggest changes begin with someone refusing to look away.

prove that young people give better presentations than adults
describe the first time anyone proposed cleaning the ocean
questions.explanation: The passage states the TEDx video went viral with 2+ million views and 'suddenly the world was watching,' demonstrating how the talk generated attention that led to support for his project.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that Slat's early attempts with System 001
options: worked perfectly on the first deployment in 2018
cost more money than the crowdfunding campaign had raised
required adjustments before achieving successful plastic collection
collected plastic from every ocean around the world
questions.explanation: The passage states that initial attempts failed with plastic escaping and equipment breaking, but an 'improved version' succeeded in 2019, indicating adjustments were necessary.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word 'formidable' most nearly means
options: impossible to overcome
frightening or scary
impressively challenging
completely mysterious
questions.explanation: The passage uses 'formidable' to describe the massive size of the garbage patch (1.8 trillion pieces, twice the size of Texas), indicating something impressively large and challenging rather than impossible or scary.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward Boyan Slat can best be described as
options: admiring
skeptical
neutral
cautionary
questions.explanation: The author uses positive descriptive language like 'revolutionary,' highlights Slat's achievements and persistence, and ends with an inspirational conclusion, demonstrating an admiring tone throughout the passage.

articles.topic_title: How elephants mourn their dead
articles.title: When Giants Grieve: How Elephants Mourn
articles.passage_html: In 2003, a female elephant named Eleanor collapsed on the red earth of Samburu National Reserve in Kenya. Dust swirled around her massive body as another elephant, Grace, rushed to her side. Grace wedged her curved tusks beneath Eleanor's motionless form and strained to lift her friend upright. When Eleanor could not rise, Grace remained beside the body, refusing to leave. This moment, witnessed by researcher Iain Douglas-Hamilton, revealed behavior that scientists have now documented across Africa and Asia: elephants mourn their dead. At Amboseli National Park in Kenya, researchers observed elephants spending hours examining the skull and ivory of a deceased matriarch. The elephants ran their trunks along every curve and hollow of the bones, as if reading a story written in calcium. When elephant researcher Cynthia Moss studied another family group at Amboseli, she recorded that the herd stood vigil over a dead family member for three days before finally departing. The elephant brain holds clues to this profound emotional response. Elephants possess a temporal lobe proportionally larger than in humans. This brain region processes memory and emotion in all mammals, suggesting elephants may experience grief with extraordinary intensity. Dr. Joyce Poole, who has studied African elephants for more than forty years, documented that elephants produce a low-frequency rumble at 14-24 hertz when encountering deceased herd members—nearly identical to the sounds they make when greeting living relatives. Some elephants refuse to abandon their dead, even when survival demands movement. In 2016, researchers in India watched elephants carry a deceased calf's body for several days, transporting it to new feeding areas rather than leaving it behind. In Myanmar in 2013, timber camp workers observed a female elephant dig a hole with her trunk and feet, place her dead calf inside, cover the body with soil, and stand beside the grave for hours. Perhaps most compelling, scientists at Samburu Reserve recorded elephants returning to death sites years later, touching the remaining bones with their trunks in what appeared to be acts of remembrance. These gentle giants carry their losses across years and miles, suggesting that grief may not belong to humans alone.

vocab.definition: female leader of a family group

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: Elephants display complex mourning behaviors that suggest they experience grief similar to humans.
Grace the elephant tried to help Eleanor stand up when she collapsed in Kenya in 2003.
Elephants have larger temporal lobes than humans, which helps them remember things better.
Scientists have been studying elephant behavior in Africa and Asia for many decades.
questions.explanation: The passage uses multiple examples and scientific evidence to support the main idea that elephants mourn their dead in ways that suggest deep emotional experiences. The other options are supporting details or partial truths that don't capture the overall purpose.

questions.stem: The author mentions the elephant's temporal lobe in order to
options: explain why elephants are more intelligent than most other animals
provide a biological reason for elephants' intense emotional responses
show that elephants can remember events from many years earlier
prove that elephants experience exactly the same emotions as humans
questions.explanation: The passage states the temporal lobe is 'proportionally larger than in humans' and 'processes memory and emotion,' suggesting this explains their 'profound emotional response.' Option A is not stated, option C is partially true but not the reason mentioned, and option D contradicts the text's careful language.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that elephants who return to death sites years later
options: are searching for food in areas where they used to live with deceased relatives
want to protect the bones from being taken by other animals or humans
continue to feel emotional connections to elephants who died long ago
are teaching younger elephants where previous generations were buried
questions.explanation: The passage describes elephants returning years later to touch bones 'in what appeared to be acts of remembrance,' suggesting ongoing emotional bonds. The other options introduce plausible but unsupported explanations for this behavior.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "vigil" most nearly means
options: a celebration of someone's life
a loud cry of sadness
a careful search for something lost
a period of watchful attention
questions.explanation: The passage describes elephants standing 'vigil over a deceased family member's body for three days,' indicating they remained watching over it. This matches 'watchful attention' rather than celebration, crying, or searching.

questions.stem: Which best describes how the passage is organized?
options: It opens with a specific example, then provides additional evidence to support a broader claim about elephant behavior.
It compares elephant mourning rituals in Africa with those observed in Asia to show regional differences.
It presents a scientific mystery about elephant behavior, then explains how researchers finally solved it.
It describes events in chronological order from the earliest documented elephant mourning to the most recent.
questions.explanation: The passage begins with Eleanor and Grace's story (specific example), then presents multiple examples and scientific evidence supporting the claim that elephants mourn. It does not compare regions, solve a mystery, or follow strict chronological order.

articles.topic_title: Coral reef restoration: growing new reefs from fragments
articles.title: Growing Hope: A New Way to Save Reefs
articles.passage_html: Dr. David Vaughan stared at the scattered coral pieces across his laboratory table at the Mote Marine Laboratory in Florida. He had accidentally dropped a tray of coral samples. Frustrated, he almost swept them into the trash. Then he noticed something peculiar: the tiny broken pieces were growing faster than the larger corals in his tanks. That accident in 2010 transformed coral restoration forever. Dr. Vaughan had discovered microfragmentation, a method that allows corals normally requiring 25 to 75 years to reach maturity to achieve that size in just 3 years. By breaking corals into small pieces, he triggered their natural healing response, causing them to grow at accelerated rates. A single fragment the size of a human hand could expand to dinner-plate size within 6 to 12 months. Coral gardening wasn't entirely new. Dr. Austin Bowden-Kerby had pioneered the technique during the 1970s in the Florida Keys, creating underwater nurseries where coral fragments could mature before being transplanted to damaged reefs. These nurseries function like tree farms, but instead of soil and sunshine, they use ocean currents and sunlight filtered through clear tropical waters. Scientists attach fragments to PVC structures using marine epoxy or plastic cable ties, watching them flourish as they sway in the gentle waves. Today, restoration projects thrive across multiple oceans. The Coral Restoration Foundation, established in 2007 in Key Largo, Florida, has planted more than 230,000 corals onto Florida's Coral Reef. In the Caribbean, the Reef Renewal Foundation Bonaire has grown more than 50,000 coral fragments since 2012. Scientists favor elkhorn and staghorn corals for these projects because both species grow quickly—4 to 8 inches per year—creating substantial structures that shelter fish and other marine life. The fragments bond to reef surfaces within 6 to 8 weeks, their limestone skeletons cementing into place. In 2019, scientists at the Hawaii Institute of Marine Biology successfully restored approximately 10,000 square feet of reef in Kaneohe Bay after a ship grounding had devastated the area. Where ruined reef once lay, colorful coral colonies now pulse with returning fish. These underwater gardens prove that with patience and science, humans can help heal what they have harmed.

Dr. Austin Bowden-Kerby had pioneered the technique during the 1970s in the Florida Keys, creating underwater nurseries where coral fragments could mature before being transplanted to damaged reefs. These nurseries function like tree farms, but instead of soil and sunshine, they use ocean currents and sunlight filtered through clear tropical waters. Scientists attach fragments to PVC structures using marine epoxy or plastic cable ties, watching them <vocab>flourish|grow vigorously and successfully</vocab> as they sway in the gentle waves.

questions.stem: Which would be the BEST title for this passage?
options: From Accident to Solution: How Scientists Restore Coral Reefs
Dr. David Vaughan: The Most Important Marine Biologist
Why Coral Reefs Are Dying Around the World
The Complete History of Coral Research in Florida
questions.explanation: The passage focuses on coral restoration methods, beginning with Vaughan's accidental discovery and describing various restoration techniques and projects. The other options are either too narrow, not addressed in the passage, or inaccurate.

questions.stem: The author mentions elkhorn and staghorn corals in order to...
options: explain why these species are endangered in the Caribbean
show which coral species work best for restoration projects
compare growth rates of different types of marine life
describe the most colorful corals found in tropical waters
questions.explanation: The passage states scientists favor these species because they grow quickly at 4 to 8 inches per year, making them ideal for restoration. The other options contain information not stated in the passage.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that Dr. Vaughan's discovery was important because it...
options: allowed scientists to create the first coral nurseries in history
proved that coral could survive in laboratory environments
dramatically reduced the time needed to grow mature corals
showed that all broken corals will automatically grow faster
questions.explanation: The passage states microfragmentation allows corals to mature in 3 years instead of 25 to 75 years. Option A is incorrect because coral nurseries existed before Vaughan. Options B and D make claims not supported by the passage.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word "substantial" most nearly means...
options: expensive
heavy
colorful
sizeable
questions.explanation: In context, 'substantial structures' refers to large coral formations that provide shelter. 'Sizeable' best captures this meaning of large and solid.

questions.stem: Which best describes how the passage is organized?
options: presenting a discovery, then explaining how it led to restoration programs
comparing coral restoration methods in different countries
describing a problem with coral reefs, then listing possible solutions
explaining the life cycle of coral from fragment to maturity
questions.explanation: The passage opens with Vaughan's accidental discovery of microfragmentation, then describes how this and other techniques are used in restoration projects worldwide. The passage focuses on a discovery leading to solutions, not on comparisons, general problems, or coral life cycles.

articles.topic_title: The Long March: 6,000 miles that changed China
articles.title: The Long March: China's 6,000-Mile Journey
articles.passage_html: Chain links rattled in the wind as Communist soldiers crept toward the Luding Bridge in May 1935. Below them, the Dadu River churned 100 feet down, its rushing waters threatening to swallow anyone who fell. Wooden planks had been removed from the bridge, leaving only bare chains stretching across the gorge. Yet these exhausted troops had no choice but to cross—Nationalist forces were closing in from behind. This perilous river crossing marked just one obstacle in an extraordinary military retreat known as the Long March. In October 1934, approximately 86,000 Communist soldiers and officials fled Jiangxi Province in southeastern China to escape Nationalist troops led by Chiang Kai-shek. What followed was a grueling 6,000-mile journey across 11 provinces that would take 368 days and reshape China's future. The marchers faced relentless hardships. They crossed 18 mountain ranges and 24 rivers, battling both nature and enemy forces. In the treacherous Jiajin Mountains, troops trudged through peaks exceeding 16,000 feet, where thin air made every breath a struggle and freezing temperatures claimed countless lives. The journey's brutal conditions took a devastating toll: of the 86,000 who began the march, only about 8,000 survived to reach Shaanxi Province in northern China by October 1935. Yet the Long March proved to be more than a military retreat—it became a political turning point. During the Zunyi Conference in January 1935, held partway through the march, Mao Zedong emerged as the undisputed leader of the Chinese Communist Party. His strategic thinking and resilience during the march earned him followers' loyalty. After reaching Yan'an in Shaanxi Province, Mao and his survivors established a new base. They rebuilt their forces over the next 14 years, eventually defeating the Nationalists in 1949 to establish the People's Republic of China. The Long March had transformed a desperate escape into a legendary story of survival—one that would inspire a revolution and change the course of the world's most populous nation.

questions.stem: Which sentence best states the central idea of the passage?
options: The Long March was a difficult 6,000-mile retreat that allowed the Communist Party to survive, regroup, and eventually take control of China.
Mao Zedong became the leader of the Chinese Communist Party during the Zunyi Conference in January 1935.
The Luding Bridge crossing demonstrated the bravery of Communist soldiers during their retreat from Nationalist forces.
Communist forces faced many natural obstacles including mountains, rivers, and extreme weather during their journey.
questions.explanation: The passage emphasizes both the hardships of the march and its ultimate significance in allowing the Communists to survive and eventually win control of China, making this the central idea rather than individual events or obstacles.

questions.stem: The author mentions the Jiajin Mountains in order to
options: describe the route the marchers followed from south to north across China.
illustrate one of the severe natural challenges that caused casualties during the Long March.
explain why Mao Zedong emerged as the Communist Party leader.
show that the marchers crossed the highest mountains in all of China.
questions.explanation: The passage specifically describes the Jiajin Mountains' 16,000-foot peaks, thin air, and freezing temperatures as examples of the brutal conditions that 'claimed countless lives,' illustrating the deadly natural challenges faced.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that the Long March was significant primarily because it
options: demonstrated the superior military tactics of the Communist forces.
allowed the Communists to recruit soldiers from the 11 provinces they crossed.
enabled the Communist Party to survive destruction and ultimately gain power in China.
proved that Chiang Kai-shek's Nationalist forces were poorly trained.
questions.explanation: The passage emphasizes that despite massive casualties, the survivors established a base, rebuilt their forces, and eventually won control of China—indicating the march's significance lay in enabling survival and eventual victory rather than immediate military success.

questions.stem: In the passage, the word 'strategic' most nearly means
options: military-related.
involving clever long-term planning.
bold and daring.
difficult to understand.
questions.explanation: The passage uses 'strategic' to describe Mao's thinking during the Long March, which led to his emergence as leader and eventual victory—implying skillful, careful planning for long-term success rather than simply military matters.

questions.stem: Which statement best summarizes the relationship between the hardships of the Long March and Mao Zedong's rise to power?
options: The extreme challenges of the march created conditions that allowed Mao to demonstrate leadership and gain control of the Communist Party.
Mao became leader because he successfully prevented most of the 86,000 marchers from dying during the journey.
The difficulties of crossing rivers and mountains forced other Communist leaders to resign and give power to Mao.
Mao's leadership during the Zunyi Conference convinced Chiang Kai-shek to stop pursuing the Communist forces.
questions.explanation: The passage connects the march's hardships with the Zunyi Conference where Mao emerged as leader, and notes his 'strategic thinking and resilience during the march' earned loyalty—showing how the challenging circumstances enabled his rise rather than simple military success or rival resignations.
